```mermaid
    nodeH["Bernoulli Downstream: Pd minus plus half rho Vc squared equals P0 plus half rho V2 squared"]
    nodeI["Thrust Pressure Form: T equals half rho A times VA squared minus V2 squared"]
    nodeJ["Thrust Momentum Form: T equals rho A Vc times V2 minus VA"]
    nodeK["Equate and Solve: Vc equals half times VA plus V2"]
    nodeL["Slipstream Relation: V2 equals VA plus 2u"]
    nodeM["Thrust Equation: T equals 2 rho A u times VA plus u"]
    nodeN["Power Equation: P equals T times Vc"]
    nodeO["Efficiency Equation: eta P equals VA divided by Vc"]
    nodeP["Loading Coefficient: CT equals 4x times 1 plus x"]
    nodeQ["Maximum Efficiency: eta P max equals 2 by 3 at x equals 0.5"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeO
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ
```

## 4.3 Interpretation of the Block Diagram

- **Top row (nodeA → nodeB → nodeC → nodeD → nodeE):** Represents the **physical flow** of water from far upstream, through the contracting stream tube, across the actuator disk (where the pressure jumps), and out to the far wake.
- **Middle row (nodeF, nodeG, nodeH):** The three **governing equations** of the theory — continuity and the two Bernoulli applications.
- **Bottom row (nodeI → nodeJ → nodeK → nodeL):** The **derivation chain** that yields the slipstream relation.
- **Final row (nodeM → nodeN → nodeO → nodeP → nodeQ):** The **engineering outputs** — thrust, power, efficiency, loading coefficient, and the famous 2/3 limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain momentum theory with a neat sketch", draw the velocity-pressure diagram (Section 4.1) on the answer sheet and label all five stations: far upstream, just before disk, just after disk, far downstream, and the stream tube boundary. This single figure, if drawn correctly, is worth 3–4 marks on its own.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the fundamental assumptions of the Rankine-Froude momentum theory for a marine propeller.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Rankine-Froude momentum theory is based on the following assumptions:
1. The propeller is replaced by an ideal **actuator disk** of zero thickness and uniform pressure jump.
2. The fluid (water) is **incompressible, inviscid, and homogeneous**.
3. The flow is **steady, uniform, and axial** (one-dimensional) at all stations.
4. **No rotational motion** exists in the slipstream (the water is irrotational outside the disk).
5. The **far-upstream and far-downstream pressures are equal** to the ambient static pressure $P_0$, and the velocity potential satisfies Bernoulli's equation at these stations.

*(1 mark per major assumption cluster; full 3 marks for clear, complete listing.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "propulsive efficiency" as obtained from momentum theory. What is its maximum theoretical value and under what condition is it achieved?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Propulsive efficiency $\eta_P$ is defined as the ratio of the **useful thrust power** ($P_U = T \cdot V_A$) to the **power delivered to the fluid** ($P = T \cdot V_C$) by the propeller:

$$
\eta_P = \frac{T V_A}{T V_C} = \frac{V_A}{V_A + u} = \frac{1}{1 + x}
$$

The **maximum theoretical value** is $\eta_{P,\max} = 2/3 \approx 66.67\%$, achieved when the **induced velocity equals half the advance velocity**, i.e., $u = V_A / 2$ (equivalently $x = 0.5$ or $C_T = 3$). This represents the **lightest practical loading** of an ideal actuator disk.

*(1 mark for definition with formula, 1 mark for 2/3 value, 1 mark for the condition $u = V_A/2$.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Choice A **or** Choice B in full. Each choice is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1, Q2]`

**Question A(a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the flow through an actuator disk. Define the terms: advance velocity, induced velocity, disk velocity, and slipstream velocity. State the assumptions of momentum theory.

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch (describe):** A horizontal streamline diagram showing a contracting stream tube with four labelled stations:
- **Station 1:** Far upstream — velocity $V_A$, pressure $P_0$.
- **Station 2:** Just before the disk — velocity $V_C$, pressure $P_d^+$.
- **Station 3:** Just after the disk — velocity $V_C$, pressure $P_d^-$.
- **Station 4:** Far downstream — velocity $V_2 = V_A + 2u$, pressure $P_0$.

The disk is drawn as a vertical line of length equal to the propeller diameter $D$, with an arrow showing the pressure jump.

**Definitions (4 marks):**
- **Advance velocity $V_A$:** Velocity of water far upstream of the propeller; equal to the ship speed in open water.
- **Induced velocity $u$:** The additional velocity imparted to water at the propeller disk, i.e., $V_C - V_A$.
- **Disk velocity $V_C$:** Velocity of water as it passes through the propeller plane, $V_C = V_A + u$.
- **Slipstream velocity $V_2$:** Velocity of water far downstream in the wake, $V_2 = V_A + 2u$.

**Assumptions (3 marks):** (List the five from Question A1 above.)

---

**Question A(b) [7 marks]:** Starting from the continuity equation and Bernoulli's equation, derive the expressions for **thrust, power delivered to the fluid, and propulsive efficiency** of an ideal actuator disk. Hence show that the maximum ideal efficiency is **2/3**.

**Model Solution:**

Apply continuity at the disk: $\rho A V_C = \rho A_2 V_2$. Apply Bernoulli upstream: $P_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^2 = P_d^+ + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_C^2$. Apply Bernoulli downstream: $P_d^- + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_C^2 = P_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$.

Pressure-based thrust: $T = A(P_d^+ - P_d^-) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A (V_A^2 - V_2^2)$. Momentum-based thrust: $T = \rho A V_C (V_2 - V_A)$.

Equating and simplifying gives the slipstream relation $V_C = \tfrac{1}{2}(V_A + V_2)$, hence $V_2 = V_A + 2u$ where $u = V_C - V_A$.

Substituting into the momentum form: $T = 2\rho A u (V_A + u)$. This is the **thrust equation** (2 marks).

Power delivered: $P = T V_C = 2\rho A u (V_A + u)^2$ (1 mark).

Propulsive efficiency: $\eta_P = T V_A / P = V_A / V_C = V_A / (V_A + u) = 1 / (1 + u/V_A)$ (2 marks).

For maximum efficiency under ideal loading $u = V_A / 2$: $\eta_P = 1 / (1 + 0.5) = 2/3$ (2 marks). Maximum ideal efficiency is therefore **2/3 ≈ 66.67%**.

---

### Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1, Q2]`

**Question B(a) [7 marks]:** A three-bladed marine propeller of diameter 2.5 m operates in open water with an advance coefficient $J = 0.7$ at a rotational speed $N = 180$ rpm. The thrust developed is 35 kN. Calculate the **advance velocity, thrust coefficient, induced velocity, power delivered to the water, useful thrust power, and propulsive efficiency**. Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

**Model Solution:**

Advance velocity:

$$
V_A = J \cdot N \cdot D = 0.7 \times \frac{180}{60} \times 2.5 = 0.7 \times 3 \times 2.5 = 5.25 \text{ m/s}
$$

Disk area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi (2.5)^2}{4} = 4.909 \text{ m}^2
$$

Thrust loading coefficient:

$$
C_T = \frac{T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_A^2} = \frac{35{,}000}{0.5 \times 1025 \times 4.909 \times (5.25)^2}
$$

$$
C_T = \frac{35{,}000}{0.5 \times 1025 \times 4.909 \times 27.5625} = \frac{35{,}000}{69{,}310} \approx 0.5051
$$

Induced velocity ratio:

$$
x = \frac{\sqrt{1 + C_T} - 1}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{1.5051} - 1}{2} = \frac{1.2269 - 1}{2} = 0.1134
$$

Induced velocity:

$$
u = x \cdot V_A = 0.1134 \times 5.25 = 0.5955 \text{ m/s}
$$

Disk velocity:

$$
V_C = V_A + u = 5.25 + 0.5955 = 5.8455 \text{ m/s}
$$

Power delivered to water:

$$
P = T \cdot V_C = 35{,}000 \times 5.8455 = 204{,}592 \text{ W} \approx 204.6 \text{ kW}
$$

Thrust (useful) power:

$$
P_U = T \cdot V_A = 35{,}000 \times 5.25 = 183{,}750 \text{ W} = 183.75 \text{ kW}
$$

Propulsive efficiency:

$$
\eta_P = \frac{P_U}{P} = \frac{183.75}{204.6} = 0.8981 \approx 89.81\%
$$

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark each for $V_A$, $C_T$, $u$, $P$, $P_U$, $\eta_P$, plus 1 mark for correct units and significant figures.)*

---

**Question B(b) [7 marks]:** What is the **thrust loading coefficient**? How is it related to the induced velocity ratio $x$? Sketch the variation of $\eta_P$ versus $C_T$ and comment on the practical significance of the curve.

**Model Solution:**

The thrust loading coefficient is a non-dimensional measure of how heavily the propeller is loaded:

$$
C_T = \frac{T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_A^2}
$$

It represents the thrust normalised by the dynamic pressure of the upstream flow and the disk area (2 marks).

From the thrust equation $T = 2\rho A u(V_A + u)$:

$$
C_T = \frac{2\rho A u(V_A + u)}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_A^2} = 4 \frac{u}{V_A}\left(1 + \frac{u}{V_A}\right) = 4x(1+x)
$$

where $x = u / V_A$ is the induced velocity ratio (2 marks).

The variation of $\eta_P$ with $C_T$ is obtained from:

$$
\eta_P = \frac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 + C_T}}
$$

**Sketch description:** A monotonically **decreasing curve** starting from $\eta_P = 1$ (i.e., 100%) at $C_T = 0$ and asymptotically approaching $\eta_P = 2/3 \approx 0.667$ as $C_T \to \infty$ (3 marks).

**Practical significance:** Heavily loaded propellers (high $C_T$, typical of heavily loaded cargo ships) operate at lower propulsive efficiency, while lightly loaded propellers (low $C_T$, typical of high-speed fine-form vessels) approach 100% efficiency. However, lightly loaded propellers are very large and expensive; ship designers must trade off efficiency against capital cost and cavitation risk (2 marks for the comment).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Momentum Theory Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $\eta_P$ with $\eta_D$ or $\eta_O$:** Momentum theory gives **ideal propulsive efficiency $\eta_P$**, NOT the overall propulsive coefficient $\eta_D$ (which includes hull efficiency $\eta_H$, rotational efficiency $\eta_R$, and mechanical efficiency $\eta_M$). Writing the wrong symbol costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always quote $V_A$ in m/s, $T$ in N, $P$ in W (or kW). The KTU checker will deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 3. **Sign errors in Bernoulli:** The pressure jump $P_d^+ > P_d^-$ gives positive thrust. Drawing the diagram backwards will give a negative efficiency, which is nonsense but is a frequent student error.
> 4. **Not deriving the slipstream relation first:** Many students jump straight to the final thrust formula. Examiners want to see $V_2 = V_A + 2u$ **derived**, not assumed.
> 5. **Using $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ in sea water problems:** Unless the problem explicitly states "fresh water", always use $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ for marine applications. Losing 1 mark for this is unfortunately common.
> 6. **Forgetting to state all five assumptions:** In 7-mark questions, listing only 2–3 assumptions will cap your marks at 3 out of 7 for that sub-part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Momentum theory replaces the propeller with an actuator disk** — a massless, porous disc that adds energy to the fluid without rotation.
- **Five core assumptions:** incompressible, inviscid, steady, uniform axial flow, no rotation in slipstream, ambient pressure at far fields.
- **Four key velocities:** $V_A$ (advance), $u$ (induced), $V_C = V_A + u$ (at disk), $V_2 = V_A + 2u$ (far wake).
- **Slipstream relation** $V_C = (V_A + V_2)/2$ is the heart of the theory — derive it by equating pressure-form and momentum-form thrust.
- **Thrust formula:** $T = 2\rho A u(V_A + u)$.
- **Power formula:** $P = T \cdot V_C = 2\rho A u (V_A + u)^2$.
- **Efficiency formula:** $\eta_P = V_A / V_C = 1/(1 + x)$ where $x = u/V_A$.
- **Maximum ideal efficiency = 2/3 ≈ 66.67%**, achieved at $u = V_A/2$ (or $x = 0.5$ or $C_T = 3$).
- **Loading coefficient:** $C_T = 4x(1+x)$ and $x = (\sqrt{1 + C_T} - 1)/2$.
- **Efficiency in terms of $C_T$:** $\eta_P = 2 / (1 + \sqrt{1 + C_T})$ — directly useful in ship design charts.
- **Always use $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ for sea water** in KTU exam problems unless stated otherwise.
- **Momentum theory is irrotational** — it does NOT predict torque. For torque and rotational effects, you must move to **blade element theory** (covered in Module 2).
- **Real propellers achieve 55–70% of the 2/3 limit**, depending on blade number, section shape, and Reynolds number.
- **The slipstream contracts** behind the disk (cross-section shrinks from $A$ to $A_2 < A$); this is the visual signature of energy being added to the flow.
- **KTU 2024 mark weightage** for momentum theory in Module 1: typically one 14-mark question in the End Semester Exam, plus one 3-mark definition in Part A. Master the derivation — it is the single most-tested concept in this module.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "CATS PEE":** **C**ontinuity → **A**ctuator disk → **T**hrust = pressure form = momentum form → **S**lipstream relation $V_2 = V_A + 2u$ → **P**ower $P = T V_C$ → **E**fficiency $\eta = V_A / V_C$ → **E**nd: $\eta_{\max} = 2/3$. If you remember **CATS PEE**, you can reconstruct the entire theory under exam pressure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blade Element Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST404 - SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Blade Element Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blade Element Theory (BET)** is a classical aerodynamic / hydrodynamic method used to predict the performance of a marine propeller (or any rotating bladed rotor) by treating each blade as a collection of independent, infinitesimally thin radial strips (called **blade elements**). The hydrodynamic forces generated on each strip are computed using two-dimensional airfoil / hydrofoil section data (lift and drag coefficients as functions of the angle of attack), and the total thrust and torque of the propeller are obtained by integrating these elemental forces along the span of the blade from the hub (root) to the tip.

Mathematically, the propeller is **decomposed** at any arbitrary radius $r$ into a small annular ring of width $dr$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi n$ (where $n$ is the rotational speed in revolutions per second). The hydrodynamic behaviour of this 2-D ring is then analysed locally, and the global thrust / torque are reconstructed by integration:

$$T = \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} dT(r) \quad , \quad Q = \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} r \, dF_{\theta}(r)$$

BET is most often used in conjunction with **Lerbs' induction-factor formulation** (analogous to BEM — Blade Element Momentum theory used in wind turbines) to obtain a self-consistent solution for the axial and tangential induction factors $a$ and $b$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Pancake Stack" Analogy**
> Imagine a fully grown propeller blade as a **tall stack of paper pancakes** held together along the rotating hub. Instead of trying to understand the complicated 3-D swirling flow around the entire blade at once, BET says: *"Let me look at just ONE pancake at a specific radius."* That pancake sees a steady 2-D flow (just like the airflow over an aircraft wing in a wind tunnel). I can find the **lift** (which contributes to thrust) and the **drag** (which resists rotation) on that pancake from standard hydrofoil tables. Then I do the same for every other pancake in the stack and **add them all up**. The sum gives me the total thrust and torque of the propeller.

This is the same logic a structural engineer uses when analysing a beam by **slicing it into thin disks and integrating** the stress across its cross-section. The trick that makes BET powerful is the assumption that **each slice is independent of its neighbours** — there is no radial communication of forces, which keeps the math tractable while still capturing the dominant physics.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit | Typical Magnitude (Ship Propeller) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\rho$ | Density of sea water | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | **1025** |
| $V_A$ | Speed of advance of ship | $\text{m/s}$ | 5 – 12 |
| $n$ | Rotational speed | $\text{rev/s}$ | 1 – 4 |
| $D$ | Propeller diameter | $\text{m}$ | 2 – 8 |
| $R$ | Propeller tip radius | $\text{m}$ | 1 – 4 |
| $Z$ | Number of blades | — | 3, 4, 5, 6 |
| $c$ | Local chord length | $\text{m}$ | 0.2 – 1.5 |
| $\beta$ | Local pitch angle (geometric) | degrees | 10° – 35° |
| $\phi$ | Local flow (inflow) angle | degrees | 5° – 25° |
| $\alpha_i$ | Local angle of attack | degrees | 1° – 8° |
| $C_L$ | 2-D lift coefficient | — | 0.4 – 1.0 |
| $C_D$ | 2-D drag coefficient | — | 0.01 – 0.05 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental assumption that allows BET to borrow airfoil/hydrofoil data is the **independence of blade elements along the span** (no radial flow). This is strictly valid only for an *ideal* actuator disk, and corrections such as **Prandtl tip-loss factor** $F$ and **hub-loss factor** $F_{hub}$ are introduced in the full BEM theory to handle the leakage of flow near the blade tips and root.

---

### 1.4 Historical Context (Why BET Exists)

Before BET, ship propellers were designed empirically (Froude, 1880s) and using **Froude's Momentum Theory** (1889), which treated the propeller as a thin actuator disk and gave the **ideal efficiency** ($\eta_i = \frac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1+C_T}}$) but provided no information about the **blade geometry** needed to achieve a given thrust. BET fills this gap by linking the geometric parameters (chord $c$, pitch $\beta$, blade count $Z$) to the resulting performance, making it the **engineering design tool** that connects fluid mechanics to propeller drawing-board work.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "How" and the "Why"

The fundamental logic of BET is built on **three layers** of physical reasoning. Mastering all three is essential for KTU 2024 scheme questions, which frequently ask for the derivation of the elemental force expressions.

**Layer 1 — Geometry of the Velocity Triangle at Radius $r$**
A blade element at radius $r$ rotates in still water (in the propeller's own reference frame, the water approaches with axial velocity $V_A$ and the blade moves tangentially with speed $\omega r$). The *resultant relative velocity* $W$ seen by the element is the vector sum of the axial and tangential components, inclined at the **flow angle** $\phi$ to the plane of rotation:

$$\tan \phi = \frac{V_A (1 + a)}{\omega r (1 - b)}$$

where $a$ is the **axial induction factor** and $b$ is the **tangential induction factor** that account for the slipstream contraction and rotational wake induced by the propeller's own action (these are solved iteratively using Lerbs' equations).

**Layer 2 — Hydrodynamic Force on the Strip (2-D Airfoil Theory)**
Once the relative velocity $W$ and the flow angle $\phi$ are known, the geometric pitch angle $\beta$ of the blade strip defines the **angle of attack** $\alpha_i$ experienced by the section:

$$\alpha_i = \beta - \phi$$

The 2-D lift and drag forces on the strip, per unit span, are given by standard airfoil equations:

$$dL = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho W^2 \, c \, C_L(\alpha_i) \, dr$$

$$dD = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho W^2 \, c \, C_D(\alpha_i) \, dr$$

**Layer 3 — Resolution into Thrust and Torque**
The lift acts perpendicular to $W$ and drag acts along $W$. These are resolved into the **axial direction** (which gives thrust) and the **tangential direction** (which, multiplied by $r$, gives the resistive torque):

$$dT = dL \cos\phi - dD \sin\phi$$

$$dF_{\theta} = dL \sin\phi + dD \cos\phi$$

$$dQ = r \, dF_{\theta} = r (dL \sin\phi + dD \cos\phi)$$

The **WHY** of each sign: Lift always tends to push the blade *forward and tangentially*; drag always opposes the relative motion. By projecting them onto the axial and tangential axes, the sign convention gives thrust as a *positive* axial force and torque as a *resistive* moment.

> [!NOTE]
> The drag term is usually one to two orders of magnitude smaller than the lift term for well-designed sections, so for a *first* estimate one may approximate $dT \approx dL \cos\phi$ and $dQ \approx r \, dL \sin\phi$. This "**lift-only**" simplification is often the first step expected in KTU derivations and is worth 4 – 5 marks by itself.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical**. They have appeared (in some form) in every KTU End-Semester Examination of the PBNST404 / equivalent Ship Propulsion paper since 2019.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Domain | Typical Use in Exam |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\tan\phi = \frac{V_A(1+a)}{\omega r (1-b)}$ | Inflow / flow angle at radius $r$ | Velocity triangle construction |
| 2 | $\alpha_i = \beta - \phi$ | Geometric angle of attack | Linking geometry to $C_L, C_D$ |
| 3 | $dT = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \,(C_L\cos\phi - C_D\sin\phi)\,dr$ | Elemental thrust | Integrate to get total $T$ |
| 4 | $dQ = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \,r\,(C_L\sin\phi + C_D\cos\phi)\,dr$ | Elemental torque | Integrate to get total $Q$ |
| 5 | $T = \tfrac{Z}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{r_h}^{R} dT$ | Total thrust (averaged over azimuth) | Final design step |
| 6 | $Q = \tfrac{Z}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{r_h}^{R} dQ$ | Total torque | Final design step |
| 7 | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ | Advance coefficient | Non-dimensional performance |
| 8 | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | Thrust coefficient (KT) | $K_T$–$J$–$K_Q$ diagrams |
| 9 | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | Torque coefficient (KQ) | $K_Q$–$J$ diagram |
| 10 | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{J}{2\pi}\dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}$ | Open-water efficiency | Propulsive efficiency |
| 11 | $\sigma = \dfrac{Z c}{2\pi r}$ | Local solidity (blade-area ratio) | Lifting-line correction |
| 12 | $W = \dfrac{V_A(1+a)}{\sin\phi} = \dfrac{\omega r(1-b)}{\cos\phi}$ | Relative velocity magnitude | Force magnitudes |

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU 2024 trick is to ask for the **propulsive efficiency** in terms of the induction factors $a$ and $b$ directly, without going through $K_T$ and $K_Q$. The answer is the elegant Lerbs expression:
> $$\eta = \frac{(1+a)(1-a)}{(1+b)} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \lambda \rightarrow \eta_{BET} = \frac{1-a}{1-b} \cdot \frac{1+a}{1} \quad \text{(re-derive in section 3)}$$
> Always check the exact form your module staff prefer.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Propeller design** — Given a target $T$ at speed $V_A$ and rpm $n$, BET is used iteratively to choose the optimum radial distribution of chord $c(r)$ and pitch $\beta(r)$, producing the **blade outline drawing** that the model basin workshop manufactures.
- **Wind-turbine design** — Identical theory is used as **BEM (Blade Element Momentum)** for wind-energy rotors, with the addition of Prandtl tip and hub loss factors.
- **Turbomachinery** — Used in preliminary design of axial-flow pumps, compressors, and marine waterjets.
- **Cavitation analysis** — The local pressure drop on the suction side of each element is computed using the local $C_L$ and $W$, allowing designers to predict cavitation inception and apply the **Keller, Burrill, or Lerbs cavitation criteria**.
- **RCS / Propeller-rudder interaction** — BET provides the local loading distribution $dT/dr$ that is fed into lifting-surface and panel codes (e.g., PROCAL, PANCAKE) to compute unsteady interaction forces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Elemental Thrust and Torque from Velocity Triangle

We derive the elemental thrust and torque expressions using a 2-D blade strip at radius $r$ with chord $c$, geometric pitch angle $\beta$, rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ in a flow of axial speed $V_A$.

**Step 1 — Construct the velocity triangle.**
The blade element, in the rotating reference frame, experiences:
- An axial component of inflow = $V_A(1+a)$ (greater than $V_A$ because the propeller "drags" the water forward — the slipstream contracts and the far-wake velocity is $V_A(1-2a)$).
- A tangential component (opposite to blade motion) = $\omega r(1-b)$, because the wake rotates slightly in the direction of blade motion, reducing the relative tangential speed.

The resultant relative velocity magnitude is:

$$W = \sqrt{\left[V_A(1+a)\right]^2 + \left[\omega r (1-b)\right]^2}$$

The flow (inflow) angle with respect to the plane of rotation is:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{V_A(1+a)}{\omega r (1-b)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\phi = \frac{V_A(1+a)}{W}, \quad \cos\phi = \frac{\omega r(1-b)}{W}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Kutta-Joukowski / 2-D airfoil theory.**
The lift per unit span of an airfoil in a 2-D flow of speed $W$ is $L' = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \, C_L$, and the drag per unit span is $D' = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \, C_D$. For the blade strip of width $dr$, multiply by $dr$:

$$dL = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \, C_L \, dr$$

$$dD = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \, C_D \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Resolve lift and drag into the propeller's axial and tangential directions.**
Lift $dL$ acts perpendicular to the relative velocity $W$ (i.e., at angle $\phi$ to the plane of rotation). Drag $dD$ acts along $W$ (i.e., at angle $\phi$ to the tangential direction).

Projecting onto the **axial direction** (thrust direction):

$$dT = dL \cos\phi - dD \sin\phi$$

Projecting onto the **tangential direction** (opposite to blade motion):

$$dF_{\theta} = dL \sin\phi + dD \cos\phi$$

**Step 4 — Form the elemental torque.**
The elemental torque about the propeller axis is the tangential force times the radius arm:

$$dQ = r \, dF_{\theta} = r \, (dL \sin\phi + dD \cos\phi)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $dL$ and $dD$.**
Substituting the expressions from Step 2:

$$dT = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \, \bigl( C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi \bigr) \, dr$$

$$dQ = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \, r \, \bigl( C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi \bigr) \, dr$$

These are the two **master equations of Blade Element Theory**. They are the starting point for every BET-based design procedure.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Thrust and Torque via Azimuthal Averaging

The expressions above are for a *single* blade element of a *single* blade. A propeller has $Z$ blades distributed symmetrically around the hub. To get the total thrust and torque at a fixed radius $r$, we average over the azimuthal angle $\theta$ from $0$ to $2\pi$. For a *uniform* wake (assumption of BET) and a *symmetric* blade layout, each blade sees identical flow at all times, so the average simply multiplies by $Z$ and divides by the total azimuthal sweep $2\pi$:

$$T = \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} \frac{Z}{2\pi}\,(2\pi) \, dT = Z \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} dT$$

Wait — this is the *uncorrected* BET result. In the rigorous BEM form, we keep the integral inside and integrate over the azimuth as well:

$$T = \frac{Z}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} dT \, dr \, d\theta$$

For uniform inflow (the BET idealisation), the integrand is independent of $\theta$, and the $2\pi$ in the denominator cancels with the $2\pi$ from azimuthal integration, recovering:

$$T = Z \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} dT(r) = \frac{Z}{2} \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} \rho W^2 c \bigl( C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi \bigr) \, dr$$

$$Q = Z \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} r \, dF_{\theta}(r) = \frac{Z}{2} \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} \rho W^2 c \, r \bigl( C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi \bigr) \, dr$$

**Why this factor of $Z$ matters:** A 5-bladed propeller does **not** produce 5 times the thrust of a 1-bladed propeller, because the wake contraction and induction factors $a, b$ themselves change. The factor $Z$ on the right-hand side is the *first-order* count of blades, but the correct BET/BEM procedure feeds this back into the momentum equations (Froude theory) to obtain *coupled* $a$ and $b$ values.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Non-Dimensional Coefficients and Efficiency

Define the following standard non-dimensional groups:

$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD} \quad \text{(Advance coefficient)}$$

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} \quad \text{(Thrust coefficient)}$$

$$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5} \quad \text{(Torque coefficient)}$$

The **useful power** delivered to the ship is $P_T = T \cdot V_A$ and the **shaft power** absorbed is $P_S = 2\pi n Q$. The **open-water efficiency** is their ratio:

$$\eta_0 = \frac{P_T}{P_S} = \frac{T V_A}{2\pi n Q} = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}$$

Substituting the integrated $T$ and $Q$ expressions from §3.2:

$$\eta_0 = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\displaystyle \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} \rho W^2 c \bigl( C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi \bigr) dr}{\displaystyle \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} \rho W^2 c \, r \bigl( C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi \bigr) dr}$$

For the **lift-only** approximation ($C_D \approx 0$), the efficiency simplifies to the elegant result:

$$\eta_{0,\text{lift-only}} = \frac{\displaystyle \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} W^2 c \, C_L \cos\phi \, dr}{\displaystyle \int_{r_{hub}}^{R} W^2 c \, C_L \sin\phi \, dr} \cdot \frac{V_A}{2\pi n r}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Lerbs' Induction-Factor Coupling (BEM Closure)

The classical BET result above is **kinematic** — it gives thrust and torque in terms of the induction factors $a$ and $b$ that are not yet known. To close the system, Lerbs (1952) proposed coupling BET with the **momentum-theory** element forces per unit radial length, which are:

$$\frac{dT}{dr} = 4\pi r \rho V_A^2 a (1+a) F$$

$$\frac{dF_\theta}{dr} = 4\pi r \rho V_A \omega r \, b (1+a) F$$

where $F$ is the Prandtl tip-loss factor (and a corresponding hub-loss factor may also be applied). Equating the BET expressions for $dT/dr$ and $dF_\theta/dr$ with these momentum expressions gives two coupled nonlinear equations for $a(r)$ and $b(r)$ at each radial station:

$$a(r) = \frac{\sigma'(r) \bigl[ C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi \bigr]}{4 F \sin^2\phi + \sigma'(r) \bigl[ C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi \bigr]}$$

$$b(r) = \frac{\sigma'(r) \bigl[ C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi \bigr]}{4 F \sin\phi \cos\phi - \sigma'(r) \bigl[ C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi \bigr]}$$

where $\sigma'(r) = \dfrac{Z c}{2\pi r}$ is the **local solidity** at radius $r$. These two equations are solved **iteratively** for $a$ and $b$ at each radius, and the converged values are then substituted back into the BET integral to obtain $T$, $Q$, and $\eta_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The iteration sequence is:
> 1. Guess $a = b = 0$ (no induction).
> 2. Compute $\phi$ from the velocity-triangle formula.
> 3. Compute $\alpha_i = \beta - \phi$ and read $C_L, C_D$ from the section's polar data.
> 4. Update $a$ and $b$ using the Lerbs equations above.
> 5. Repeat steps 2 – 4 until $\vert a_{k+1} - a_k \vert < 10^{-4}$ and $\vert b_{k+1} - b_k \vert < 10^{-4}$.
> 6. Proceed to the next radial station and to the next iteration on $c(r), \beta(r)$ in a design loop.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Term Matters

| Term | Physical Meaning | Effect of Doubling | KTU Board Insight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $W^2$ | Square of relative inflow speed | $dT, dQ$ scale by 4× | Frequently mis-spelled as $W$ in student work; examiners specifically check the *square* |
| $c$ | Local chord (projected blade width) | $dT, dQ$ scale linearly | Wider chord ⇒ more thrust but more cavitation risk |
| $C_L$ | Lift coefficient (function of $\alpha_i$) | $dT$ scales linearly | The dominant design variable for efficiency |
| $C_D$ | Profile drag coefficient | $dT$ decreases, $dQ$ increases | Almost always a parasitic loss term |
| $\cos\phi$ | Projection of lift onto thrust axis | $dT$ ∝ $\cos\phi$ | Falls as $r \to 0$ (inner radii contribute little thrust) |
| $\sin\phi$ | Projection of lift onto tangential axis | $dQ$ ∝ $r\sin\phi$ | Torque is *peaked* at mid-blade radius (75% R region) |
| $\sigma'$ | Local solidity $Zc/2\pi r$ | Higher $\sigma'$ ⇒ higher $a$ for given $C_L$ | Directly determines whether the rotor is "heavily" or "lightly" loaded |
| $F$ | Prandtl tip-loss factor ($F \le 1$) | $F \to 1$ ⇒ full actuator disk | Important at low blade counts (Z = 2 or 3) |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — BET Computational Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **step-by-step architecture** of a complete Blade Element Theory computation. Each node represents a processing stage; arrows indicate the flow of data and the iterative loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input: Ship speed VA, rpm n, diameter D, blade count Z]
    nodeB[Define blade geometry: chord c r and pitch beta r as functions of radius r]
    nodeC[Load hydrofoil polar: CL and CD versus angle of attack alpha_i]
    nodeD[Set initial induction factors: a = 0, b = 0]
    nodeE[Compute velocity triangle: tan phi = VA 1 plus a divided by omega r 1 minus b]
    nodeF[Compute angle of attack: alpha_i = beta minus phi]
    nodeG[Read CL and CD from polar data at alpha_i]
    nodeH[Compute solidity sigma prime = Z c divided by 2 pi r]
    nodeI[Compute Prandtl tip loss factor F]
    nodeJ[Update induction factors a and b using Lerbs coupled equations]
    nodeK{Convergence check: difference in a and b less than 1e-4}
    nodeL[Compute relative velocity W and elemental forces dT and dQ]
    nodeM[Increment radius and return to velocity triangle step]
    nodeN[Integrate dT and dQ from hub to tip to obtain T and Q]
    nodeO[Compute KT, KQ, J and open water efficiency eta zero]
    nodeP[Output propeller performance curves]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK -- Not converged --> nodeE
    nodeK -- Converged --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeE
    nodeM -- All radii done --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Velocity Triangle & Force Resolution

The figure below maps out the velocity triangle construction and the lift/drag-to-thrust/torque resolution. It is the most important sketch to memorise for the KTU 2024 paper.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeV1[Axial inflow component: VA times 1 plus a]
    nodeV2[Tangential inflow component: omega r times 1 minus b]
    nodeV3[Relative velocity W at flow angle phi to plane of rotation]
    nodeV4[Geometric pitch angle beta of blade section]
    nodeV5[Angle of attack alpha i equals beta minus phi]
    nodeF1[2D Lift dL perpendicular to W]
    nodeF2[2D Drag dD along W]
    nodeF3[Axial thrust dT equals dL cos phi minus dD sin phi]
    nodeF4[Tangential force dF theta equals dL sin phi plus dD cos phi]
    nodeF5[Elemental torque dQ equals r times dF theta]

    nodeV1 --> nodeV3
    nodeV2 --> nodeV3
    nodeV3 --> nodeF1
    nodeV3 --> nodeF2
    nodeV3 --> nodeV4
    nodeV4 --> nodeV5
    nodeF1 --> nodeF3
    nodeF1 --> nodeF4
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3
    nodeF2 --> nodeF4
    nodeF4 --> nodeF5
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — BET vs Momentum Theory Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeG1[Froude Momentum Theory]
        nodeM1[Assumes propeller is a continuous actuator disk]
        nodeM2[Computes ideal efficiency and thrust loading without blade geometry]
        nodeM3[Result: eta ideal = 2 divided by 1 plus sqrt 1 plus CT]
    end

    subgraph nodeG2[Blade Element Theory]
        nodeB1[Treats each blade as collection of 2D strips]
        nodeB2[Uses airfoil section data CL alpha and CD alpha]
        nodeB3[Integrates elemental dT and dQ along the span]
        nodeB4[Result: KT J KQ and eta zero curves with explicit blade geometry]
    end

    subgraph nodeG3[Blade Element Momentum Method BEM]
        nodeX1[Couples BET strip forces with momentum-theory induction factors]
        nodeX2[Iteratively solves for a and b at every radial station]
        nodeX3[Result: realistic KT J KQ with Prandtl tip and hub losses]
    end

    nodeM1 --> nodeM2 --> nodeM3
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2 --> nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    nodeM3 --> nodeX1
    nodeB4 --> nodeX1
    nodeX1 --> nodeX2 --> nodeX3
```

---

### 4.4 Visual Summary of the Master Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Aid for the Velocity Triangle at Radius $r$**
>
> The axial leg is **$V_A(1+a)$** (water arrives faster than the ship moves because the slipstream contracts in front of the disk).
> The tangential leg is **$\omega r(1-b)$** (the wake rotates in the direction of blade motion, so the *relative* tangential speed is reduced).
> The hypotenuse is **$W$** (the relative velocity the airfoil actually feels).
> The angle between the hypotenuse and the tangential leg is **$\phi$** (the flow / inflow angle).
> The angle between the hypotenuse and the chord line is **$\alpha_i$** (the angle of attack).
> The angle between the chord line and the plane of rotation is **$\beta$** (the geometric pitch angle).
> Trigonometric identity: $\beta = \phi + \alpha_i$.
