## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

State the fundamental assumptions on which Blade Element Theory for marine propellers is based. Mention any two limitations of the theory.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The fundamental assumptions of BET are:

1. **Independence of blade elements:** The hydrodynamic behaviour of each annular blade element at radius $r$ depends only on the local 2-D flow conditions at that radius, with no radial flow or force communication between adjacent elements. *(1 mark)*
2. **Uniform wake:** The inflow to each element is assumed to be steady and circumferentially uniform, so the same velocity triangle applies at every azimuthal position. *(1 mark)*
3. **Quasi-2D section data:** The lift and drag coefficients $C_L$ and $C_D$ are obtained from 2-D airfoil/hydrofoil tests and are applied as if the element were isolated in a uniform stream of velocity $W$ at angle of attack $\alpha_i$. *(1 mark)*

**Limitations:**

- The theory ignores **radial flow** and the 3-D tip vortex system, leading to over-prediction of thrust near the blade tip.
- It cannot predict the **3-D cavitation pattern** on the blade surface.
- **Compressibility** (for high-speed tip sections) is unaccounted for.
- Wake non-uniformity and **hull-propeller interaction** are not modelled.

---

**Q.A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

Define the following terms used in Blade Element Theory:
(a) Advance coefficient $J$
(b) Local solidity $\sigma'$
(c) Open-water efficiency $\eta_0$

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

(a) **Advance coefficient $J$** *(1 mark)*: It is the non-dimensional ratio of the speed of advance of the ship $V_A$ to the product of rotational speed $n$ (in rev/s) and propeller diameter $D$:
$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD}$$

(b) **Local solidity $\sigma'$** *(1 mark)*: It is the non-dimensional ratio of the total blade chord at a given radius to the circumferential spacing of the blades at that radius, defined as:
$$\sigma'(r) = \frac{Z \, c(r)}{2\pi r}$$
where $Z$ is the number of blades, $c(r)$ is the local chord, and $r$ is the radius.

(c) **Open-water efficiency $\eta_0$** *(1 mark)*: It is the ratio of the useful thrust power ($T V_A$) to the shaft power delivered to the propeller ($2 \pi n Q$):
$$\eta_0 = \frac{T V_A}{2\pi n Q} = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}$$
measured in open-water (unbounded) conditions with no hull present.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always state assumptions explicitly** at the start of any BET derivation — failing to do so is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Do not confuse the *flow angle* $\phi$ with the *pitch angle* $\beta$ or the *angle of attack* $\alpha_i$** — examiners routinely test the relationship $\alpha_i = \beta - \phi$.
> 3. **Show the velocity triangle diagram** even if the question does not ask for it — it carries 2 marks by itself in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Carry units through the derivation** — at least once. Marks are lost for purely numerical errors that a unit check would have caught.
> 5. **Sign convention:** thrust is positive *forward*; torque is positive *resistive* (i.e., opposes the direction of rotation).

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the aid of a velocity triangle at radius $r$, derive the expressions for the elemental thrust $dT$ and the elemental torque $dQ$ produced by a blade element of chord $c$ and pitch angle $\beta$ in a marine propeller. Clearly state all assumptions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the expressions derived in part (a), show that the open-water efficiency of the propeller in the **lift-only** approximation ($C_D \approx 0$) is given by:
$$\eta_0 = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\displaystyle \int_{r_h}^{R} \rho W^2 c \, C_L \cos\phi \, dr}{\displaystyle \int_{r_h}^{R} \rho W^2 c \, C_L \, r \sin\phi \, dr}$$
Comment briefly on how the integrand varies from the hub to the tip and identify the radius at which the torque contribution is maximum for a typical merchant-ship propeller. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Assumptions (1 mark):*
- The blade is divided into independent radial elements of width $dr$ (no radial flow).
- The wake is circumferentially uniform and steady.
- 2-D section data ($C_L, C_D$ as functions of $\alpha_i$) is valid.
- The blade sees axial inflow $V_A(1+a)$ and tangential inflow $\omega r(1-b)$ in the rotating frame.

*Velocity triangle (1 mark):* The axial component of relative inflow is $V_A(1+a)$ and the tangential component is $\omega r(1-b)$. The relative velocity magnitude is $W$ and the flow angle is $\phi$, with:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{V_A(1+a)}{\omega r(1-b)} \quad ; \quad \alpha_i = \beta - \phi$$

*2-D airfoil forces (1 mark):* For the element of width $dr$ and chord $c$:
$$dL = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \, C_L \, dr \quad ; \quad dD = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \, C_D \, dr$$

*Resolution into axial and tangential directions (2 marks):*
$$dT = dL \cos\phi - dD \sin\phi = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \,(C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi)\, dr$$
$$dF_{\theta} = dL \sin\phi + dD \cos\phi = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \,(C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi)\, dr$$

*Torque (1 mark):*
$$dQ = r \, dF_{\theta} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \, r \,(C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi)\, dr$$

*Vector diagram / units check (1 mark):* Force units are N (thrust) and N·m (torque) — dimensional check passes.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — total thrust and torque (2 marks):* For $Z$ blades, the total thrust and torque are obtained by integrating $dT$ and $dQ$ from hub radius $r_h$ to tip radius $R$:
$$T = Z \int_{r_h}^{R} \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \,(C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi)\, dr$$
$$Q = Z \int_{r_h}^{R} \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c \, r \,(C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi)\, dr$$

*Step 2 — apply lift-only approximation (1 mark):* With $C_D = 0$:
$$T = \tfrac{Z}{2}\int_{r_h}^{R} \rho W^2 c \, C_L \cos\phi \, dr \quad ; \quad Q = \tfrac{Z}{2}\int_{r_h}^{R} \rho W^2 c \, C_L \, r \sin\phi \, dr$$

*Step 3 — efficiency formula (2 marks):*
$$\eta_0 = \frac{T V_A}{2\pi n Q} = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\displaystyle \int_{r_h}^{R} \rho W^2 c \, C_L \cos\phi \, dr}{\displaystyle \int_{r_h}^{R} \rho W^2 c \, C_L \, r \sin\phi \, dr}$$

*Step 4 — comment on hub-to-tip variation (2 marks):* Near the hub, $\omega r$ is small, so $\tan\phi$ is large and $\phi$ approaches 90°. The integrand of $T$ contains $\cos\phi$ which is small near the hub, while the integrand of $Q$ contains $r \sin\phi$ which is small because $r$ is small. The torque contribution peaks at approximately **$r \approx 0.75\,R$** (the 75% radius region) for a typical merchant-ship propeller, because this is where $r \sin\phi$ (which is the lever-arm times the tangential force coefficient) reaches its maximum. The thrust contribution is more evenly distributed and tends to peak slightly outboard, near $0.7R$ – $0.8R$.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Explain the **velocity triangle** at a typical blade section of a marine propeller. Define the flow angle $\phi$, the geometric pitch angle $\beta$, and the angle of attack $\alpha_i$, and obtain the relationship between them. Derive an expression for the **non-dimensional thrust coefficient** $K_T$ in terms of the local hydrofoil coefficients $C_L, C_D$ and the flow geometry. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of the Blade Element Theory, derive the **Lerbs induction-factor equations** for the axial induction factor $a$ and tangential induction factor $b$ at any radius $r$. State the meaning of the Prandtl tip-loss factor $F$ and explain why it must be included for accurate predictions on low-blade-count propellers. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Velocity triangle description (2 marks):* In the rotating reference frame, the blade element at radius $r$ sees an axial inflow $V_A(1+a)$ and a tangential inflow (in the direction opposite to blade motion) of magnitude $\omega r(1-b)$. The relative velocity $W$ is the vector sum of these two, and it is inclined at an angle $\phi$ (the flow angle) to the plane of rotation. The chord line of the blade is inclined at an angle $\beta$ (the geometric pitch angle) to the plane of rotation. The angle between the chord line and the relative velocity vector is the **angle of attack** $\alpha_i$.

*Geometric relationship (1 mark):*
$$\alpha_i = \beta - \phi$$

*Thrust coefficient (4 marks):* The total thrust is
$$T = \frac{Z}{2}\int_{r_h}^{R} \rho W^2 c \bigl( C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi \bigr) dr$$

Introducing the advance coefficient $J = V_A / nD$ and writing $W = V_A(1+a)/\sin\phi$, after some algebra the thrust coefficient becomes:
$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{\pi J^2}{8}\int_{\lambda_h}^{1} \frac{\sigma'}{\sin^2\phi}\bigl( C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi \bigr)(1+a)^2 \,\lambda \, d\lambda$$
where $\lambda = r/R$ is the non-dimensional radius and $\lambda_h = r_h / R$ is the hub-radius ratio. This is the **standard BET form** of the thrust coefficient as it appears in the Wageningen B-series regression and in Kerwin's lifting-line code.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Momentum-theory element forces (2 marks):* The thrust and tangential force per unit radial length, obtained by applying the Froude actuator-disk theory to an annular element of radius $r$ and width $dr$, are:
$$\frac{dT}{dr} = 4\pi r \rho V_A^2 a(1+a) F$$
$$\frac{dF_{\theta}}{dr} = 4\pi r \rho V_A \omega r \, b(1+a) F$$

*BET element forces (2 marks):* From §3.1:
$$\frac{dT}{dr} = \tfrac{1}{2} Z \rho W^2 c \bigl( C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi \bigr)$$
$$\frac{dF_{\theta}}{dr} = \tfrac{1}{2} Z \rho W^2 c \bigl( C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi \bigr)$$

*Equating and simplifying (2 marks):* Equating the two expressions for $dT/dr$ and $dF_\theta/dr$ and using $\sin\phi = V_A(1+a)/W$ and $\cos\phi = \omega r(1-b)/W$, we obtain the Lerbs coupled equations:
$$a(r) = \frac{\sigma' \bigl( C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi \bigr)}{4 F \sin^2\phi + \sigma' \bigl( C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi \bigr)}$$
$$b(r) = \frac{\sigma' \bigl( C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi \bigr)}{4 F \sin\phi \cos\phi - \sigma' \bigl( C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi \bigr)}$$
with $\sigma' = Zc / 2\pi r$.

*Meaning of $F$ (1 mark):* The Prandtl tip-loss factor $F$ accounts for the fact that the pressure difference between the front and back of the actuator disk is *not* maintained all the way to the blade tip — the flow "leaks" around the tip in the form of a tip vortex, reducing the effective thrust-producing area. Mathematically, $F = \frac{2}{\pi}\arccos\bigl(e^{-f}\bigr)$ with $f = \tfrac{Z}{2}\bigl(\tfrac{R}{r} - 1\bigr)\frac{1}{\sin\phi_{tip}}$. As $Z$ decreases or as the loading shifts towards the tip, $F$ drops below 1, and the *uncorrected* (no-tip-loss) BET over-predicts the thrust by a significant margin. For $Z = 2$ or $Z = 3$ propellers at high thrust loading, neglecting $F$ can produce errors exceeding 15–20 % in $K_T$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (exam the night before):**

- **BET = strip-by-strip analysis** of a propeller using 2-D hydrofoil section data. *No radial flow, uniform wake, steady inflow.* ✅
- **Velocity triangle** at radius $r$ has legs $V_A(1+a)$ (axial) and $\omega r(1-b)$ (tangential), hypotenuse $W$, and the flow angle $\phi$ between $W$ and the plane of rotation. ✅
- **Three key angles** satisfy $\alpha_i = \beta - \phi$ — never confuse them. ✅
- **Elemental thrust:** $dT = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c\,(C_L \cos\phi - C_D \sin\phi)\,dr$ ✅
- **Elemental torque:** $dQ = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^2 c\,r\,(C_L \sin\phi + C_D \cos\phi)\,dr$ ✅
- **Lift-only approximation** sets $C_D = 0$ — this is the *first* simplification to try in any exam derivation. ✅
- **Total $T$ and $Q$** = $Z$ times the radial integral of $dT$ and $dQ$ from $r_h$ to $R$ (in uniform inflow). ✅
- **Advance coefficient** $J = V_A / nD$ — non-dimensionalises the speed of advance. ✅
- **Thrust coefficient** $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$; **Torque coefficient** $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$. ✅
- **Open-water efficiency** $\eta_0 = (J/2\pi) \cdot (K_T / K_Q) = T V_A / (2\pi n Q)$. ✅
- **Local solidity** $\sigma' = Zc / 2\pi r$ — appears in the Lerbs equations and measures blade "thickness" at each radius. ✅
- **Lerbs equations** couple BET strip forces to momentum-theory induction factors $a$ and $b$ — must be solved **iteratively** at each radius. ✅
- **Prandtl tip-loss factor $F$** is critical for low blade counts ($Z = 2, 3$); $F \to 1$ for $Z \to \infty$. ✅
- **Torque contribution peaks at $\sim 0.75\,R$**; thrust contribution peaks at $\sim 0.7R$–$0.8R$. ✅
- **Common exam trick:** ask for the efficiency in *induction-factor* form: $\eta_{BET} = (1-a)/(1-b)$ (lift-only, no rotation losses). ✅
- **Dimensional check:** $T$ in N, $Q$ in N·m, $K_T$ and $K_Q$ dimensionless, $\eta_0$ dimensionless and $\le 1$. ✅
- **Sign convention:** thrust forward = positive, torque resistive = positive. ✅
- **BEM = BET + Momentum theory coupled** (Lerbs formulation). BET alone does *not* predict $a$ and $b$ — it only uses them. ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensional Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST404 - SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dimensional Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensional Analysis** is a systematic mathematical technique used in physical sciences and engineering to study the nature of physical quantities by reducing complex physical phenomena into their fundamental dimensions (Mass $[M]$, Length $[L]$, Time $[T]$, Temperature $[\theta]$, etc.). It is a powerful tool for checking the validity of physical equations, deriving relationships between variables, and establishing dimensionless parameters that govern the behavior of physical systems.

In the context of **Ship Propulsion Engineering (PBNST404)**, dimensional analysis forms the mathematical foundation of **model-to-full-scale extrapolation**, **propeller open-water testing**, and **cavitation analysis**. Every important non-dimensional number used in naval architecture — such as the Froude number, Reynolds number, and propeller advance coefficient — is born out of dimensional analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 of PBNST404 (Ship Propulsion), the topic "Dimensional Analysis" introduces students to the mathematical backbone of physical similarity, which is critical for understanding the scaling laws used in towing tanks, cavitation tunnels, and propeller test beds.

## 1.2 Physical Quantities, Dimensions & Units

A **physical quantity** is expressed as:

$$Q = \{Q\} \cdot [Q]$$

Where:
- $\{Q\}$ = numerical value (magnitude)
- $[Q]$ = unit of measurement

The **dimensional formula** of a quantity expresses it in terms of fundamental dimensions. For ship propulsion, the dominant fundamental dimensions are:

> **$[M]$** = Mass, **$[L]$** = Length, **$[T]$** = Time.

For example, **ship speed** $V$ has dimensions $[LT^{-1}]$, and **propeller thrust** $T$ has dimensions $[MLT^{-2}]$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Recipe Metaphor):**
> Imagine you are baking a cake. A recipe for a small 1-kg cake tells you: "use 2 eggs, 200 g flour, 100 g sugar." If you want to bake a 5-kg cake (5× larger), you might naively multiply every ingredient by 5. But baking is a **physical process** — heat transfer, moisture evaporation, and rising depend on the cake's *shape* and *surface area* in non-linear ways. Scaling all ingredients linearly will ruin the cake.
>
> **Dimensional analysis is the "baker's science" of physics.** It tells you which *combinations* of variables (ingredients) actually govern the outcome, so you can scale things correctly. In ship propulsion, we cannot build a full-scale 300 m oil tanker in a towing tank — instead we test a 3 m model, and **dimensional analysis tells us exactly how to translate the model's behavior to the real ship.**

## 1.4 Types of Physical Quantities

| Type | Description | Example in Ship Propulsion |
|------|-------------|---------------------------|
| **Dimensional Quantity** | Has dimensions (units) attached | Ship speed $(m/s)$, Thrust $(N)$ |
| **Dimensionless Quantity** | Pure number, no units | Froude number, Propeller efficiency |
| **Primary (Fundamental) Dimensions** | Independent base dimensions | $[M], [L], [T]$ |
| **Secondary (Derived) Dimensions** | Expressed via primary dimensions | Force $[MLT^{-2}]$, Power $[ML^{2}T^{-3}]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Dimensional Homogeneity:** A physical equation is *dimensionally homogeneous* if every term on both sides of the equation has the **same fundamental dimensions**. Dimensional analysis rests entirely on the principle that an equation describing a physical phenomenon must be dimensionally homogeneous. This is a *necessary but not sufficient* condition for the equation to be correct.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Dimensional Homogeneity — The Foundational Principle

Every physically meaningful equation must obey the principle of **dimensional homogeneity**:

> [!NOTE]
> *"You cannot add apples and oranges."*
> An equation like $V = \sqrt{2gh} + 5$ is **physically invalid** because the constant $5$ is dimensionless while every other term has dimensions of $[LT^{-1}]$. The correct form is $V = \sqrt{2gh}$, where the dimensionless constant $2$ carries no dimensions.

In ship propulsion, dimensional homogeneity is the very basis of dimensional analysis, allowing us to reduce the number of independent variables in a problem.

## 2.2 Methods of Dimensional Analysis

There are two principal methods used universally in engineering (and prescribed by KTU 2024 Module 1):

### Method 1: Rayleigh's Method (Index Method)

**Logical Steps:**
1. List all variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ that influence the physical phenomenon.
2. Assume a power-product relationship: $$X_1 = k \cdot X_2^{a_1} \cdot X_3^{a_2} \cdots X_n^{a_{n-1}}$$
3. Express each variable in its fundamental dimensions $[M, L, T]$.
4. Equate the exponents of corresponding dimensions on both sides.
5. Solve the resulting system of linear equations for the unknown exponents.
6. Substitute the exponents back to obtain the dimensionless groups.

**Why it works:** The *Why* behind Rayleigh's method is that a physically correct relationship must be dimensionally homogeneous. By forcing homogeneity, we constrain the exponents such that the only freedom left is dimensionless combinations.

**Limitation:** Becomes cumbersome when the number of variables exceeds 4–5 (gives more exponents than equations, leaving indeterminacies).

### Method 2: Buckingham's $\pi$ Theorem (Most Important for KTU Exams)

**Statement:**
> *"If a physical phenomenon involves $n$ physical variables and $k$ fundamental dimensions, then the relationship can be reduced to exactly $(n - k)$ independent dimensionless groups, called $\pi$ terms."*

**Logical Steps:**
1. List all $n$ dimensional variables involved in the phenomenon.
2. Identify $k$ fundamental dimensions (usually $M, L, T$ — so $k = 3$).
3. Select $k$ *repeating variables* that collectively contain all fundamental dimensions and are dimensionally independent (cannot form a $\pi$ group among themselves).
4. Form $(n - k)$ dimensionless $\pi$ groups, each by multiplying one non-repeating variable with powers of the repeating variables.
5. Solve for the exponents in each $\pi$ group.
6. Express the final relationship: $f(\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_{n-k}) = 0$.

**How the $\pi$ Theorem Works:**
- Each $\pi$ group is **dimensionless** by construction.
- The set of $\pi$ groups is **independent** — no group can be formed from the others.
- The result reduces a complex multi-variable problem into a smaller set of dimensionless groups, which is enormously useful in model testing.

## 2.3 Critical Dimensionless Numbers in Ship Propulsion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following dimensionless groups are the **"Hall of Fame"** of ship propulsion dimensional analysis. KTU examiners expect students to know their definitions, physical meaning, and applications.

| $\pi$ Group | Formula | Physical Meaning | Use in Ship Propulsion |
|-------------|---------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Froude Number ($F_n$)** | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Ratio of inertial force to gravitational force | Hull resistance scaling, wave-making similarity |
| **Reynolds Number ($R_n$)** | $R_n = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | Ratio of inertial force to viscous force | Viscous resistance, propeller blade Reynolds effects |
| **Euler Number ($E_u$)** | $E_u = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2}$ | Ratio of pressure force to inertial force | Cavitation analysis, pressure coefficient |
| **Weber Number ($W_e$)** | $W_e = \dfrac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma}$ | Ratio of inertial force to surface tension force | Spray, free-surface flows |
| **Cavitation Number ($\sigma$)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2}$ | Ratio of pressure margin to dynamic pressure | Propeller cavitation onset, cavitation tunnel tests |
| **Thrust Coefficient ($K_T$)** | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | Non-dimensional propeller thrust | Propeller open-water test results |
| **Torque Coefficient ($K_Q$)** | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | Non-dimensional propeller torque | Propeller open-water test results |
| **Advance Coefficient ($J$)** | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ | Non-dimensional advance speed | Propeller performance mapping |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the **International Towing Tank Conference (ITTC) 1978** standard, ship resistance prediction requires simultaneous similarity in **Froude number** (for wave pattern) and **Reynolds number** (for viscous boundary layer). Since both cannot be matched at the same model scale, the standard approach splits resistance into *frictional* (Reynolds-based) and *residual* (Froude-based) components — a direct outcome of dimensional analysis.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Dimension |
|---|---------|-----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Force (Newton's 2nd law) | $F = ma$ | $[MLT^{-2}]$ |
| 2 | Pressure | $p = F/A$ | $[ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$ |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | $[L^{2}T^{-1}]$ |
| 4 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | $[ML^{-1}T^{-1}]$ |
| 5 | Froude Number | $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Reynolds Number | $R_n = \rho V L/\mu$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Euler Number | $E_u = \Delta p/(\rho V^2)$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Power | $P = F \cdot V$ | $[ML^{2}T^{-3}]$ |
| 9 | Buckingham $\pi$ Theorem | Number of $\pi$ groups $= n - k$ | — |
| 10 | Rayleigh's Method | $X_1 = k X_2^{a_1} X_3^{a_2} \cdots$ | Dimensional homogeneity enforced |
| 11 | Surface Tension | $\sigma$ | $[MT^{-2}]$ |
| 12 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | $[ML^{-2}T^{-2}]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reminder:** When writing equations in markdown tables, never use the vertical bar `|` symbol (e.g., for $\vert x \vert$). Use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table layout.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example 1 — Derivation of Froude Number via Buckingham $\pi$ Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** The resistance $R$ experienced by a ship hull moving at speed $V$ through a fluid of density $\rho$ and kinematic viscosity $\nu$, under gravity $g$, depends on the hull length $L$. Use Buckingham's $\pi$ theorem to derive the governing dimensionless groups.

### Step 1: List All Variables

The variables involved are:

$$R, \quad V, \quad \rho, \quad \nu, \quad g, \quad L$$

Total number of variables: $n = 6$.

### Step 2: Identify Fundamental Dimensions

Using the M-L-T system:

| Variable | Symbol | Dimensional Formula |
|----------|--------|--------------------|
| Resistance | $R$ | $[MLT^{-2}]$ |
| Speed | $V$ | $[LT^{-1}]$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | $[ML^{-3}]$ |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | $[L^{2}T^{-1}]$ |
| Gravity | $g$ | $[LT^{-2}]$ |
| Length | $L$ | $[L]$ |

Fundamental dimensions: $k = 3$ (namely $M, L, T$).

### Step 3: Number of $\pi$ Groups

$$\text{Number of } \pi \text{ groups} = n - k = 6 - 3 = 3$$

### Step 4: Select Repeating Variables

A good selection must contain all fundamental dimensions and be dimensionally independent. We choose:

$$\rho, \quad V, \quad L$$

These three collectively contain $[M], [L], [T]$ and cannot form a $\pi$ group among themselves.

The non-repeating variables are: $R, \nu, g$.

### Step 5: Form the $\pi$ Groups

**$\pi_1$ (involving $R$):**

$$\pi_1 = R \cdot \rho^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot L^{c}$$

For dimensional homogeneity:

$$\begin{aligned}
[R] \cdot [\rho]^{a} \cdot [V]^{b} \cdot [L]^{c} &= [M^{0}L^{0}T^{0}] \\
[MLT^{-2}] \cdot [ML^{-3}]^{a} \cdot [LT^{-1}]^{b} \cdot [L]^{c} &= [M^{0}L^{0}T^{0}] \\
[M^{1+a} \cdot L^{1-3a+b+c} \cdot T^{-2-b}] &= [M^{0}L^{0}T^{0}]
\end{aligned}$$

Equating exponents:

- $M$: $1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$
- $T$: $-2 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -2$
- $L$: $1 - 3(-1) + (-2) + c = 0 \Rightarrow 1 + 3 - 2 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -2$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\pi_1 = \frac{R}{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}}$$

This is the **resistance coefficient** $C_R$.

**$\pi_2$ (involving $\nu$):**

$$\pi_2 = \nu \cdot \rho^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot L^{c}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
[L^{2}T^{-1}] \cdot [ML^{-3}]^{a} \cdot [LT^{-1}]^{b} \cdot [L]^{c} &= [M^{0}L^{0}T^{0}] \\
[M^{a} \cdot L^{2-3a+b+c} \cdot T^{-1-b}] &= [M^{0}L^{0}T^{0}]
\end{aligned}$$

Equating exponents:

- $M$: $a = 0$
- $T$: $-1 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -1$
- $L$: $2 - 0 + (-1) + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -1$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\pi_2 = \frac{\nu}{VL} = \frac{1}{R_n}}$$

This is the **inverse Reynolds number**.

**$\pi_3$ (involving $g$):**

$$\pi_3 = g \cdot \rho^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot L^{c}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
[LT^{-2}] \cdot [ML^{-3}]^{a} \cdot [LT^{-1}]^{b} \cdot [L]^{c} &= [M^{0}L^{0}T^{0}] \\
[M^{a} \cdot L^{1-3a+b+c} \cdot T^{-2-b}] &= [M^{0}L^{0}T^{0}]
\end{aligned}$$

Equating exponents:

- $M$: $a = 0$
- $T$: $-2 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -2$
- $L$: $1 - 0 + (-2) + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = 1$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\pi_3 = \frac{gL}{V^{2}} = \frac{1}{F_n^{2}}}$$

This is the **inverse square of the Froude number**.

### Step 6: Final Functional Relationship

$$\boxed{f\!\left(\frac{R}{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}, \frac{VL}{\nu}, \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}\right) = 0}$$

Or equivalently:

$$C_R = f(R_n, F_n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The resistance coefficient of a ship hull is a function of two independent dimensionless groups — the Reynolds number and the Froude number. This is the **mathematical basis for model testing in towing tanks**.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example 2 — Propeller Thrust via Rayleigh's Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** The thrust $T$ developed by a marine propeller depends on the propeller diameter $D$, rotational speed $n$, fluid density $\rho$, fluid viscosity $\mu$, and the speed of advance $V_A$. Using Rayleigh's method, find the dimensionless groups governing the thrust.

### Step 1: List Variables and Their Dimensions

| Variable | Symbol | Dimensional Formula |
|----------|--------|--------------------|
| Thrust | $T$ | $[MLT^{-2}]$ |
| Diameter | $D$ | $[L]$ |
| Rotational Speed | $n$ | $[T^{-1}]$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | $[ML^{-3}]$ |
| Viscosity | $\mu$ | $[ML^{-1}T^{-1}]$ |
| Advance Speed | $V_A$ | $[LT^{-1}]$ |

### Step 2: Assume Power-Product Relationship

$$T = k \cdot D^{a} \cdot n^{b} \cdot \rho^{c} \cdot \mu^{d} \cdot V_A^{e}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Dimensions

$$\begin{aligned}
[MLT^{-2}] &= [L]^{a} \cdot [T^{-1}]^{b} \cdot [ML^{-3}]^{c} \cdot [ML^{-1}T^{-1}]^{d} \cdot [LT^{-1}]^{e} \\
&= [M^{c+d} \cdot L^{a-3c-d+e} \cdot T^{-b-d-e}]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Equate Exponents of $M, L, T$

**For $M$:**

$$1 = c + d \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**For $T$:**

$$-2 = -b - d - e \quad \Rightarrow \quad b + d + e = 2 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**For $L$:**

$$1 = a - 3c - d + e \quad \text{...(3)}$$

### Step 5: Solve the System — 3 Equations, 5 Unknowns

We have 5 unknowns ($a, b, c, d, e$) and 3 equations. Per Buckingham's theorem, the number of independent dimensionless groups is $n - k = 6 - 3 = 3$. We choose 2 free variables and express others in terms of them.

**Free variables:** Choose $c$ and $e$ as free parameters.

From (1): $d = 1 - c$

From (2): $b = 2 - d - e = 2 - (1 - c) - e = 1 + c - e$

From (3): $a = 1 + 3c + d - e = 1 + 3c + (1 - c) - e = 2 + 2c - e$

### Step 6: Construct the Dimensionless Groups

Substituting back into the assumed relationship:

$$T = k \cdot D^{2+2c-e} \cdot n^{1+c-e} \cdot \rho^{c} \cdot \mu^{1-c} \cdot V_A^{e}$$

Rearranging to group terms with each free variable:

$$T = k \cdot \left(D^{2} n^{1}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\rho n D^{2}}{\mu}\right)^{c} \cdot \left(\frac{V_A}{nD}\right)^{e}$$

Verifying dimensional homogeneity of the prefactor:

$$\begin{aligned}
[D^{2} n] &= [L^{2} T^{-1}] \\
[T / (D^{2} n)] &= \frac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^{2} T^{-1}]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-1}] = [\mu]
\end{aligned}$$

So the prefactor has dimensions of viscosity, and we write:

$$\frac{T}{\mu D^{2} n} = f\!\left(\frac{\rho n D^{2}}{\mu}, \frac{V_A}{nD}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{\rho n^{2} D^{4}} = \phi\!\left(\frac{\rho n D^{2}}{\mu}, \frac{V_A}{nD}\right)}$$

That is:

$$\boxed{K_T = \phi(R_n^{\prime}, J)}$$

Where:
- $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^{2} D^{4}}$ is the **Thrust Coefficient**
- $R_n^{\prime} = \dfrac{\rho n D^{2}}{\mu}$ is the **Propeller Reynolds Number**
- $J = \dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ is the **Advance Coefficient**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The non-dimensional thrust coefficient $K_T$ is a universal function of the Reynolds number and the advance coefficient. This is the entire theoretical foundation of the **$K_T$–$K_Q$–$J$ curves** used worldwide in propeller design and tested in cavitation tunnels.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dimensional Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete operational workflow of applying dimensional analysis to a ship propulsion problem — from variable identification to model-test extrapolation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[STEP 0: PROBLEM STATEMENT] --> A1[Identify the physical phenomenon e.g. ship hull resistance]
    A1 --> A2[List all relevant dimensional variables: V, L, rho, nu, g, R]
    A2 --> A3[Express each variable in M L T fundamental dimensions]
    A3 --> A4[Count n variables and k fundamental dimensions]
    A4 --> A5{Choose method: Rayleigh or Buckingham Pi}
    A5 -->|Rayleigh| A6[Assume power product X1 = k X2^a1 X3^a2 ...]
    A5 -->|Buckingham| A7[Select k repeating variables containing all M L T]
    A6 --> A8[Enforce dimensional homogeneity on each exponent]
    A7 --> A9[Form n minus k dimensionless Pi groups]
    A8 --> A10[Solve system of linear equations for exponents]
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Obtain the dimensionless Pi groups: pi1, pi2, pi3]
    A11 --> A12[Write functional relationship f pi1 pi2 pi3 equals 0]
    A12 --> A13[Identify physical meaning: Froude, Reynolds, Euler, Cavitation]
    A13 --> A14[Apply to model testing: equate dimensionless groups model equals ship]
    A14 --> A15[Predict full scale ship performance from model test data]
    A15 --> A16[VALIDATE: Engineer uses results for hull and propeller design]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Key Dimensionless Groups in Ship Propulsion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Reynolds Number Rn] -->|Viscous forces| B5[Viscous Resistance Rv]
    B2[Froude Number Fn] -->|Gravitational forces| B6[Wave Resistance Rw]
    B3[Euler Number Eu] -->|Pressure forces| B7[Cavitation Number sigma]
    B4[Weber Number We] -->|Surface tension| B8[Surface Tension Effects]
    B5 --> B9[Total Hull Resistance RT]
    B6 --> B9
    B7 --> B10[Propeller Cavitation Onset]
    B8 --> B11[Free Surface Phenomena]
    B9 --> B12[Effective Power PE]
    B10 --> B13[Propeller Design Constraints]
    B12 --> B14[Ship Performance Prediction]
    B11 --> B14
    B13 --> B14
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Model-to-Prototype Extrapolation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[FULL SCALE SHIP: Length Ls, Speed Vs, Density rhos] --> C2[Define geometric scale factor lambda = Lm / Ls]
    C2 --> C3[Set dimensionless similarity criteria: Fn_m equals Fn_s and Rn_m equals Rn_s]
    C3 --> C4{Conflict: Fn and Rn cannot be simultaneously matched}
    C4 --> C5[ITTC 1978 Split: Frictional Rn based, Residual Fn based]
    C5 --> C6[Measure model resistance Rm in towing tank]
    C6 --> C7[Compute friction resistance using ITTC 1957 line]
    C7 --> C8[Subtract friction to get residual resistance Cr]
    C8 --> C9[Scale residual resistance to full scale via Froude similarity]
    C9 --> C10[Add full scale friction to get total full scale resistance Rs]
    C10 --> C11[Compute effective power PE equals Rs times Vs]
    C11 --> C12[Apply propulsive coefficient to get brake power PB]
    C12 --> C13[FINAL DESIGN: Select main engine rating]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The architecture above shows that every step from a raw set of dimensional variables down to a final ship-engine power rating flows through dimensionless groups. This is the *practical engineering reason* why dimensional analysis is treated as a Module-1 cornerstone in the KTU 2024 Ship Propulsion syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Dimensional Homogeneity**. State the **Buckingham $\pi$ Theorem** and explain its significance in ship model testing.

**Model Answer:**

**Dimensional Homogeneity:** A physical equation is said to be dimensionally homogeneous if every term on both sides of the equation has the **same fundamental dimensions**. For example, in $V = \sqrt{2gh}$, both $V$ and $\sqrt{2gh}$ have dimensions of $[LT^{-1}]$. This is a *necessary* condition for any physically valid equation.

**Buckingham $\pi$ Theorem:** *"If a physical phenomenon involves $n$ dimensional variables and $k$ fundamental dimensions, then the relationship among them can be expressed in terms of $(n - k)$ independent dimensionless groups called $\pi$ terms."*

Mathematically:

$$\boxed{f(\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_{n-k}) = 0}$$

**Significance in Ship Model Testing:**
- Reduces the number of variables that must be studied experimentally.
- Establishes the **similarity laws** between model and prototype (e.g., Froude similarity for wave resistance).
- Enables extrapolation of model test data (conducted in towing tanks) to predict full-scale ship performance.
- Forms the theoretical basis of all cavitation tunnel tests for marine propellers.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Rayleigh's Method** and **Buckingham $\pi$ Theorem** as applied to ship propulsion problems. List the dimensionless groups derived for **ship resistance**.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Rayleigh's Method | Buckingham $\pi$ Theorem |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Approach** | Assumes a power-product form of the relationship | Systematic selection of repeating variables to form $\pi$ groups |
| **Mathematical Basis** | Equating exponents of each fundamental dimension | Linear algebra: matrix of dimensional exponents |
| **Variable Limit** | Practical up to 4–5 variables (otherwise underdetermined) | Handles any number of variables systematically |
| **Number of $\pi$ Groups** | Not explicitly pre-determined | Exactly $n - k$ groups guaranteed |
| **Use in Ship Propulsion** | Quick estimation of trends | Standard tool for model-to-prototype scaling |

**Dimensionless Groups for Ship Resistance:**

1. **Resistance Coefficient:** $C_R = \dfrac{R}{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}$
2. **Reynolds Number:** $R_n = \dfrac{VL}{\nu} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$
3. **Froude Number:** $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$

Final functional form: $C_R = f(R_n, F_n)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] The resistance $R$ experienced by a ship of length $L$ moving at speed $V$ through sea water of density $\rho$ and kinematic viscosity $\nu$, under gravity $g$, is given by a relation of the form $R = f(V, L, \rho, \nu, g)$. Apply **Buckingham's $\pi$ Theorem** to obtain the dimensionless groups governing the resistance.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] A model of length $L_m = 3 \text{ m}$ is towed in fresh water at speed $V_m$ such that **Froude similarity** is maintained with a prototype of length $L_s = 150 \text{ m}$. If the prototype speed is $V_s = 15 \text{ m/s}$ and the model's measured resistance is $R_m = 12 \text{ N}$, find: (i) the model speed, (ii) the expected full-scale resistance (neglect Reynolds number effects). Take $\rho_m = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\rho_s = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1: List Variables**

$$R, \quad V, \quad L, \quad \rho, \quad \nu, \quad g$$

So $n = 6$ variables.

**Step 2: Fundamental Dimensions**

Using $M, L, T$ system: $k = 3$.

**Step 3: Number of $\pi$ Groups**

$$\text{Number of } \pi \text{ groups} = n - k = 6 - 3 = 3$$

**Step 4: Choose Repeating Variables**

Select: $\rho, V, L$ — they collectively contain all $M, L, T$ and are dimensionally independent.

**Step 5: Form $\pi$ Groups**

- $\pi_1 = R \rho^{a_1} V^{b_1} L^{c_1}$
- $\pi_2 = \nu \rho^{a_2} V^{b_2} L^{c_2}$
- $\pi_3 = g \rho^{a_3} V^{b_3} L^{c_3}$

Solving each (as shown in Section 3.1):

$$\boxed{\pi_1 = \frac{R}{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}, \quad \pi_2 = \frac{VL}{\nu}, \quad \pi_3 = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}}$$

**Step 6: Final Relationship**

$$\boxed{f\!\left(\frac{R}{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}, \frac{VL}{\nu}, \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}\right) = 0}$$

Or: $C_R = f(R_n, F_n)$.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**(i) Model Speed:**

For Froude similarity: $F_{n,m} = F_{n,s}$

$$\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}$$

$$V_m = V_s \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_s}} = 15 \times \sqrt{\frac{3}{150}} = 15 \times \sqrt{0.02} = 15 \times 0.1414 = 2.121 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_m \approx 2.12 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Full-Scale Resistance:**

From Froude similarity, the resistance coefficient is the same:

$$\frac{R_m}{\rho_m V_m^{2} L_m^{2}} = \frac{R_s}{\rho_s V_s^{2} L_s^{2}}$$

$$R_s = R_m \cdot \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} \cdot \left(\frac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^{2}$$

Compute the speed ratio:

$$\frac{V_s}{V_m} = \frac{15}{2.121} = 7.071 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^{2} = 50$$

Length ratio:

$$\frac{L_s}{L_m} = \frac{150}{3} = 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^{2} = 2500$$

Density ratio:

$$\frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} = \frac{1025}{1000} = 1.025$$

Substitute:

$$R_s = 12 \times 1.025 \times 50 \times 2500$$

$$R_s = 12 \times 1.025 \times 125{,}000 = 12 \times 128{,}125 = 1{,}537{,}500 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{R_s \approx 1.5375 \text{ MN}}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Using **Rayleigh's Method**, derive the functional relationship for the **pressure drop $\Delta p$** in a pipeline carrying oil of density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$, with pipe diameter $D$, flow velocity $V$, and pipe length $L$. Express the final answer in terms of dimensionless groups.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] A naval architect is to design a 1:25 scale model of a submarine to be tested in a water tunnel. The prototype submarine operates at $15 \text{ knots}$ in sea water ($\rho_s = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu_s = 1.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$). The water tunnel uses fresh water ($\rho_m = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu_m = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$). Calculate the **water tunnel speed** required for **Reynolds similarity**. Comment on whether this speed is practical.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1: Variables**

$$\Delta p, \quad D, \quad L, \quad V, \quad \rho, \quad \mu$$

**Step 2: Assume Power-Product**

$$\Delta p = k \cdot D^{a} \cdot L^{b} \cdot V^{c} \cdot \rho^{d} \cdot \mu^{e}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Dimensions**

$$\begin{aligned}
[ML^{-1}T^{-2}] &= [L]^{a} \cdot [L]^{b} \cdot [LT^{-1}]^{c} \cdot [ML^{-3}]^{d} \cdot [ML^{-1}T^{-1}]^{e} \\
&= [M^{d+e} \cdot L^{a+b+c-3d-e} \cdot T^{-c-e}]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Equate Exponents**

- $M$: $d + e = 1 \Rightarrow e = 1 - d$
- $T$: $-c - e = -2 \Rightarrow c = 2 - e = 1 + d$
- $L$: $a + b + c - 3d - e = -1 \Rightarrow a + b + (1+d) - 3d - (1-d) = -1$
  $$\Rightarrow a + b + 1 + d - 3d - 1 + d = -1 \Rightarrow a + b - d = -1 \Rightarrow a = -1 - b + d$$

**Step 5: Choose Free Variables**

Let $b$ and $d$ be free. Then:

$$c = 1 + d, \quad e = 1 - d, \quad a = -1 - b + d$$

**Step 6: Substitute Back**

$$\Delta p = k \cdot D^{-1-b+d} \cdot L^{b} \cdot V^{1+d} \cdot \rho^{d} \cdot \mu^{1-d}$$

Rearranging:

$$\Delta p = k \cdot \left(\frac{\mu V}{D}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{L}{D}\right)^{b} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho V D}{\mu}\right)^{d}$$

Verifying the prefactor:

$$\frac{\mu V}{D} = \frac{[ML^{-1}T^{-1}][LT^{-1}]}{[L]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}] = [\Delta p] \;\; \checkmark$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta p \cdot D}{\mu V} = \phi\!\left(\frac{L}{D}, \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}\right) = \phi\!\left(\frac{L}{D}, R_n\right)}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Convert Prototype Speed**

$$V_s = 15 \text{ knots} = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Apply Reynolds Similarity**

For Reynolds similarity:

$$R_{n,m} = R_{n,s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\rho_m V_m L_m}{\mu_m} = \frac{\rho_s V_s L_s}{\mu_s}$$

$$V_m = V_s \cdot \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} \cdot \frac{\mu_m}{\mu_s} \cdot \frac{L_s}{L_m}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

Geometric scale: $\dfrac{L_s}{L_m} = 25$

Density ratio: $\dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} = \dfrac{1025}{1000} = 1.025$

Viscosity ratio: $\dfrac{\mu_m}{\mu_s} = \dfrac{1.00 \times 10^{-3}}{1.08 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.9259$

$$V_m = 7.716 \times 1.025 \times 0.9259 \times 25$$

$$V_m = 7.716 \times 23.72 = 183.04 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_m \approx 183 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Comment on Practicality:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Practicality Comment:** A water tunnel speed of $\approx 183$ m/s (about 356 knots) is **completely impractical** for routine model testing. Water tunnels typically operate at speeds of $5$–$15$ m/s. This is the classic *scale-effect dilemma* in ship model testing: satisfying Reynolds similarity requires unrealistically high model speeds (and would also induce cavitation). The standard naval-architecture workaround is to test at Froude similarity (manageable speeds) and use empirical corrections (such as the ITTC 1978 formulation) to account for Reynolds number differences. **This very example is often posed in KTU exams to test whether students understand the practical limitations of strict dimensional similarity.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Dimensional Analysis Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Choosing the wrong repeating variables:** Students often pick repeating variables that do *not* collectively contain all fundamental dimensions, leading to an unsolvable $\pi$ group. Always verify that the repeating variables span $M, L, T$ (and $\theta$ if temperature is involved).
> 
> 2. **Confusing $\pi$ groups with $\pi$ values:** $\pi_2 = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ is the **Reynolds number itself**, not its inverse. Some students write the inverse by mistake.
> 
> 3. **Unit mismatches in Froude/Reynolds calculations:** Forgetting to convert knots to m/s in marine problems is a common $1$–$2$ mark deduction.
> 
> 4. **Assuming both $F_n$ and $R_n$ similarity can be satisfied simultaneously:** In ship model testing, this is *impossible* unless the model fluid is changed. Examiners specifically look for awareness of this conflict.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the density ratio in resistance scaling:** Full-scale resistance depends on $\rho_s / \rho_m$, not just length and speed ratios.
> 
> 6. **Confusing "number of $\pi$ groups" with "number of variables":** The answer is always $n - k$, not $n$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dimensional Analysis for KTU PBNST404**

- **Dimensional Homogeneity** is a *necessary* (not sufficient) condition for any physical equation to be correct.
- **Buckingham $\pi$ Theorem:** Number of independent $\pi$ groups = $n - k$ (where $n$ = variables, $k$ = fundamental dimensions).
- **Repeating variables** must (a) collectively contain all $M, L, T$ and (b) be dimensionally independent.
- **Froude Number** $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ → governs **wave-making resistance** and free-surface flows.
- **Reynolds Number** $R_n = \rho V L / \mu$ → governs **viscous resistance** and boundary layer behavior.
- **Euler Number** $E_u = \Delta p / (\rho V^{2})$ → governs **pressure forces**; central to **cavitation analysis**.
- **Cavitation Number** $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v) / (\frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2})$ → defines the **cavitation inception** boundary.
- **Thrust Coefficient** $K_T = T / (\rho n^{2} D^{4})$; **Torque Coefficient** $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^{2} D^{5})$; **Advance Coefficient** $J = V_A / (nD)$.
- **In ship model testing, $F_n$ and $R_n$ cannot be simultaneously matched** at the same model scale — this is the famous *scale-effect problem* in naval architecture.
- **ITTC 1978 procedure** splits resistance into frictional (Reynolds-based) + residual (Froude-based) — directly derived from dimensional analysis.
- **Rayleigh's method** is fast but limited; **Buckingham's $\pi$ method** is systematic and universally applicable.
- Every term in a physically valid equation **must** have the same fundamental dimensions.
- KTU expects: ability to (1) apply $\pi$ theorem from scratch, (2) identify dimensionless groups, (3) solve scaling problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interaction Between Hull and Propeller - Wake and Wake Fraction, Resistance Augmentation and Thrust Deduction, Propulsive Efficiency in Open Water and Behind Conditions, Hull Efficiency, Quasi Propulsive Coefficient, Transmission Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST404 - SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interaction Between Hull and Propeller - Wake and Wake Fraction, Resistance Augmentation and Thrust Deduction, Propulsive Efficiency in Open Water and Behind Conditions, Hull Efficiency, Quasi Propulsive Coefficient, Transmission Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Hull-Propeller Interaction

When a ship moves through water, the hull and the propeller do not operate in isolation. The hull creates a flow field that disturbs the water entering the propeller disc, while the propeller in turn alters the pressure field around the stern of the ship, modifying the resistance of the hull. This **mutual interaction** is the foundation of ship propulsion analysis and is essential for predicting the speed, power, and efficiency of a vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Hull–propeller interaction refers to the combined hydrodynamic effect where the velocity of water approaching the propeller (wake) is different from the ship's speed, and the thrust produced by the propeller is partly absorbed in overcoming the additional resistance created by the propeller's suction effect on the hull.

## 1.2 Wake and Wake Fraction — Formal Definition

**Wake (W)** is defined as the difference between the speed of the ship ($V_S$) and the speed of the water actually arriving at the propeller disc ($V_A$).

$$V_A = V_S - W$$

The **Wake Fraction (w)** is the non-dimensional form of wake, expressed as a fraction of either the ship's speed or the speed of advance, depending on convention.

**Taylor's Wake Fraction:**
$$w_T = \frac{V_S - V_A}{V_S}$$

**Effective Wake Fraction (used in KTU problems):**
$$w = \frac{V_S - V_A}{V_A}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine walking forward on a moving walkway (like at an airport) while wearing roller skates. The walkway moves backward at a certain speed relative to you. Now imagine a fan (propeller) placed behind you that pulls air. The air the fan "sees" is not the still room air — it is the air disturbed by your body and the moving walkway. The fan has to work in this disturbed flow. Similarly, the propeller on a ship operates in water that has been disturbed (slowed and rotated) by the hull moving through it. This "disturbance" is the **wake**.

## 1.4 Thrust Deduction — Formal Definition

When the propeller operates behind the hull, it creates a low-pressure region at the stern, which **increases the resistance** of the hull. This increase in resistance is called the **resistance augmentation** ($\Delta R$). To overcome this, the propeller must produce a thrust $T$ that is greater than the resistance $R$ of the bare hull.

The **Thrust Deduction Fraction (t)** is defined as:

$$t = \frac{T - R}{T} = 1 - \frac{R}{T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The effective thrust $T_E$ (also called the useful thrust) is the thrust available to overcome the ship's resistance:
> $$T_E = T(1 - t) = R$$

## 1.5 Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient (QPC) and Related Efficiencies

The overall efficiency chain of ship propulsion links the delivered power at the engine to the effective power required to tow the ship:

$$\eta_D = \frac{P_E}{P_D} = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R$$

where each term represents a stage of power loss. These efficiencies are discussed in detail in later sections.

## 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Ship Speed | $V_S$ | 10–30 | knots |
| Wake Fraction | $w$ | 0.2–0.4 | dimensionless |
| Thrust Deduction | $t$ | 0.1–0.25 | dimensionless |
| Hull Efficiency | $\eta_H$ | 1.0–1.3 | dimensionless |
| Open Water Efficiency | $\eta_O$ | 0.5–0.7 | dimensionless |
| Rotational Efficiency | $\eta_R$ | 0.95–1.05 | dimensionless |
| QPC ($\eta_D$) | $\eta_D$ | 0.5–0.65 | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of the Velocity of Advance

The velocity of water at the propeller disc is influenced by three components:

1. **Axial Velocity Component** — caused by the boundary layer and the displacement effect of the hull.
2. **Tangential Velocity Component** — caused by the rotation of the boundary layer around the hull.
3. **Radial Velocity Component** — usually small and neglected in standard analysis.

The **Wake** is the vector difference between ship velocity and water velocity at the propeller disc.

## 2.2 Why Wake Exists — The "Why" and "How"

**Why does wake occur?**
- The hull has a finite thickness and pushes water aside as it moves.
- Frictional effects in the boundary layer slow the water near the hull.
- The pressure field around the stern causes inward and rotational flow.

**How is wake classified?**

| Type of Wake | Description | Symbol |
|--------------|-------------|--------|
| **Frictional Wake** | Caused by viscous boundary layer | $w_F$ |
| **Wave Wake** | Caused by wave generation at stern | $w_W$ |
| **Potential Wake** | Caused by inviscid flow effects | $w_P$ |
| **Total Wake** | Combined effect | $w = w_F + w_W + w_P$ |

## 2.3 Resistance Augmentation — Detailed Mechanism

When the propeller rotates behind the hull, it draws water forward (axial induction) and accelerates it. This causes:

- A **drop in pressure** at the stern (propeller suction).
- An **increase in frictional resistance** due to accelerated flow along the hull.
- A **change in wave pattern** at the stern.

The total effective resistance of the ship with propeller working is:
$$R_{eff} = R + \Delta R$$

where $\Delta R$ is the **resistance augmentation**.

The thrust deduction fraction is directly linked to this:
$$t = \frac{\Delta R}{T} = \frac{T - R}{T}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definition |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Wake | $W = V_S - V_A$ | Difference of ship speed and advance speed |
| 2 | Taylor's Wake Fraction | $w_T = W / V_S$ | Wake / Ship speed |
| 3 | Effective Wake Fraction | $w = W / V_A$ | Wake / Advance speed |
| 4 | Advance Coefficient | $J = V_A / (nD)$ | Advance / (rpm × diameter) |
| 5 | Thrust Deduction | $t = (T - R) / T$ | Ratio of resistance augmentation to thrust |
| 6 | Effective Thrust | $T_E = T(1-t)$ | Useful thrust |
| 7 | Hull Efficiency | $\eta_H = (1-t) / (1-w)$ | Propulsive efficiency gain due to wake |
| 8 | Open Water Efficiency | $\eta_O = (T \cdot V_A) / (2\pi n Q)$ | Thrust power / rotational power |
| 9 | Rotational Efficiency | $\eta_R = \eta_O / \eta_P$ | Ratio (accounts for rotational losses) |
| 10 | Propulsive Efficiency | $\eta_P = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$ | Combined hull-propeller efficiency |
| 11 | Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient | $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R$ | EHP / DHP |
| 12 | Transmission Efficiency | $\eta_{tr} = P_D / P_S$ | Delivered / Shaft power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on $\eta_R$:** In some KTU references, $\eta_R$ is the **rotational efficiency** (= $\eta_O / \eta_P$), and in others, it is the **transmission efficiency** (= $P_D / P_S$). In the 2024 scheme, the convention is that $\eta_R$ in $\eta_D = \eta_O \eta_H \eta_R$ is the **rotational efficiency**, while transmission efficiency is separate. Always verify the convention in the question.

## 2.5 Physical Meaning of Hull Efficiency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $\eta_H > 1$ possible?** Because the propeller works in a slower water flow (wake), the thrust produced for a given power is greater than in open water. The hull therefore "helps" the propeller by reducing the relative velocity. This is why $\eta_H$ can exceed unity, typically ranging from **1.0 to 1.3**.

**Engineering Utility:** Hull efficiency is one of the few efficiencies in marine propulsion that can be **greater than 1**, indicating that the wake improves propulsive performance. Designers aim to optimize hull form to maximize $\eta_H$ without increasing $R$ excessively.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern ship design (KRISO, Hyundai Heavy Industries, naval architects), these concepts are used to:

- Predict the **delivered power** from sea trial data.
- Conduct **self-propulsion model tests** in towing tanks.
- Optimize the **wake field** through stern bulb design or wake equalizing ducts.
- Design **energy-saving devices** (ESDs) like Mewis ducts, propeller boss cap fins (PBCF).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hull Efficiency Formula

Starting from the fundamental definitions:

**Step 1:** Define the resistance of the bare hull as $R$ (in Newtons). The **effective thrust** required to overcome this resistance is:
$$T_E = R$$

**Step 2:** The total thrust produced by the propeller is $T$, and due to resistance augmentation, it is greater than $R$:
$$T = R + \Delta R$$

**Step 3:** The thrust deduction fraction is defined as:
$$t = \frac{\Delta R}{T} = \frac{T - R}{T}$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging for effective thrust:
$$T_E = T(1 - t)$$

**Step 5:** The power delivered to the ship (effective power) is:
$$P_E = R \cdot V_S = T(1-t) \cdot V_S$$

**Step 6:** The power delivered to the propeller (thrust power) in behind condition is:
$$P_T = T \cdot V_A$$

**Step 7:** The hull efficiency is defined as the ratio of useful power to thrust power:
$$\eta_H = \frac{P_E}{P_T} = \frac{T(1-t) \cdot V_S}{T \cdot V_A}$$

**Step 8:** Substituting $V_S = V_A(1 + w)$ from the wake definition $V_A = V_S(1-w)$ rearranged:
$$\eta_H = \frac{(1-t)(1+w)}{1} \approx \frac{1-t}{1-w}$$

**Step 9:** This is the standard hull efficiency formula used in KTU examinations:
$$\boxed{\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact derivation uses $V_S = V_A(1 + w)$ where $w = W/V_A$, but for small wake values, the approximation is widely accepted in KTU problems.

## 3.2 Derivation of Open Water Efficiency

The open water efficiency describes the propeller working in an **infinite, undisturbed water stream**.

**Step 1:** The thrust power (useful work per second by the propeller) is:
$$P_T = T \cdot V_A$$

**Step 2:** The torque power delivered to the propeller shaft is:
$$P_Q = 2\pi n Q$$

where $n$ is the rotational speed in revolutions per second (Hz) and $Q$ is the torque in N·m.

**Step 3:** The open water efficiency is:
$$\eta_O = \frac{P_T}{P_Q} = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2\pi n Q}$$

**Step 4:** Using the advance coefficient $J = V_A / (nD)$:
$$\eta_O = \frac{T}{2\pi n^2 D^2 / J \cdot Q^{-1}} = \frac{J \cdot K_T}{2\pi K_Q}$$

where $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$ and $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$ are the standard propeller coefficients.

## 3.3 Derivation of Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient

**Step 1:** The QPC is defined as the ratio of effective power to delivered power:
$$\eta_D = \frac{P_E}{P_D}$$

**Step 2:** The effective power is:
$$P_E = R \cdot V_S = T(1-t) \cdot V_S$$

**Step 3:** The delivered power is the power at the propeller shaft in behind condition:
$$P_D = 2\pi n Q$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:
$$\eta_D = \frac{T(1-t) V_S}{2\pi n Q}$$

**Step 5:** Separating the terms:
$$\eta_D = \frac{T V_A}{2\pi n Q} \cdot \frac{(1-t) V_S}{V_A} = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$$

**Step 6:** Including rotational efficiency (which accounts for the difference between model and full-scale rotation, wake rotation effects):
$$\boxed{\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem Solving Framework

| Given | Find | Formula Chain |
|-------|------|---------------|
| $V_S$, $V_A$ | $w$ | $w = (V_S - V_A)/V_A$ |
| $T$, $R$ | $t$ | $t = (T - R)/T$ |
| $w$, $t$ | $\eta_H$ | $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$ |
| $\eta_O$, $\eta_H$ | $\eta_P$ | $\eta_P = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$ |
| $\eta_O$, $\eta_H$, $\eta_R$ | $\eta_D$ | $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R$ |
| $P_D$, $\eta_D$ | $P_E$ | $P_E = \eta_D \cdot P_D$ |
| $P_E$, $V_S$ | $R$ | $R = P_E / V_S$ |
| $P_D$, $\eta_{tr}$ | $P_S$ | $P_S = P_D / \eta_{tr}$ |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A ship moves at $V_S = 20$ knots. The wake fraction is $w = 0.30$ and the thrust deduction fraction is $t = 0.20$. The open water efficiency is $\eta_O = 0.60$ and the rotational efficiency is $\eta_R = 1.00$. Find:
(a) Hull efficiency
(b) Propulsive efficiency
(c) QPC
(d) If the delivered power is $P_D = 10{,}000$ kW, find the effective power.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Hull efficiency:
$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w} = \frac{1 - 0.20}{1 - 0.30} = \frac{0.80}{0.70} = 1.143$$

**(b)** Propulsive efficiency:
$$\eta_P = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H = 0.60 \times 1.143 = 0.686$$

**(c)** Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient:
$$\eta_D = \eta_P \cdot \eta_R = 0.686 \times 1.00 = 0.686$$

**(d)** Effective power:
$$P_E = \eta_D \cdot P_D = 0.686 \times 10{,}000 = 6{,}860 \text{ kW}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hull-Propeller Interaction Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the energy flow and the various efficiency links between the engine, shaft, propeller, hull, and the surrounding water.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
