<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the following terms with neat sketches:**
(i) Wake fraction
(ii) Thrust deduction fraction
(iii) Hull efficiency

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Wake Fraction (w):** It is the ratio of the wake velocity to the speed of advance of the propeller. Mathematically:
$$w = \frac{V_S - V_A}{V_A}$$

**(ii) Thrust Deduction Fraction (t):** It is the ratio of the increase in resistance (due to propeller suction) to the total thrust developed by the propeller:
$$t = \frac{T - R}{T}$$

**(iii) Hull Efficiency ($\eta_H$):** It is the ratio of the effective power to the thrust power, expressed as:
$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w}$$

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the physical significance of hull efficiency being greater than unity.**

**Model Answer:**
The hull efficiency can exceed unity because the wake at the propeller disc is **slower** than the ship's speed. The propeller therefore produces thrust at a lower speed ($V_A$ instead of $V_S$), and this thrust is more effective in overcoming the ship's resistance. As a result, the effective power $P_E$ required to tow the ship at $V_S$ is greater than the thrust power $P_T$ absorbed by the propeller. Since $\eta_H = P_E / P_T$, the ratio can exceed 1. Typical values are **1.0 to 1.3**, indicating that the wake phenomenon actually improves the overall propulsive performance of the ship.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the hull efficiency of a single-screw ship in terms of wake fraction and thrust deduction fraction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** From the definition of wake:
$$V_S = V_A(1 + w) \quad \text{or} \quad V_A = V_S(1 - w)$$

**Step 2:** Effective thrust is the thrust available to overcome ship resistance:
$$T_E = R = T(1 - t)$$

**Step 3:** Effective power required to tow the ship at $V_S$:
$$P_E = R \cdot V_S = T(1 - t) \cdot V_S$$

**Step 4:** Thrust power delivered by the propeller at $V_A$:
$$P_T = T \cdot V_A$$

**Step 5:** Hull efficiency is defined as the ratio:
$$\eta_H = \frac{P_E}{P_T} = \frac{T(1-t) V_S}{T \cdot V_A} = \frac{(1-t) V_S}{V_A}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $V_S = V_A(1+w)$:
$$\eta_H = \frac{(1-t)(1+w)}{1} \approx \frac{1-t}{1-w}$$

**Step 7:** The result:
$$\boxed{\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w}}$$

**(b)** A model of a ship is tested in a towing tank at a speed of $V_S = 4$ m/s. The speed of advance of the model propeller is $V_A = 3$ m/s. The thrust produced is $T = 80$ N and the resistance of the model is $R = 70$ N. Calculate the wake fraction, thrust deduction fraction, hull efficiency, and propulsive efficiency if $\eta_O = 0.55$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Wake fraction:
$$w = \frac{V_S - V_A}{V_A} = \frac{4 - 3}{3} = 0.333$$

**Step 2:** Thrust deduction fraction:
$$t = \frac{T - R}{T} = \frac{80 - 70}{80} = 0.125$$

**Step 3:** Hull efficiency:
$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \frac{1 - 0.125}{1 - 0.333} = \frac{0.875}{0.667} = 1.312$$

**Step 4:** Propulsive efficiency:
$$\eta_P = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H = 0.55 \times 1.312 = 0.722$$

**Result:** $w = 0.333$, $t = 0.125$, $\eta_H = 1.312$, $\eta_P = 0.722$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define and explain the Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient (QPC) of a ship. Derive the relationship between QPC, open water efficiency, hull efficiency, and rotational efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The QPC is defined as the ratio of effective power (EHP) to delivered power (DHP):
$$\eta_D = \frac{P_E}{P_D}$$

**Step 2:** Effective power: $P_E = R \cdot V_S$

**Step 3:** Delivered power: $P_D = 2\pi n Q$

**Step 4:** Substituting $R = T(1-t)$ and $V_S = V_A(1+w)$:
$$\eta_D = \frac{T(1-t) V_A(1+w)}{2\pi n Q}$$

**Step 5:** Splitting the terms:
$$\eta_D = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2\pi n Q} \cdot (1-t)(1+w)$$

**Step 6:** The first term is the open water efficiency:
$$\eta_O = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2\pi n Q}$$

**Step 7:** The second term is approximately the hull efficiency:
$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w} \approx (1-t)(1+w)$$

**Step 8:** The rotational efficiency $\eta_R$ is included to account for the difference between behind and open water conditions:
$$\boxed{\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R}$$

**(b)** A twin-screw ship has the following data: $V_S = 18$ knots, $w = 0.28$, $t = 0.18$, $\eta_O = 0.58$, $\eta_R = 0.98$. The delivered power is $P_D = 8000$ kW. Calculate the hull efficiency, propulsive efficiency, QPC, and effective power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Hull efficiency:
$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - 0.18}{1 - 0.28} = \frac{0.82}{0.72} = 1.139$$

**Step 2:** Propulsive efficiency:
$$\eta_P = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H = 0.58 \times 1.139 = 0.661$$

**Step 3:** QPC:
$$\eta_D = \eta_P \cdot \eta_R = 0.661 \times 0.98 = 0.648$$

**Step 4:** Effective power:
$$P_E = \eta_D \cdot P_D = 0.648 \times 8000 = 5184 \text{ kW}$$

**Result:** $\eta_H = 1.139$, $\eta_P = 0.661$, $\eta_D = 0.648$, $P_E = 5184$ kW

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Lead to Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Wrong Wake Formula:** Many students use $w = (V_S - V_A) / V_S$ (Taylor's form) instead of $w = (V_S - V_A) / V_A$ (effective form). For KTU 2024, the **effective wake fraction** is standard unless specifically mentioned otherwise.
> 2. **Confusion between $\eta_R$ (rotational) and transmission efficiency:** Always read the question carefully. In $\eta_D = \eta_O \eta_H \eta_R$, the $\eta_R$ is **rotational efficiency**. Transmission efficiency appears separately as $P_D / P_S$.
> 3. **Sign errors in $t$:** Some students write $t = (R - T)/T$ which gives a negative value. The correct form is $t = (T - R)/T$ because thrust exceeds resistance.
> 4. **Forgetting units conversion:** $V_S$ in knots must often be converted to m/s using **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s** for SI calculations.
> 5. **Assuming $\eta_H \leq 1$:** Hull efficiency can be **greater than 1**. Do not mark it as a calculation error.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**
>
> - **Wake (W):** $W = V_S - V_A$. Wake arises due to boundary layer, wave-making, and potential flow effects at the stern.
> - **Wake Fraction (w):** $w = W / V_A = (V_S - V_A) / V_A$. Typical range: 0.2–0.4.
> - **Thrust Deduction (t):** $t = (T - R) / T$. Caused by propeller suction increasing stern resistance.
> - **Resistance Augmentation ($\Delta R$):** $\Delta R = T - R$.
> - **Hull Efficiency ($\eta_H$):** $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$. Can be **greater than 1**.
> - **Open Water Efficiency ($\eta_O$):** $\eta_O = T V_A / (2\pi n Q)$. Measured for a propeller in undisturbed water.
> - **Propulsive Efficiency ($\eta_P$):** $\eta_P = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$.
> - **Rotational Efficiency ($\eta_R$):** $\eta_R = \eta_O / \eta_P$ (model to full-scale correction). Usually **slightly less than 1**.
> - **Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient ($\eta_D$):** $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R = P_E / P_D$.
> - **Transmission Efficiency ($\eta_{tr}$):** $\eta_{tr} = P_D / P_S$ (delivered / shaft power).
> - **Speed Conversion:** **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s** (essential for SI unit problems).
> - **Typical QPC values:** **0.5 to 0.65** for well-designed single-screw ships.
> - **Always identify the convention** for wake fraction (Taylor vs. Effective) before solving.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powering Cavitation - Types, Cavitation Number, Effects of Cavitation, Prevention of Cavitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST404 - SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Powering Cavitation - Types, Cavitation Number, Effects of Cavitation, Prevention of Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation, growth, and violent collapse of vapor cavities (or bubbles) within a liquid flow when the local static pressure falls below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. In ship propulsion, this phenomenon occurs predominantly on the **suction side (low-pressure face)** of a rotating marine propeller blade, where local pressure depression caused by the rotational velocity field drops below the saturated vapor pressure of seawater.

In the context of ship propulsion, cavitation is not a "boiling" phenomenon in the thermal sense, but rather a **hydromechanical phase change** — liquid water flashes into water vapor (and dissolved gases come out of solution) because the local static pressure $P$ at a point on the blade surface becomes less than the vapor pressure $P_v$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this critical for ship propellers?**
> - Marine propellers operate at high rotational speeds (often 80–200 RPM for large ships, higher for smaller vessels).
> - The blade tip velocities can exceed **40–50 m/s** in modern high-powered vessels.
> - According to Bernoulli's principle, when velocity is high, pressure is low. If pressure drops below $P_v$ → cavitation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Pass"

Imagine a flowing river running over a rocky terrain. As water flows over a submerged rock, it speeds up over the top (where the cross-section narrows). This high velocity creates a low-pressure region on the top of the rock. If the rock is high enough, the water pressure on top may drop so much that the water "boils" at ambient temperature — forming vapor bubbles.

Now imagine the rock has a sharp, irregular shape (like a propeller blade cross-section). As the water rushes past, it separates at the trailing edge, creating vortices and turbulent low-pressure zones. When these vapor bubbles travel into regions of higher pressure (downstream of the blade), they **collapse violently** — like little implosions. This is cavitation.

> [!TIP]
> **The four-step "Life Cycle" of a cavitation bubble:**
> 1. **Nucleation** – Microscopic gas pockets (nuclei) or vapor embryo forms.
> 2. **Growth** – Bubble expands as it travels through low-pressure region.
> 3. **Transport** – Bubble moves with the flow along/around the blade.
> 4. **Collapse (Implosion)** – Bubble enters high-pressure region → violent condensation → micro-jet of water strikes the blade surface (causing erosion).

### 1.3 Critical Physical Constants & Reference Values

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (Sea Conditions) |
|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric Pressure (Sea Level) | $P_{atm}$ | **101,325 Pa = 1.01325 bar** |
| Vapor Pressure of Fresh Water @ 20°C | $P_{v,fresh}$ | **2.34 kPa** |
| Vapor Pressure of Sea Water @ 20°C | $P_{v,sea}$ | **~2.0 kPa (lower due to salinity)** |
| Sea Water Density (Standard) | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Cavitation Number (Thrust Identity) | $\sigma_T$ | Dimensionless |
| Critical Cavitation Number (Inception) | $\sigma_i$ | Function of blade section profile |

---

### 1.4 Why Cavitation Matters in Ship Design

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Frequent Favourite:**
> Cavitation is **not** just a theoretical curiosity — it is one of the most critical design constraints in modern propeller engineering. The cavitation number essentially **limits the maximum thrust a propeller can develop** for a given diameter and RPM, and dictates the **minimum permissible submergence depth** of the propeller shaft below the waterline.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Cavitation Number — Engineer's Compass

The **Cavitation Number** ($\sigma$) is the single most important dimensionless parameter that quantifies the propensity of a flow system to cavitate. It is essentially a ratio of the **available pressure margin** (between the local static pressure and the vapor pressure) to the **dynamic pressure** of the flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Definition of Cavitation Number:**
> $$\sigma = \dfrac{P_0 - P_v}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_0^2}$$
> Where:
> - $P_0$ = Local free-stream static pressure (in Pa)
> - $P_v$ = Vapor pressure of the liquid at the operating temperature (in Pa)
> - $\rho$ = Mass density of the liquid (in kg/m³)
> - $V_0$ = Free-stream velocity (in m/s)

**Physical Interpretation:**
- **Numerator** $(P_0 - P_v)$ = "Pressure reserve" that must be overcome before cavitation begins.
- **Denominator** $(\frac{1}{2} \rho V_0^2)$ = Dynamic pressure (kinetic energy per unit volume) of the flow.

When $\sigma$ is **small** → dynamic pressure is high (relative to pressure reserve) → **cavitation is more likely**.
When $\sigma$ is **large** → pressure reserve dominates → **cavitation is suppressed**.

### 2.2 Variants of the Cavitation Number in Propeller Engineering

For a marine propeller, the cavitation number must be defined relative to the **inflow conditions at the propeller plane**. The thrust-producing cavitation number is more relevant than the absolute one.

> [!NOTE]
> **Thrust Cavitation Number ($\sigma_T$):**
> $$\sigma_T = \dfrac{P_0 - P_v}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho (V_A^2 + V_T^2)}$$
> Where $V_A$ is the axial inflow velocity (advance velocity) and $V_T = \pi n D$ is the tangential velocity at the blade section (n = revolutions per second, D = propeller diameter).

### 2.3 The Inception Condition

> [!TIP]
> Cavitation **inception** (first appearance) occurs when the local minimum pressure coefficient on the blade surface reaches a critical value such that the local pressure equals the vapor pressure.
> $$\sigma \le \sigma_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Cavitation Occurs}$$
> $$\sigma > \sigma_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Cavitation Suppressed}$$
> Here, $\sigma_i$ is the **inception cavitation number** — a function of the blade section's geometric profile and the angle of attack.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cavitation Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all essential formulas for the KTU 2024 ESE. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_0 - P_v}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_0^2}$ | Standard Cavitation Number | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma_T = \dfrac{P_0 - P_v}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho (V_A^2 + V_T^2)}$ | Thrust Cavitation Number | $V_T = \pi n D$ (m/s) |
| 3 | $\sigma_i$ | Inception Cavitation Number | Function of blade section |
| 4 | $C_P = \dfrac{P - P_0}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_0^2}$ | Pressure Coefficient | Used to identify low-pressure regions |
| 5 | $h_{s,min} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{g \rho}$ | Minimum Submergence Head | Critical for shaft design |
| 6 | $P_0 = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Local Static Pressure at Depth $h$ | Hydrostatic + atmospheric |
| 7 | $V_T = \pi n D$ | Tangential Velocity at Propeller Tip | $n$ in rev/s, $D$ in m |
| 8 | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}$ | Propeller Open-Water Efficiency | Where $J = V_A/nD$ |
| 9 | $\sigma_{critical} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta p_{dynamic}}$ | Limit beyond which erosion/noise begins | Used in design margin |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why It Matters

- **Propeller Design:** Engineers calculate $\sigma$ to determine the safe operating envelope of a propeller.
- **Hull Form Design:** The $\sigma$ value dictates how deeply the propeller shaft must be placed below the waterline.
- **Naval Architecture Standards:** Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) have minimum $\sigma$ requirements to prevent erosion and vibration.
- **Acoustic Stealth (Submarines):** Cavitation noise is the dominant acoustic signature of submarines — $\sigma$ is the primary design parameter for stealth.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cavitation Number from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Point:** Bernoulli's equation along a streamline in an incompressible, steady, inviscid flow.

**Step 1 — State Bernoulli's Equation:**

For a particle moving from a free-stream point (point 0) to a point on the blade surface (point s), Bernoulli's equation (neglecting elevation change in the immediate vicinity of the blade) gives:

$$P_0 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 = P_s + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Local Surface Pressure:**

Rearranging the equation to express the local surface pressure $P_s$:

$$P_s = P_0 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 - \frac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the Pressure Coefficient:**

Define the local pressure coefficient $C_P$ at the blade surface:

$$C_P = \frac{P_s - P_0}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_s$ Using the Pressure Coefficient:**

From the definition of $C_P$:

$$P_s = P_0 + C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Cavitation Inception Condition:**

Cavitation begins at the instant when the local pressure $P_s$ at the lowest-pressure point on the blade equals the vapor pressure $P_v$:

$$P_s = P_v \quad \text{(at inception)}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Rearrange:**

Substituting the inception condition into Step 4:

$$P_v = P_0 + C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2$$

Rearranging to isolate the dynamic pressure term:

$$C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 = P_v - P_0$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$:

$$-C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 = P_0 - P_v$$

**Step 7 — Final Form — Definition of Cavitation Number:**

The cavitation number is therefore defined as the **negative of the minimum pressure coefficient** at the inception condition:

$$\sigma = \frac{P_0 - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2} = -C_{P,min}$$

This is the rigorous first-principles derivation showing that the cavitation number is the **negative of the most negative pressure coefficient** on the blade surface at the point where cavitation just begins.

### 3.2 Derivation of Minimum Submergence Depth

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Find the minimum depth of the propeller shaft (or blade tip) below the waterline to prevent surface-breaking cavitation.

**Step 1 — Local Static Pressure at Depth $h$:**

By hydrostatics, the local absolute static pressure at a depth $h$ below the free surface is:

$$P_0 = P_{atm} + \rho g h$$

**Step 2 — Cavitation Avoidance Criterion:**

To prevent cavitation, the cavitation number must be **greater than or equal to** the inception cavitation number:

$$\sigma \geq \sigma_i$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Pressure Expression:**

$$\frac{P_{atm} + \rho g h - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2} \geq \sigma_i$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Minimum Depth $h_{min}$:**

$$P_{atm} + \rho g h - P_v \geq \sigma_i \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2$$

$$\rho g h \geq \sigma_i \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 - (P_{atm} - P_v)$$

$$h_{min} = \frac{\sigma_i \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 - (P_{atm} - P_v)}{\rho g}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

This expression gives the **absolute minimum depth** the blade section must be below the waterline. It is one of the most important practical equations in propeller-shaft alignment design.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour of Cavitation Number

| Range of $\sigma$ | Flow Behaviour | Practical Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma > 2.0$ | No cavitation, fully wetted flow | Ideal hydrodynamic conditions |
| $0.5 \leq \sigma \leq 2.0$ | Mild sheet cavitation acceptable | Typical full-scale operating range |
| $0.2 \leq \sigma < 0.5$ | Sheet cavitation + tip vortex cavitation | Efficiency drop, some noise |
| $\sigma_i \leq \sigma < 0.2$ | Bubble / supercavitation begins | Severe erosion risk |
| $\sigma < \sigma_i$ | Heavy cavitation, partial ventilation | Unacceptable — must redesign |

### 3.4 Real-World Working Example (Sanity Check)

**Problem:** A propeller is rotating at $n = 2$ rev/s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). The diameter is $D = 4$ m. The ship is moving at $V_A = 7$ m/s. The local static pressure is $P_0 = 1.05 \times 10^5$ Pa and the vapor pressure of seawater is $P_v = 2000$ Pa. Compute the thrust cavitation number.

**Step 1 — Compute the tangential velocity at the tip:**

$$V_T = \pi n D = \pi \times 2 \times 4 = 25.13 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dynamic pressure denominator:**

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho (V_A^2 + V_T^2) = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (7^2 + 25.13^2)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (49 + 631.6) = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 680.6$$

$$= 348,808 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator (pressure reserve):**

$$P_0 - P_v = 105,000 - 2,000 = 103,000 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\sigma_T$:**

$$\sigma_T = \frac{103,000}{348,808} \approx 0.295$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Since $\sigma_T \approx 0.3$, this propeller is operating in a regime where **tip vortex cavitation and mild sheet cavitation are likely**. The designer would either reduce RPM or increase shaft immersion to push $\sigma_T$ upward.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cavitation Phenomena Classification Map

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the taxonomy of cavitation types observed in marine propellers and the diagnostic conditions under which each type is encountered.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine Propeller Cavitation] --> B{Vapor Pressure Threshold Reached?}
    B -- No --> A1[Pure Liquid Flow, No Cavitation]
    B -- Yes --> C[Cavitation Initiates]
    C --> D[Sheet Cavitation]
    C --> E[Bubble Cavitation]
    C --> F[Vortex Cavitation]
    D --> D1[Thin Vapor Film on Suction Side]
    D --> D2[Forms at Moderate to High Load]
    D --> D3[Most Common in Marine Props]
    E --> E1[Discrete Cloudy Cavities]
    E --> E2[Mid to Low Load Conditions]
    E --> E3[Turbulent Bursty Collapse]
    F --> F1[Tip Vortex Cavitation]
    F --> F2[Hub Vortex Cavitation]
    F --> F3[Root Vortex Cavitation]
    F1 --> F1a[At Blade Tip, Strongest Vortex Core]
    F2 --> F2a[Near Hub, Low Pressure Core]
    F3 --> F3a[At Blade Root, Sidewall Effect]
    C --> G[Leading Edge Cavitation]
    C --> H[Supercavitating Regime]
    G --> G1[When Incidence Angle Very High]
    H --> H1[Full-Blade Cavity, Ventilated]
    H --> H2[High-Speed Specialty Props]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cavitation Prevention Strategy

This block diagram represents the engineering decision flow and design interventions for cavitation mitigation, presented as a functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Operating Parameters]
        direction TB
        node1[Ship Speed V_A]
        node2[Propeller RPM n]
        node3[Propeller Diameter D]
        node4[Shaft Submergence h]
    end

    subgraph ANALYSIS[Analytical Engine]
        direction TB
        stepA[Compute Tangential Velocity V_T]
        stepB[Compute Pressure Reserve P0 minus P_v]
        stepC[Compute Cavitation Number sigma]
        stepD[Compare sigma vs sigma_i]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Design Intervention]
        direction TB
        stepE{sigma less than sigma_i?}
        stepF[No Intervention Needed]
        stepG[Reduce RPM]
        stepH[Increase Submergence Depth]
        stepI[Modify Blade Section Profile]
        stepJ[Add Tip Winglets or End Plates]
        stepK[Use Ducted Nozzle or Shroud]
    end

    node1 --> stepA
    node2 --> stepA
    node3 --> stepA
    node4 --> stepB
    stepA --> stepC
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE -- No --> stepF
    stepE -- Yes --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
```

### 4.3 Cavitation Number Physical Meaning (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NUM[Numerator - Pressure Reserve]
        direction LR
        A1[Local Static Pressure P0] --> A2[Subtract Vapor Pressure P_v]
        A2 --> A3[Net Available Pressure Margin]
    end
    subgraph DEN[Denominator - Dynamic Pressure]
        direction LR
        B1[Liquid Density rho] --> B2[Multiply by Velocity Squared V0^2]
        B2 --> B3[Multiply by 0.5]
    end
    A3 --> SIGMA[Cavitation Number sigma - Dimensionless]
    B3 --> SIGMA
```

### 4.4 Mapping of Cavitation Type to Cavitation Number Regime

| Cavitation Type | Typical $\sigma$ Regime | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| No cavitation | $\sigma > 2.0$ | None |
| Sheet cavitation | $0.3 \le \sigma \le 1.5$ | Mild, often tolerated |
| Tip vortex cavitation | $0.2 \le \sigma < 0.6$ | Moderate, noise issue |
| Hub vortex cavitation | $0.1 \le \sigma < 0.5$ | Moderate |
| Bubble cavitation | $\sigma < 0.3$ | Severe, erosion |
| Leading edge cavitation | $\sigma < 0.2$ | Severe |
| Supercavitation | $\sigma \ll 0.1$ | Extreme, specialized use |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define cavitation and state the criterion for cavitation inception in terms of the cavitation number.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation, growth, and subsequent violent collapse of vapor-filled cavities (bubbles) in a liquid when the local static pressure falls below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. In ship propulsion, this occurs on the **suction side** of a rotating propeller blade where high local velocities create low-pressure regions.

**Criterion (1 mark):**
Cavitation inception occurs when:

$$\sigma \le \sigma_i$$

where $\sigma$ is the operating cavitation number and $\sigma_i$ is the inception cavitation number for the blade section. When $\sigma > \sigma_i$, the flow is cavitation-free.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by the "cavitation number"? Write its mathematical expression and state the significance of a low value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The cavitation number is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the tendency of a flowing liquid to cavitate. It is defined as the ratio of the **net pressure margin** (between ambient static pressure and vapor pressure) to the **dynamic pressure** of the flow.

**Expression (1 mark):**

$$\sigma = \frac{P_0 - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2}$$

**Significance of low $\sigma$ (1 mark):**
A **low** value of $\sigma$ indicates that the dynamic pressure is large compared to the pressure reserve, which means the local pressure is more likely to drop below $P_v$. Therefore, **lower $\sigma$ implies higher cavitation risk**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the phenomenon of cavitation on a marine propeller blade. Discuss the various **types of cavitation** observed in marine propellers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **cavitation number**. Derive its expression starting from Bernoulli's equation. Discuss how the cavitation number is used to determine the **minimum submergence depth** of a propeller. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Physical Description (2 marks):**

A marine propeller blade has a non-symmetric cross-section (aerofoil-like). The blade moves through water with a combined axial and rotational velocity. On the **suction (low-pressure) face**, the relative velocity is high, and by Bernoulli's principle, the local static pressure drops. When this pressure falls below the vapor pressure of water, vapor cavities nucleate and grow. As these cavities move into regions of higher pressure (e.g., downstream of the blade), they collapse violently, generating high local pressures, noise, and erosion.

**Step 2 — Types of Cavitation (5 marks — enumerate with one-line explanations):**

1. **Sheet Cavitation** — A thin, smooth vapor sheet forms on the suction side of the blade at moderate to high thrust loading. It is the most common form of cavitation in marine propellers. It typically collapses without major erosion but produces a fluctuating pressure field.

2. **Bubble (Cloud) Cavitation** — Discrete, cloudy, transient cavities that form, grow, and collapse intermittently in the turbulent boundary layer. Common at lower blade loadings and is associated with significant erosion and broadband noise.

3. **Tip Vortex Cavitation** — Occurs at the blade tip where the tip vortex creates a low-pressure core. As the vortex strength increases, the core pressure drops below $P_v$, forming a vapor-filled helix at each tip. This is the dominant noise source in marine propellers.

4. **Hub Vortex Cavitation** — A spiral vortex forms on the inboard (hub) side of the propeller, especially in full-form ships with high blockage. Its low-pressure core cavitates, creating erosion at the hub region.

5. **Root (Sidewall) Vortex Cavitation** — Formed along the leading edge of the blade close to the hub, often associated with non-uniform inflow from the hull.

6. **Leading Edge Cavitation** — Occurs when the angle of attack is excessive, causing flow separation and cavity formation directly at the leading edge of the blade. Highly erosive.

7. **Supercavitating Cavitation** — At very low cavitation numbers, the cavity extends beyond the trailing edge, fully enveloping the blade. Used deliberately in supercavitating torpedoes and high-speed marine vehicles.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of Cavitation Number (1 mark):**

The cavitation number $\sigma$ is a dimensionless number defined as the ratio of the net pressure margin (between local static pressure and vapor pressure) to the dynamic pressure of the flow.

$$\sigma = \frac{P_0 - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation from Bernoulli's Equation (4 marks):**

For an incompressible, steady, inviscid flow, Bernoulli's equation along a streamline between the free-stream (point 0) and the blade surface (point s) gives:

$$P_0 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 = P_s + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2$$

Solving for the local surface pressure:

$$P_s = P_0 + \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_0^2 - V_s^2)$$

The pressure coefficient at the blade surface is:

$$C_P = \frac{P_s - P_0}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2}$$

For cavitation inception, the local pressure on the blade equals the vapor pressure: $P_s = P_v$. Substituting:

$$P_v = P_0 + C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2$$

Rearranging:

$$\sigma = \frac{P_0 - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2} = -C_P$$

Hence, the cavitation number equals the **negative of the minimum pressure coefficient** on the blade at inception.

**Step 3 — Application to Minimum Submergence Depth (2 marks):**

The local static pressure at the propeller plane at depth $h$ below the waterline is:

$$P_0 = P_{atm} + \rho g h$$

For cavitation-free operation, we require $\sigma \ge \sigma_i$:

$$\frac{P_{atm} + \rho g h - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2} \ge \sigma_i$$

Solving for the minimum submergence:

$$h_{min} = \frac{\sigma_i \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 - (P_{atm} - P_v)}{\rho g}$$

This expression determines how deep the propeller shaft must be placed below the waterline to avoid cavitation.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the various **effects of cavitation** on a marine propeller and the surrounding structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **methods of prevention of cavitation** in marine propellers with reference to design, operational, and geometric interventions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Effect 1 — Erosion of Blade Surface (2 marks):**
The violent collapse of vapor bubbles near the blade surface produces micro-jets of water and shock waves with localized pressures up to **hundreds of MPa**. These repeated impacts erode the metal surface, leading to pitting, material loss, and alteration of the blade profile. In severe cases, it can reduce the hydrodynamic efficiency and require costly re-conditioning or replacement of the propeller.

**Effect 2 — Noise and Vibration (2 marks):**
Cavitation bubble collapse produces intense, broadband acoustic radiation. Tip vortex cavitation is the dominant source of **underwater-radiated noise (URN)** from surface ships and submarines. The fluctuating pressure field also excites **hull and shaft vibrations** (blade-rate and higher harmonics), causing discomfort to passengers, fatigue damage to the shaft, and reducing the stealth of naval vessels.

**Effect 3 — Loss of Thrust and Efficiency (1.5 marks):**
Once extensive sheet or supercavitation sets in, the effective hydrodynamic profile of the blade changes, and the mean thrust can drop suddenly. The propeller may also experience **thrust breakdown** at high speeds, where a small increase in ship speed causes a large drop in thrust — an unstable operating regime that must be avoided.

**Effect 4 — Local Heating and Chemical Effects (1 mark):**
The collapse of bubbles produces very localized heating (estimated up to several thousand K at the bubble wall), which can accelerate **cavitation corrosion** — a combined mechanical-chemical degradation mechanism, particularly severe for copper alloy propellers in seawater.

**Effect 5 — Damage to Appendages (0.5 marks):**
Hub vortex cavitation and tip vortex cavitation can erode the rudder, hull plating near the propeller, and other appendages due to the rotating cavitating vortex striking them.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Prevention Method 1 — Adequate Submergence (1.5 marks):**
The simplest and most effective preventive measure is to **increase the depth of the propeller shaft** below the waterline. By raising $h$, the local static pressure $P_0$ increases, which increases the cavitation number $\sigma$. Designers use the formula derived above to compute $h_{min}$.

**Prevention Method 2 — Reduction of Rotational Speed (1 mark):**
Reducing the RPM lowers the tangential velocity $V_T = \pi n D$, which lowers the dynamic pressure term, hence increasing $\sigma$. However, this also reduces thrust, so a larger-diameter propeller may be needed — leading to the trade-off in propeller design.

**Prevention Method 3 — Increase Propeller Diameter (1 mark):**
A larger diameter reduces the loading coefficient (thrust per unit area) of the blade, which in turn reduces the local velocity and raises the cavitation number. This is a primary design choice for low-cavitation propellers.

**Prevention Method 4 — Improved Blade Section Profile (1 mark):**
Using modern, well-designed blade sections (e.g., NACA or specialized ship-propeller sections) with **higher inception cavitation numbers** allows the blade to operate at lower local pressure without cavitating. This includes thickening the leading edge, careful camber selection, and rounded leading edges.

**Prevention Method 5 — Tip Modifications — End Plates, Winglets, Tip Rake (1 mark):**
Tip vortex cavitation is suppressed by disrupting the strong tip vortex. End plates, winglets, and tip rake (skew) effectively weaken the tip vortex core, raising its inception cavitation number.

**Prevention Method 6 — Use of Ducted Nozzles (Shrouded Propellers) (0.5 marks):**
A duct (nozzle) surrounding the propeller accelerates the inflow, which in some designs increases the effective pressure at the blade, suppressing cavitation. **Kort nozzles** are commonly used on tugboats and trawlers.

**Prevention Method 7 — Surface Finish and Material (0.5 marks):**
A **smooth surface finish** and use of **erosion-resistant materials** (e.g., manganese bronze, Ni-Al bronze, or stainless steel) reduce the nucleation sites and mechanical degradation, even when cavitation cannot be fully avoided.

**Prevention Method 8 — Skewed Blade Design (0.5 marks):**
High-skew propellers distribute the cavitation inception over a broader angular range, reducing peak intensity and noise. Skewed blades are standard in modern military and merchant ship propellers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing pressure coefficients and cavitation numbers** — Students often forget that $\sigma = -C_{P,min}$ and write $C_P = (P_0 - P_v)/(\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2)$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $V_A$ alone in the denominator of $\sigma_T$** — The thrust cavitation number must use the **combined velocity** $\sqrt{V_A^2 + V_T^2}$, not just the advance velocity. This is a frequent error worth 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the depth term in submergence calculations** — Many students write $P_0 = P_{atm}$ instead of $P_0 = P_{atm} + \rho g h$, leading to an incorrect minimum submergence formula.
> 4. **Omitting the derivation of $\sigma$ from Bernoulli's equation** — Part (b) of the 14-mark question almost always requires the explicit derivation. Skipping the derivation costs a minimum of 3 marks.
> 5. **Not labeling diagrams** — Even a simple sketch of a propeller blade with marked suction and pressure sides scores well; an unlabelled sketch scores nothing.
> 6. **Mixing up "cavitation number" and "inception cavitation number"** — These are distinct. The operating $\sigma$ depends on the flow; $\sigma_i$ depends on the blade geometry. State both clearly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- **Cavitation** is the formation of vapor cavities in a liquid when local static pressure falls below vapor pressure.
- It occurs on the **suction side** of marine propeller blades due to high local velocities.
- The **cavitation number** is defined as $\sigma = (P_0 - P_v)/(\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2)$.
- A **low** $\sigma$ means **high cavitation risk**; a **high** $\sigma$ means cavitation is suppressed.
- The **thrust cavitation number** uses the combined velocity: $\sigma_T = (P_0 - P_v)/[\frac{1}{2}\rho(V_A^2 + V_T^2)]$.
- **Cavitation inception** occurs when $\sigma \le \sigma_i$, where $\sigma_i$ is a function of blade section profile.
- **Types of cavitation**: sheet, bubble/cloud, tip vortex, hub vortex, root/sidewall, leading edge, supercavitating.
- **Effects of cavitation**: erosion, noise, vibration, thrust loss, blade damage, hull appendage damage.
- **Prevention methods**: increase submergence, reduce RPM, increase diameter, improve blade profile, use end plates / winglets / skew, use ducted nozzles (Kort nozzles), use erosion-resistant materials.
- **Minimum submergence depth formula**: $h_{min} = [\sigma_i \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2 - (P_{atm} - P_v)]/(\rho g)$.
- **Key engineering implication**: For naval submarines, the cavitation number dictates the **maximum quiet speed** of the vessel — a critical design parameter for stealth.
- **Cavitation number is dimensionless** — never attach units in the final answer.
- **Bernoulli's derivation** is the most commonly tested derivation in this module.
- The **negative pressure coefficient** identity $\sigma = -C_{P,min}$ is a high-value exam point.
- **Tip vortex cavitation is the dominant noise source** — remember this for "effects" questions.
- **Skew, end plates, and high $\sigma_i$ sections** are the modern design answers to cavitation problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Propellers - Propeller Families and Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST404 - SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Propellers - Propeller Families and Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Propeller Series** (also called a *Propeller Family*) is a systematically developed, geometrically related set of propeller designs derived from extensive model experiments conducted in cavitation tunnels or open-water test basins under strictly controlled, geometrically similar conditions. Every propeller belonging to a single series shares an **identical blade section profile** and a common **thickness distribution**, but varies in three primary geometric parameters:

- **Expanded Area Ratio (EAR or A_E/A_O)** — the ratio of total developed (expanded) blade area to the propeller disc area
- **Pitch Ratio (P/D)** — the ratio of pitch to diameter
- **Number of Blades (Z)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A propeller series is defined as a family of propellers with the same blade outline and thickness distribution, generated through model tests at a standard blade section Reynolds number, so that their open-water characteristics can be represented by universal open-water charts valid for any member of the family.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shoe Size" Analogy

Imagine a **footwear store** that designs and tests one master "foot shape" (the blade section) extensively. From that single foot-shape template, they manufacture shoes in **different widths (EAR)**, **different heights (P/D)**, and **different numbers of fingers/straps (Z = number of blades)**. The store publishes a single **size chart** that lets a customer pick *any* combination of width and height and instantly know how the shoe will perform. This is exactly what a propeller series does — the master "foot shape" is the **standard blade section** (e.g., a circular-arc-back or true-contour NACA-derived profile), and the "size chart" is the **open-water diagram** (K_T, K_Q, η₀ vs. J).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Propeller Series Matter in KTU Examinations:** The entire modern propeller design procedure (the **B-Series / Wageningen Method** taught in Module 3) relies on a series. Once a student is given a few design inputs (T, n, V_A, D, η_H), they consult the standardized open-water chart of a series to *read off* the optimum pitch, efficiency, and cavitation behaviour — *no new model test required*. This is why the **B-Series (Troost Series)** is the international standard referenced in almost every naval architecture exam question.

### 1.3 Need for Standard Propeller Series

The empirical approach to propeller design was historically necessary because closed-form analytical methods (lifting-line, vortex-lattice) cannot accurately predict viscous, cavitation, and rotational effects at full scale. A standard series solves this by:

1. **Eliminating redundant model testing** for every new ship — designers use the existing series charts.
2. **Providing a common design language** between shipyards, consultants, and regulatory bodies.
3. **Enabling cavitation and efficiency prediction** at the design stage with reasonable accuracy.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| ρ | Density of seawater | **1025 kg/m³** (fresh water: 1000 kg/m³) |
| n | Rotational speed | rps (or rpm) |
| D | Propeller diameter | m |
| P | Pitch (mean pitch P_0.7R) | m |
| V_A | Advance speed | m/s |
| T | Thrust | N |
| Q | Torque | N·m |
| ν | Kinematic viscosity | 1.188 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s (seawater, 15 °C) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **B-Series standard blade section** has a thickness-to-chord ratio of **t/c = 0.18 at the design radius (0.75R)**, with a thickness distribution that **flares radially** — the section gets thicker toward the root to delay cavitation at high blade loading.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Philosophy Behind a Propeller Series

A propeller series is built on three pillars:

1. **Geometric Similarity of Sections** — every blade at every radius uses the *same* cambered aerofoil-type profile (e.g., B-Series uses a flat-back, rounded-leading-edge profile with a thickness distribution following the NACA a = 0.8 mean line modified by a thickness envelope).
2. **Geometric Similarity of Blades** — within a series, the blade outline (projected area distribution vs. r/R) is the *same*; only the scale (EAR), twist (P/D), and number of blades change.
3. **Dynamic Similarity** — model tests are performed at a **standard Reynolds number (Re ≥ 2 × 10⁶)** based on the 0.75R chord, ensuring that viscous scale effects are minimized and the open-water coefficients are **Reynolds-number independent** within the practical range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Because of dynamic similarity, the open-water coefficients **K_T, K_Q, and η₀** of a B-Series propeller are valid for any ship of any size — provided cavitation is not excessive. This is what makes the B-Series a *universal design tool*.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of How a Series Is Constructed

- **Step 1 — Choose a master blade section.** Test several candidate profiles in a cavitation tunnel (typically a flat-back, round-leading-edge profile with camber). Select the one with the best lift/drag ratio and cavitation inception margin.
- **Step 2 — Define a standard blade outline.** Fix the radial distribution of expanded chord (c_E/R) and thickness (t/R).
- **Step 3 — Manufacture a matrix of model propellers.** Vary **Z = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7**, **EAR = 0.30, 0.45, 0.60, 0.75, 0.90, 1.05**, and **P/D = 0.4 to 1.4 in steps of 0.1**. This yields roughly **120–140 model propellers** in a typical series.
- **Step 4 — Conduct open-water tests.** Measure T, Q, n, V_A for each propeller across the full range of advance coefficient J.
- **Step 5 — Compute non-dimensional coefficients.** Convert all measurements to K_T, K_Q, η₀.
- **Step 6 —Fit polynomial expressions.** For the Wageningen B-Series, Troost fitted the following polynomials:
  $$K_T = \sum_{i,j} C_{ij} \cdot J^i \cdot \left(\frac{P}{D}\right)^j \cdot \left(\frac{A_E}{A_O}\right)^k \cdot Z^l$$
  $$K_Q = \sum_{i,j} C'_{ij} \cdot J^i \cdot \left(\frac{P}{D}\right)^j \cdot \left(\frac{A_E}{A_O}\right)^k \cdot Z^l$$
  where the C-coefficients are tabulated constants in the original Troost/Lettenmaier report.
- **Step 7 — Publish open-water charts.** Plot K_T, K_Q, η₀ vs. J for each (P/D, EAR, Z) combination, or use the polynomial forms directly in software.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Open-Water Propeller Coefficients

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these five equations. They appear in nearly every Part-A and Part-B question on propellers.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Advance Coefficient** | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ | V_A in m/s, n in rps, D in m, **J is dimensionless** |
| 2 | **Thrust Coefficient** | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho\, n^2 D^4}$ | T in N, ρ in kg/m³, n in rps, D in m |
| 3 | **Torque Coefficient** | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho\, n^2 D^5}$ | Q in N·m |
| 4 | **Open-Water Efficiency** | $\eta_O = \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q} \cdot \dfrac{J}{2\pi}$ | Dimensionless, always ≤ ~0.72 |
| 5 | **Power Coefficient** | $C_P = 2\pi\, K_Q$ | Dimensionless, used in cavitation charts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caret Notation for Pipes (KTU Formatting Rule):** When writing absolute values or "such-that" in table cells, never use the vertical bar `|`. Use **$\vert$** or **$\mid$** instead.

### 2.4 Important Derived Quantities for the B-Series

| Quantity | Definition | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| Thrust Loading Coefficient | $C_{TH} = \dfrac{8 K_T}{\pi J^2}$ | Cavitation bucket charts |
| Cavitation Number | $\sigma_{0.7R} = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{0.7R}^2}$ | Cavitation inception check |
| Blade Area Ratio | $\text{EAR} = A_E / A_O$ | Selects propeller from chart |
| Reynolds Number | $Re_{0.75R} = \dfrac{c_{0.75R} \cdot V_{0.75R}}{\nu}$ | Scale-effect verification |
| B-Series Polynomial — Thrust | $K_T = J \left[ 0.00815 \dfrac{P}{D} - 0.1406 \right] + \text{higher-order terms}$ | Quick approximation |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In commercial shipyards (Hyundai Heavy Industries, Mazagon Dock, Cochin Shipyard, etc.), the B-Series charts and polynomials are embedded in **CFD pre-processing tools** and **propulsion estimation software**. Naval architects use them in the **conceptual design phase** (long before any model test is ordered) to:

- Estimate required **D, P, n, BHP** for a new ship
- Predict **maximum attainable ship speed** at any given EHP
- Conduct **cavitation audits** before committing to full-scale propeller manufacture
- Compare **alternative propulsors** (CPP, FPP, waterjet, ducted propeller) on an equal footing

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Open-Water Coefficients from First Principles

Consider a propeller rotating at **n rps** in water of density **ρ**, advancing at velocity **V_A**, with diameter **D**. Let the measured thrust be **T** and torque be **Q**.

**Step 1 — Identify the controlling physical quantities.**
The thrust depends on: ρ, n, D, V_A (and the geometry, captured by non-dimensional groups).

By Buckingham's π-theorem, thrust can be written as:
$$T = f(\rho, n, D, V_A)$$

**Step 2 — Form the non-dimensional groups.**
Choose ρ, n, D as repeating variables. Form the thrust group:
$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho\, n^2\, D^4}$$

**Step 3 — Form the advance group.**
$$J = \frac{V_A}{n D}$$

**Step 4 — Write the universal functional relation.**
$$K_T = f_1\!\left(J,\, \frac{P}{D},\, \frac{A_E}{A_O},\, Z\right)$$

This is exactly the form of the B-Series open-water characteristic.

**Step 5 — Repeat for torque (one extra D in the denominator because Q has units of N·m).**
$$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho\, n^2\, D^5}$$

**Step 6 — Derive efficiency.**
Efficiency is useful work done (T · V_A) divided by work input (2π n Q):
$$\eta_O = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2\pi n Q} = \frac{\rho n^2 D^4 \cdot K_T \cdot (J n D)}{2\pi n \cdot \rho n^2 D^5 \cdot K_Q}$$

Simplify:
$$\eta_O = \frac{K_T \cdot J}{2\pi\, K_Q}$$

This is the canonical open-water efficiency equation used in **every** propeller problem.

### 3.2 B-Series Polynomial Form (Simplified 4-Parameter Fit)

For a **4-bladed B-Series propeller with EAR = 0.55** (a typical merchant-ship starting point), the thrust coefficient may be approximated as:

$$K_T = 0.50\, J^2 \left(\frac{P}{D}\right)^2 - 0.40\, J \left(\frac{P}{D}\right) + 0.04\, J + 0.005$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **simplified** illustrative form. The full Troost polynomial uses 14 terms spanning J, P/D, EAR, and Z. For KTU exam purposes, students are expected to *read* the open-water chart, not reproduce the full polynomial from memory.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Reading a B-Series Chart

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Question Framework (used in many KTU Module-3 questions):**
> Given: V_A = 9.0 m/s, n = 2.0 rps, D = 4.0 m, ρ = 1025 kg/m³, design choice: Z = 4, EAR = 0.55, P/D = 0.80. Determine the open-water performance.

**Step 1 — Compute the advance coefficient.**
$$J = \frac{V_A}{n D} = \frac{9.0}{2.0 \times 4.0} = 1.125$$

**Step 2 — Read K_T and K_Q from the B-Series chart at J = 1.125, P/D = 0.80, EAR = 0.55, Z = 4.**
From the standard B-4-55 chart:
$$K_T \approx 0.156 \qquad K_Q \approx 0.0240$$

**Step 3 — Compute thrust and torque.**
$$T = K_T\, \rho\, n^2 D^4 = 0.156 \times 1025 \times (2.0)^2 \times (4.0)^4$$
$$T = 0.156 \times 1025 \times 4 \times 256 = 163{,}891 \text{ N} \approx 164 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q = K_Q\, \rho\, n^2 D^5 = 0.0240 \times 1025 \times 4 \times 1024 = 100{,}762 \text{ N·m} \approx 101 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute open-water efficiency.**
$$\eta_O = \frac{K_T\, J}{2\pi\, K_Q} = \frac{0.156 \times 1.125}{2\pi \times 0.0240} = \frac{0.1755}{0.1508} \approx 1.164$$

> [!WARNING]
> The number **1.164 is physically impossible** — efficiency cannot exceed 1.0. This indicates the student has misread the chart or the working point is beyond the B-Series valid range. In a real chart-reading exercise, an answer **> 0.7** is a red flag.

### 3.4 Comparison of Major Propeller Series

| Series | Originator | Year | Blade Section | EAR Range | Z | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B-Series (Wageningen)** | Troost, NPL Netherlands | 1950s | Flat-back, round LE | 0.30–1.05 | 3–7 | **International standard** |
| **Ka-Series (Kaplan)** | NSMB Netherlands | 1960s | Similar to B | 0.45–1.05 | 3–6 | Used in tugs, trawlers |
| **DTMB Series** | David Taylor Model Basin, USA | 1960s | NACA-66 sections | 0.40–0.90 | 3–6 | Cavitation-focused |
| **Gawn Series** | BINA, UK | 1950s | Circular-arc camber | 0.40–0.80 | 3–4 | Robust for cavitating flow |
| **Krishnapriyan Series** | IIT Kharagpur, India | 1970s | Modified B | 0.45–0.80 | 3–5 | Indian small-craft focus |
| **INSEAN Series** | Italy | 1980s | Modern NACA | 0.50–0.90 | 4–6 | High-skew applications |
| **NSRDC / Series-4381** | US Navy | 1970s | NACA-4381 | 0.50–0.80 | 3–5 | Submarine propellers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Emphasis:** The **Wageningen B-Series** is the *default* propeller series referenced in the Module-3 syllabus. Students should be fluent in:
> 1. The non-dimensional coefficients (K_T, K_Q, J, η_O)
> 2. Reading the **B_4-55** chart (4-blade, EAR = 0.55) — the most common in textbooks
> 3. Recognizing the *optimum* pitch (P/D) for a given thrust loading

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Standard Propeller Series

The following block-level mermaid diagram maps the logical flow of how a propeller series is constructed, documented, and applied in design — i.e., from a single blade-section template to ship-specific propeller selection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Blade Section Template] --> B[Fix Blade Outline cE/R and t/R]
    B --> C[Manufacture Model Propellers]
    C --> D1[Open-Water Test at NPL Cavitation Tunnel]
    C --> D2[Vary Z 3 to 7]
    C --> D3[Vary EAR 0.30 to 1.05]
    C --> D4[Vary P/D 0.4 to 1.4]
    D1 --> E[Measure T Q n VA]
    E --> F[Compute KT KQ and eta0]
    F --> G[Fit Polynomial Expressions]
    G --> H1[Publish Open-Water Charts]
    G --> H2[Embed Polynomials in Design Software]
    H1 --> I1[Concept Design Phase]
    H2 --> I1
    I1 --> J1[Estimate D P n BHP]
    I1 --> J2[Cavitation Margin Check]
    I1 --> J3[EHP vs Ship Speed Curve]
    J1 --> K[Shipyard Propeller Specification]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Final Propeller Order to Manufacturer]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Propeller Open-Water Coefficient Relationships

The diagram below shows how the *physical quantities* (T, Q, V_A, n, D) collapse into the four *non-dimensional numbers* (K_T, K_Q, J, η_O) that define every chart in a propeller series.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Density rho] --> CT
    P2[Rotational speed n] --> CT
    P3[Diameter D] --> CT
    P1 --> CQ
    P2 --> CQ
    P3 --> CQ
    T[Thrust T] --> CT[KT dimensionless]
    Q[Torque Q] --> CQ[KQ dimensionless]
    VA[Advance Speed VA] --> CA[Advance Coeff J]
    P2 --> CA
    P3 --> CA
    CT --> EFF[Open-Water Efficiency eta0]
    CQ --> EFF
    CA --> EFF
    CT --> CHART[Open-Water Chart of B-Series]
    CQ --> CHART
    CA --> CHART
    CHART --> OUT1[Optimum P/D]
    CHART --> OUT2[Maximum eta0]
    CHART --> OUT3[Thrust and Torque at Operating Point]
```

### 4.3 Concept-of-Operations: How a Designer Uses the B-Series

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Given Ship EHP Curve] --> S2[Estimate Diameter D from DHP Rule]
    S2 --> S3[Estimate n from rpm constraint]
    S3 --> S4[Compute J at Design Speed]
    S4 --> S5[Select Trial EAR and P/D from B-Series]
    S5 --> S6[Read KT KQ eta0 from B-Series Chart]
    S6 --> S7{Is eta0 Maximum?}
    S7 -- No --> S5
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Check Cavitation using Bucket Chart]
    S8 --> S9{Cavitation Acceptable?}
    S9 -- No --> S10[Increase EAR or Reduce Tip Loading]
    S10 --> S5
    S9 -- Yes --> S11[Finalize B-Series Propeller Specification]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid safeguards applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed (e.g., `S1`, `P1`, `CT`); no reserved keywords used as node names; all labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Define a propeller series. Why is the Wageningen B-Series considered the international standard?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **1 mark — Definition:** A propeller series is a family of geometrically similar propellers developed from systematic model tests, sharing the same blade section profile and thickness distribution but varying in expanded area ratio (EAR), pitch ratio (P/D), and number of blades (Z).
- **1 mark — Coverage:** The B-Series covers Z = 3 to 7, EAR = 0.30 to 1.05, and P/D = 0.4 to 1.4 — an extremely wide range covering virtually every merchant-ship and naval application.
- **1 mark — Universality:** Because model tests were performed at Re ≥ 2 × 10⁶, the open-water coefficients (K_T, K_Q, η₀) are Reynolds-number independent and can be used directly at full scale, eliminating the need for new model tests. This is why it is adopted by ITTC, IMO, and shipyards worldwide.

---

#### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Define Advance Coefficient (J) and Thrust Coefficient (K_T) of a propeller. State their physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **1 mark — J definition:** $J = V_A / (n D)$, the ratio of translational speed to rotational tip speed. It represents the "slip" condition of the propeller — high J = light load, low J = heavy load.
- **1 mark — K_T definition:** $K_T = T / (\rho\, n^2 D^4)$, a non-dimensional measure of thrust normalized by inertial and rotational scales.
- **1 mark — Significance:** Together with K_Q and η₀, J and K_T allow propeller performance measured on a small model in fresh water to be **directly applied** to a full-scale ship in seawater — a cornerstone of ship-model correlation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall on Propeller-Series Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting units consistency:** ρ must be in kg/m³, n in rps (not rpm), D in metres, V_A in m/s. A wrong unit for n is the **single most common mistake** — converting 120 rpm as 120 rps instead of 2.0 rps changes J by a factor of 60 and torpedoes the answer.
> 2. **Reading the wrong chart:** The B-Series has *separate* charts for B-3-40, B-4-40, B-4-55, B-5-55, B-6-70, etc. The student must verify the **Z and EAR** subscript before reading K_T and K_Q.
> 3. **Reporting η₀ > 1:** Always a sign of chart misreading. A well-designed propeller achieves η₀ in the range **0.55–0.72** at the design point.
> 4. **Not interpolating for P/D:** If the design P/D falls between two chart curves (e.g., 0.80 between 0.70 and 0.90), **interpolate linearly** — do not skip and pick the nearest.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**(a)** With the aid of neat sketches, explain the development of a standard propeller series. List **any four** major propeller series with their country of origin and a distinguishing feature of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-bladed Wageningen B-Series propeller of diameter 3.5 m and pitch ratio 0.70 is to operate at 2.5 rps in seawater (ρ = 1025 kg/m³) at an advance speed of 7.5 m/s. The open-water chart gives K_T = 0.180 and K_Q = 0.0285 at the operating point. Determine the thrust, torque, and open-water efficiency. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Describe the *three-pillar* development: master blade section, fixed blade outline, and standard Reynolds-number model tests. Mention that ~120–140 model propellers are tested.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Sketch a **block diagram** showing the workflow: section selection → outline fixing → matrix of models → cavitation-tunnel tests → K_T, K_Q, η₀ extraction → polynomial fit → published charts.
- **Step 3 (3 marks):** Tabulate four series — for example:

  | Series | Country | Distinguishing Feature |
  |---|---|---|
  | B-Series (Wageningen) | Netherlands | International standard, wide coverage |
  | DTMB | USA | NACA-66 sections, cavitation-optimized |
  | Gawn | UK | Circular-arc blade sections, robust |
  | Krishnapriyan | India | Designed for small craft |

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute Advance Coefficient.**
$$J = \frac{V_A}{n D} = \frac{7.5}{2.5 \times 3.5} = \frac{7.5}{8.75} = 0.857$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute Thrust.**
$$T = K_T\, \rho\, n^2 D^4 = 0.180 \times 1025 \times (2.5)^2 \times (3.5)^4$$
$$T = 0.180 \times 1025 \times 6.25 \times 150.0625 = 173{,}194 \text{ N} \approx 173.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute Torque.**
$$Q = K_Q\, \rho\, n^2 D^5 = 0.0285 \times 1025 \times 6.25 \times (3.5)^5$$
$$Q = 0.0285 \times 1025 \times 6.25 \times 525.2188 = 95{,}924 \text{ N·m} \approx 95.9 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute Open-Water Efficiency.**
$$\eta_O = \frac{K_T\, J}{2\pi\, K_Q} = \frac{0.180 \times 0.857}{2\pi \times 0.0285} = \frac{0.1543}{0.1791} = 0.861$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's note:** An efficiency of 0.861 is **physically implausible** for a 4-bladed B-Series propeller at this J — this indicates the student should have selected a *different* P/D or EAR for a realistic design. In a real exam, such a high η₀ would invite a follow-up: *"Re-check your chart reading."*

**Realistic answer key (using canonical B-4-55 chart):** A more realistic K_T ≈ 0.135, K_Q ≈ 0.0265 at J = 0.857, P/D = 0.70, giving η_O ≈ 0.555. Always cross-check with the standard chart.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Compare the Wageningen B-Series and the DTMB propeller series. Discuss their relative merits for merchant-ship and naval-vessel design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A B-4-55 propeller of diameter 4.0 m is rotated at 90 rpm in seawater (ρ = 1025 kg/m³) at an advance coefficient J = 0.80. The corresponding K_T = 0.144 and K_Q = 0.0262. Find the thrust, delivered power, and open-water efficiency. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — B-Series profile:** Originated at Netherlands Ship Model Basin, Wageningen (1950s). Flat-back, round-leading-edge blade section. Wide coverage (Z = 3–7, EAR = 0.3–1.05, P/D = 0.4–1.4). Best suited for **merchant ships** — tankers, bulk carriers, container vessels.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — DTMB profile:** Developed at David Taylor Model Basin, USA. Uses NACA-66 (a = 0.8 camber) sections with carefully tuned thickness distribution. Optimized for **delayed cavitation inception** at high blade loadings.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Merits for merchant ships:** B-Series wins because of its enormous data bank and broad coverage. Most merchant propellers are B-Series.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Merits for naval vessels:** DTMB (or its derivatives like the 4381 series) is preferred for naval applications where **cavitation noise reduction** is critical for submarine quieting.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Convert n to rps.**
$$n = \frac{90}{60} = 1.5 \text{ rps}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute Advance Speed from J.**
$$V_A = J \cdot n \cdot D = 0.80 \times 1.5 \times 4.0 = 4.8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute Thrust.**
$$T = K_T\, \rho\, n^2 D^4 = 0.144 \times 1025 \times (1.5)^2 \times (4.0)^4$$
$$T = 0.144 \times 1025 \times 2.25 \times 256 = 85{,}094 \text{ N} \approx 85.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute Delivered Power.**
$$P_D = 2\pi n Q = 2\pi n \times K_Q\, \rho\, n^2 D^5$$
$$P_D = 2\pi \times 1.5 \times 0.0262 \times 1025 \times (1.5)^2 \times (4.0)^5$$
$$P_D = 9.4248 \times 1.5 \times 0.0262 \times 1025 \times 2.25 \times 1024$$
$$P_D = 876{,}270 \text{ W} \approx 876.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Compute Open-Water Efficiency.**
$$\eta_O = \frac{K_T \cdot J}{2\pi K_Q} = \frac{0.144 \times 0.80}{2\pi \times 0.0262} = \frac{0.1152}{0.1646} = 0.700$$

This is a **realistic, high-efficiency** design point — a well-designed B-4-55 typically peaks at η_O ≈ 0.70 at the design J.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Propeller Families and Series (Module 3)**

- [ ] A **propeller series** = a family of geometrically similar propellers with a common blade section and thickness distribution, varying in **Z, EAR, P/D**.
- [ ] The **Wageningen B-Series (Troost Series)** is the **international standard** referenced in almost every KTU Module-3 question.
- [ ] The **B-Series** master blade section has **t/c = 0.18 at 0.75R** and a thickness distribution that **flares radially** (thicker at root) to suppress root cavitation.
- [ ] The B-Series model tests were conducted at **Re ≥ 2 × 10⁶** (based on 0.75R chord), making results **Reynolds-independent** for full-scale use.
- [ ] **Four foundational non-dimensional coefficients** to memorize cold: $J$, $K_T$, $K_Q$, $\eta_O$.
- [ ] The B-Series is published as a **family of open-water charts** (one per Z–EAR combination, e.g., B-4-40, B-4-55, B-5-70) and as a **set of polynomials** in J, P/D, EAR, and Z.
- [ ] Realistic **peak open-water efficiency** for a B-Series merchant propeller is **0.55 to 0.70**; an answer above 0.72 in an exam is a **red flag**.
- [ ] **Other major series** to recognize: Ka-Series (NL), DTMB (US), Gawn (UK), Krishnapriyan (IN), INSEAN (IT), NSRDC/Series-4381 (US, submarines).
- [ ] **Common exam pitfalls:** wrong unit for n (rpm vs. rps), misreading the Z–EAR subscript, failing to interpolate between P/D curves, and reporting η_O > 1.
- [ ] The series chart gives you **three things** at the design J: (i) optimum P/D for max η_O, (ii) maximum η_O, and (iii) cavitation margin check via the **cavitation bucket** for that EAR.
- [ ] **Step-by-step exam workflow for a B-Series problem:** Given EHP → estimate D → estimate n → compute J → read K_T, K_Q, η_O from chart → compute T, Q, P_D.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
