> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST404 - SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Open Water Tests, Presentation of Data, Design Charts - Kt-Kq, Bp-δ, τ-J, P-J Charts, Use of Charts in Propeller Design Including Selection of Prime Movers and Optimisation, Propeller Drawing, Design Project Report and Drawing Submission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Open Water Test (OWT) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Open Water Test (OWT):** An experimental procedure conducted on a geometrically accurate scale model of a marine propeller, towed or driven in open water (free from hull interaction effects) at constant rotational speed *n* (rps) and varying speeds of advance *V<sub>a</sub> (m/s), to measure the steady-state thrust *T* (N) and torque *Q* (N·m) developed by the propeller. The non-dimensional coefficients so obtained form the hydrodynamic signature of the propeller series and are used in the design of full-scale propellers.

The test is a model-scale experiment (typically at Froude similarity with Reynolds number independence ensured by turbulence stimulation on the model blades) and is the **single most important experimental input** to the Wageningen B-Series propeller family used universally for ship propeller design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a ceiling fan spinning in still air. If you walk towards the fan (increasing *V<sub>a</sub>), the air pushed backward by the blades produces less push on the fan's mount, and the motor feels less resistance. Now imagine measuring how much push the fan blades give and how hard the motor has to turn it at every walking speed. That is exactly what an Open Water Test does — but with water as the working fluid and a precisely made model propeller in a large towing tank (or a cavitation tunnel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The water must be **"open"** — meaning the propeller is NOT fitted behind a ship hull. The hull's wake and pressure field are deliberately excluded so the result is a *clean* propeller characteristic, which we can later modify by the **wake fraction (w)** and **thrust deduction (t)** to get behind-condition behaviour.

### 1.3 Why OWT Data Is Presented in Non-Dimensional Form

Raw test results come out as *T* versus *V<sub>a</sub>* at constant *n*, and *Q* versus *V<sub>a</sub>*. These depend on the size and speed of the model. To make the data **universally reusable for any full-scale propeller** of the same geometry family, dimensional analysis (Buckingham π theorem, with ρ, n, D as repeating variables) reduces *T* and *Q* into two non-dimensional coefficients:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Sea-Water Density:** **ρ = 1025 kg/m³** (the international standard towing-tank reference value for fresh-water tanks a correction of ≈ 0.998 × 1000 = 998 kg/m³ is used, but KTU naval-architecture problems always take **ρ = 1025 kg/m³** unless otherwise specified).

The two non-dimensional groups are the **Thrust Coefficient (K<sub>T</sub>)** and the **Torque Coefficient (K<sub>Q</sub>)**, plotted against the **Advance Coefficient (J)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Dimensional Reduction — The Three Key Coefficients

Starting from a propeller whose behaviour depends on the variables *T, Q, ρ, n, D, V<sub>a</sub>*, dimensional analysis yields three independent non-dimensional groups:

1. **Thrust Coefficient (K<sub>T</sub>)** — the strength of the propeller's push relative to the dynamic pressure of rotation.
2. **Torque Coefficient (K<sub>Q</sub>)** — the resistance to rotation relative to the same.
3. **Advance Coefficient (J)** — the ratio of translational water speed at the disc to the rotational blade-tip speed.

These three are the **building blocks** of every chart described in this module.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are in **SI units** unless noted. **n** is revolutions per second (rps); **D** is propeller diameter in **metres (m)**; **V<sub>a</sub>** is speed of advance in **m/s**; **T** is in **N**; **Q** is in **N·m**; **P<sub>D</sub>** (delivered power at propeller shaft) is in **Watts**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Examiner Tip |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Advance Coefficient | $J = \dfrac{V_a}{nD}$ | Ratio of translational to tip speed | Often confused with $\lambda$ — they are the same in OWT. |
| 2 | Thrust Coefficient | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho\, n^2 D^4}$ | Non-dimensional thrust | Always use ρ = **1025 kg/m³** unless stated. |
| 3 | Torque Coefficient | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho\, n^2 D^5}$ | Non-dimensional torque | Note D⁵ (extra D vs K<sub>T</sub>). |
| 4 | Open-Water Efficiency (Propeller Efficiency Alone) | $\eta_O = \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}\cdot\dfrac{J}{2\pi}$ | How well the propeller converts shaft power to thrust power | This is **NOT** propulsive efficiency (η<sub>D</sub> = η<sub>O</sub>·η<sub>H</sub>·R/(R+1) etc.) |
| 5 | Power Coefficient | $K_P = 2\pi\, K_Q$ | Useful for P-D charts | K<sub>P</sub> is sometimes denoted C<sub>P</sub>. |
| 6 | Thrust Loading (used in τ-J) | $\tau = \dfrac{K_Q}{J}$ | Non-dimensional torque loading | See τ-J chart discussion below. |
| 7 | Blade-area ratio (geometric input) | $\theta = \dfrac{A_E}{A_O} = \dfrac{A_E}{\pi D^2/4}$ | Expanded-area ratio (EAR) | Symbol is **θ** in this sheet (K-T charts use EAR on the right axis). |
| 8 | Reynolds Number Check | $Re = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_a^2 + (0.75\,\pi n D)^2}\cdot c_{0.75R}}{\nu}$ | Verifies viscous similarity; c<sub>0.75R</sub> is chord at 0.75R | For KTU problems, assume Re-independent if the model was at ITTC 1978 standard. |
| 9 | Cavitation Number | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho n^2 D^2}$ | Cavitation similarity check | Reuse ρ = **1025 kg/m³**. |
| 10 | Delivered Power (from OWT) | $P_D = 2\pi n Q$ | Watts | The OWT measures Q directly. |
| 11 | Thrust Power | $P_T = T\,V_a$ | Watts | Used in computing η<sub>O</sub> directly. |

### 2.3 Kt–Kq Chart (The Wageninen B-Series Chart)

The **K<sub>T</sub>–K<sub>Q</sub> chart** is the workhorse of propeller design. The horizontal axis is **J**. A family of curves is plotted, **one for each value of Pitch Ratio (P/D)**. Each curve has **K<sub>T</sub> on the left vertical axis** and **K<sub>Q</sub> on the right vertical axis** (both usually positive downward — i.e., the chart grows downward, mimicking the wake-fraction contribution).

Reading convention:
- For a chosen P/D curve, as **J increases** (ship speeds up at constant rpm), K<sub>T</sub> and K<sub>Q</sub> **decrease** (the propeller "unloads").
- The slope of K<sub>T</sub> vs J tells you the thrust-derivative (∂T/∂V<sub>a</sub>)<sub>n</sub> used in accelerations and course-stability studies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K<sub>T</sub> = 0 point** on a K<sub>T</sub>–J curve is the *zero-thrust* J — the J at which the propeller produces no useful thrust. For Wageningen B-Series, J<sub>0T</sub> ≈ 0.7 to 1.0 depending on EAR and P/D.

### 2.4 Bp–δ Chart

The Bp–δ chart is a **designer-friendly** remapping of the K<sub>T</sub>–K<sub>Q</sub> chart. Two new variables are introduced:

$$B_P = \dfrac{K_T^{3/2}}{K_Q^{1/2}}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{K_Q}{K_T}\cdot\dfrac{nD}{V_a}\cdot 1000 = \dfrac{K_Q}{K_Q}\cdot\dfrac{1000}{2\pi} = \text{simplified form actually} \;\; \delta = \dfrac{nD}{V_a}\cdot \dfrac{K_Q}{K_T}\cdot 1000$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait — let me write the correct, KTU-board-exam canonical δ definition** (this is a common trap):
>
> $$\delta = \dfrac{nD}{V_a}\cdot \dfrac{K_Q}{K_T}\cdot 1000 = \dfrac{1}{J}\cdot \dfrac{K_Q}{K_T}\cdot 1000$$
>
> Students often forget the **×1000** factor. Without it, δ is dimensionless but inconveniently small; the ×1000 makes it a two- or three-digit number (typically 50–150) and easier to read on a chart.

- **B<sub>P</sub>** is a measure of the propeller's **loading per unit disc area** at design — it groups thrust and torque into a single "size" parameter.
- **δ** measures **rotor speed reduction (rpm reduction at design point)** — i.e., how much the rpm drops between bollard and free-running condition. It is therefore also called the **"rotational speed ratio"** or **"rpm drop coefficient"**.

Use of Bp–δ chart:
- Enter with required thrust and ship speed → compute design **B<sub>P</sub>** and **δ**.
- Locate this point on a grid of (P/D, EAR) lines.
- The intersection gives the propeller's design P/D and EAR.

### 2.5 τ–J Chart (Torque–Loading / Torque Characteristic Chart)

The **τ–J** chart plots a new non-dimensional torque-loading:

$$\tau = \dfrac{K_Q}{J} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho\, n^2 D^5}\cdot\dfrac{nD}{V_a} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho\, n D^4 V_a}$$

This is essentially the ratio of **torque × tip speed** to **thrust × advance speed × (ρ, D, etc.)** — physically, it is a measure of the propeller's torque characteristic per unit slip. It is used in:

- **Engine selection**: the τ–J curve for a candidate propeller must match the **envelope curve** of the prime mover (diesel or gas turbine) drawn on the same τ–J plane.
- **Matching**: the intersection of the propeller curve and the engine envelope gives the operating point.

### 2.6 P–J Chart (Power Characteristic)

Plots **C<sub>P</sub> (or K<sub>P</sub>) = 2π K<sub>Q</sub>** versus J. The P–J curve is essentially a power-demand curve for the propeller as a function of advance coefficient at a given P/D. Reading:

- For a fixed P/D curve, **C<sub>P</sub> falls** with rising J (less torque needed at higher J).
- The **slope dC<sub>P</sub>/dJ** is the partial-differential input to **propulsion-engine dynamic response** (engine-governor design).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU trap:** Some textbooks plot C<sub>P</sub> and some plot K<sub>P</sub>; the conversion is **C<sub>P</sub> = 2π K<sub>P</sub> = 2π K<sub>Q</sub>**. A factor of 2π error is a **full-marks-losing** error.

### 2.7 Use of These Charts in Propeller Design

The standard design procedure (which KTU 14-mark problems expect) is:

**Step 1 — Specify the resistance and wake:**
- Effective power P<sub>E</sub> = R<sub>T</sub> · V<sub>S</sub>
- Thrust required: T = R<sub>T</sub> / (1 − t)
- Speed of advance: V<sub>a</sub> = V<sub>S</sub> (1 − w)

**Step 2 — Pick a candidate P/D and EAR from Bp–δ chart** using the design B<sub>P</sub> and δ.

**Step 3 — Use the K<sub>T</sub>–K<sub>Q</sub> chart** to back-calculate n (rpm) and P<sub>D</sub>:

$$n = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\rho D^4 K_T}}, \quad P_D = 2\pi n Q = 2\pi \rho n^3 D^5 K_Q$$

**Step 4 — Use the τ–J chart** to select a prime mover whose envelope (in τ–J space) contains the design point.

**Step 5 — Use the P–J chart** for engine-propeller matching and to estimate fuel consumption (via specific fuel consumption × P<sub>D</sub>).

### 2.8 Selection of Prime Movers

Prime-mover selection criteria:
1. **MCR (Maximum Continuous Rating)** must equal or slightly exceed the design **P<sub>D</sub>** (with a service margin of 10–15 % for naval, 5–10 % for merchant).
2. **RPM of the prime mover at MCR** must be reducible by the reduction gear to the propeller's design n.
3. **Envelope shape**: the engine's τ–J envelope must encompass the propeller's operating range from light ship to ballast.
4. **Fuel type & emissions** (modern: IMO Tier III).
5. **Cavitation margin**: ensure the B-Series minimum-pressure criterion σ ≥ σ<sub>cav</sub> at the design point.

### 2.9 Optimisation

Optimisation seeks the **P/D and EAR** that give:
- **Maximum η<sub>O</sub>** at the design point, OR
- **Minimum P<sub>D</sub>** for the given T and V<sub>a</sub> (fuel-optimum), OR
- **Minimum cavitation risk** (silent-ship optimisation).

The Bp–δ chart is central: by overlaying lines of constant η<sub>O</sub> on the B<sub>P</sub>–δ grid, the optimal (P/D, EAR) is read directly as the tangent point of the highest-η<sub>O</sub> line to the design B<sub>P</sub>–δ point.

> [!TIP]
> **Lerbs' criterion** (for lightly loaded propellers) and **van Manen's criterion** (for highly loaded, e.g., escort or naval propellers) are advanced alternatives that KTU students should *name* in answers but not derive in full.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Thrust, Torque, and Advance Coefficients (Dimensional Analysis)

**Step 1 — List the variables.** The thrust *T* of a propeller in open water is assumed to depend on:

$$T = f(\rho, V_a, n, D, \mu, g)$$

For a model where viscous and gravity effects are matched (or the model is at sufficient Re), we drop μ and g:

$$T = f(\rho, V_a, n, D)$$

**Step 2 — Count variables and fundamental dimensions.** We have **n = 4** variables (T, ρ, V<sub>a</sub>, n, D) and **k = 3** fundamental dimensions (M, L, T). By Buckingham's π-theorem, the number of π-groups is:

$$P = n - k = 5 - 3 = 2$$

**Step 3 — Choose repeating variables.** Take **ρ, n, D** (cannot themselves form a π-group).

**Step 4 — Form the first π-group with T:**

$$\pi_1 = T \cdot \rho^a \cdot n^b \cdot D^c$$

Dimensions:
- [T] = M L T⁻²
- [ρ] = M L⁻³
- [n] = T⁻¹
- [D] = L

$$M L T^{-2} \cdot (M L^{-3})^a \cdot (T^{-1})^b \cdot L^c = M^{1+a}\, L^{1-3a+c}\, T^{-2-b}$$

Set exponents to zero:
- M: 1 + a = 0 ⟹ a = −1
- T: −2 − b = 0 ⟹ b = −2
- L: 1 − 3a + c = 0 ⟹ 1 − 3(−1) + c = 0 ⟹ 1 + 3 + c = 0 ⟹ c = −4

Therefore:

$$\pi_1 = T \cdot \rho^{-1}\cdot n^{-2}\cdot D^{-4} = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} \equiv K_T$$

**Step 5 — Form the second π-group with V<sub>a</sub>:**

$$\pi_2 = V_a \cdot \rho^a \cdot n^b \cdot D^c$$

Dimensions: [V<sub>a</sub>] = L T⁻¹
$$L T^{-1} \cdot (M L^{-3})^a \cdot (T^{-1})^b \cdot L^c = M^a\, L^{1-3a+c}\, T^{-1-b}$$

- M: a = 0
- T: −1 − b = 0 ⟹ b = −1
- L: 1 − 0 + c = 0 ⟹ c = −1

Therefore:

$$\pi_2 = \dfrac{V_a}{nD} \equiv J$$

**Step 6 — Torque.** By identical steps applied to Q (dimensions M L² T⁻²):

$$K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$$

This completes the formal Buckingham derivation. **QED.**

### 3.2 Derivation of Open-Water Efficiency

Open-water (propeller-alone) efficiency η<sub>O</sub> is the ratio of useful thrust power to absorbed (delivered) shaft power:

$$\eta_O = \dfrac{P_T}{P_D} = \dfrac{T V_a}{2\pi n Q}$$

Substitute the non-dimensional coefficients:

$$\eta_O = \dfrac{K_T (\rho n^2 D^4)\cdot V_a}{2\pi n \cdot K_Q (\rho n^2 D^5)}$$

$$\eta_O = \dfrac{K_T \rho n^2 D^4 V_a}{2\pi K_Q \rho n^3 D^5}$$

$$\eta_O = \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}\cdot\dfrac{V_a}{2\pi n D}$$

But J = V<sub>a</sub>/(nD), so:

$$\boxed{\eta_O = \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}\cdot\dfrac{J}{2\pi}}$$

**QED.** This is the canonical form KTU examiners expect on sight.

### 3.3 Derivation of B<sub>P</sub>

The Bp–δ chart is constructed by combining K<sub>T</sub> and K<sub>Q</sub> into a parameter that is **proportional to thrust per disc area**. Define:

$$B_P = \dfrac{K_T^{3/2}}{K_Q^{1/2}}$$

Its physical meaning: recall that thrust loading is C<sub>T</sub> = T/(0.5 ρ A<sub>O</sub> V<sub>a</sub>²). Substituting the K<sub>T</sub>–J form and manipulating yields that B<sub>P</sub> is **proportional to the thrust loading coefficient divided by J to some power**, so each design point has a unique B<sub>P</sub> for a given P/D and EAR.

### 3.4 Derivation of δ

$$\delta = \dfrac{nD}{V_a}\cdot \dfrac{K_Q}{K_T}\cdot 1000 = \dfrac{1}{J}\cdot\dfrac{K_Q}{K_T}\cdot 1000$$

Substituting J = V<sub>a</sub>/(nD) and the η<sub>O</sub> expression:

$$\dfrac{K_Q}{K_T} = \dfrac{J}{2\pi \eta_O}$$

Therefore:

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{J}\cdot \dfrac{J}{2\pi \eta_O}\cdot 1000 = \dfrac{1000}{2\pi \eta_O} \approx \dfrac{159.15}{\eta_O}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beautiful result:** δ depends ONLY on open-water efficiency. A high-η<sub>O</sub> propeller has a **low** δ. This is the deep insight behind the Bp–δ chart's utility: δ is essentially an inverse-efficiency meter.

### 3.5 The Bp–δ Chart in Numerical Use — Worked Analytical Example

**Problem:** A propeller has K<sub>T</sub> = 0.20 and K<sub>Q</sub> = 0.030 at its design J = 0.70. Find (a) η<sub>O</sub>, (b) B<sub>P</sub>, (c) δ.

**(a) Open-water efficiency:**

$$\eta_O = \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}\cdot\dfrac{J}{2\pi} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.030}\cdot\dfrac{0.70}{2\pi} = 6.667 \times 0.1114 = 0.7428 \;(\approx 74.3\%)$$

**(b) B<sub>P</sub>:**

$$B_P = \dfrac{K_T^{3/2}}{K_Q^{1/2}} = \dfrac{0.20^{1.5}}{0.030^{0.5}} = \dfrac{0.08944}{0.17321} = 0.5164$$

**(c) δ:**

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{J}\cdot\dfrac{K_Q}{K_T}\cdot 1000 = \dfrac{1}{0.70}\cdot\dfrac{0.030}{0.20}\cdot 1000 = 1.4286 \times 0.15 \times 1000 = 214.3$$

Equivalently, using η<sub>O</sub> = 0.7428:

$$\delta = \dfrac{1000}{2\pi \eta_O} = \dfrac{159.155}{0.7428} = 214.2 \;(\text{✓ consistent})$$

### 3.6 Derivation of the τ–J Relationship for Engine Matching

For a prime mover, the engine's **torque envelope** at MCR (Maximum Continuous Rating) is a near-constant torque: Q<sub>E</sub> ≈ P<sub>MCR</sub>/(2π n<sub>E</sub>). The **propeller torque** at the same n is Q<sub>P</sub> = K<sub>Q</sub> ρ n² D⁵. The **torque-loading τ** is defined as:

$$\tau = \dfrac{Q_P \cdot n}{0.5 \rho A_O V_a^3} = \dfrac{K_Q \rho n^2 D^5 \cdot n}{0.5 \rho (\pi D^2/4) V_a^3} = \dfrac{K_Q}{J} \cdot \dfrac{4 n^2 D^3}{\pi V_a^2}\cdot 2$$

A simpler KTU-board form is:

$$\tau \equiv \dfrac{K_Q}{J}$$

This is plotted versus J for a candidate P/D; the engine's τ envelope (constant or weakly varying) is overlaid; the **intersection** is the design operating point.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Chart

| Chart | Independent Axis | Dependent Axes | Primary Use | KTU Exam Frequency |
|-------|------------------|----------------|-------------|--------------------|
| K<sub>T</sub>–K<sub>Q</sub> | J | K<sub>T</sub>, K<sub>Q</sub> (one curve per P/D) | Back-calculate n, Q, P<sub>D</sub> | **Very High** |
| B<sub>P</sub>–δ | B<sub>P</sub> (or δ) | δ (or B<sub>P</sub>), with grid of (P/D, EAR) | Initial P/D, EAR selection | **Very High** |
| τ–J | J | τ, with grid of P/D | Engine-propeller matching | **Medium** |
| P–J | J | C<sub>P</sub> or K<sub>P</sub> | Power-demand curves, fuel use | **Medium** |
| η<sub>O</sub>–J | J | η<sub>O</sub> (often implicit) | Optimisation | **High** |
| σ–J | J | σ | Cavitation margin check | **Medium** |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Open Water Test Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Towing Carriage / Drive Motor] --> B[Force Balance]
    B --> C[Propeller Model on Shaft]
    C --> D[Open-Water Basin / Cavitation Tunnel]
    B --> E[T and Q Transducers]
    E --> F[Data Acquisition System]
    F --> G[Computer for Kt and Kq Calculation]
    G --> H[Output: Kt-J and Kq-J curves for fixed P/D]
    H --> I[Master Kt-Kq Chart, Bp-delta Chart]
    I --> J[Propeller Design]
    J --> K[Selection of Prime Mover via tau-J]
    K --> L[Optimisation and Final Drawing]
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Chart-Based Propeller Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Resistance RT and Speed VS] --> B[Compute Thrust T = RT / 1-t]
    A --> C[Compute Advance Speed Va = VS 1-w]
    B --> D[Compute Bp using T, rho, D, Va]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Enter Bp-delta Chart]
    E --> F[Read off candidate P/D and EAR]
    F --> G[Enter Kt-Kq Chart at design J]
    G --> H[Read Kt and Kq for chosen P/D]
    H --> I[Compute n = sqrt T over rho D4 Kt]
    H --> J[Compute PD = 2 pi rho n3 D5 Kq]
    I --> K[Enter tau-J Chart]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Overlay Prime Mover Envelope]
    L --> M[Engine Selection Completed]
    M --> N[P-J Chart for Power and Fuel Verification]
    N --> O[Optimisation: Adjust P/D and EAR for Max etaO]
    O --> P[Final Propeller Drawing and Report]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Prime-Mover Selection via τ–J Matching

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PropellerCharacteristics
        P1[Propeller tau-J Curve at chosen P/D and EAR]
    end
    subgraph PrimeMoverEnvelope
        E1[Engine MCR Torque Envelope in tau-J space]
    end
    P1 --> M{Match Point: tau-propeller equals tau-engine}
    E1 --> M
    M --> Q1[Operating n and Va are fixed]
    Q1 --> R1[Service Margin Check: 10 to 15 percent]
    R1 --> S1[Final Engine Propeller Pair Locked]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Optimisation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Guess P/D and EAR] --> B[Read Kt, Kq, etaO from Chart]
    B --> C{etaO is maximum?}
    C -- No --> D[Vary P/D by small delta]
    D --> E[Vary EAR by small delta]
    E --> B
    C -- Yes --> F[Check Cavitation sigma and Slip]
    F --> G{Cavitation OK?}
    G -- No --> H[Increase EAR or reduce thrust loading]
    H --> B
    G -- Yes --> I[Optimum Design Locked]
    I --> J[Propeller Drawing: Blade Outline, Section Profiles]
    J --> K[Design Project Report Assembly]
```
