topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST404 - SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electromagnetic Propulsion etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Electromagnetic Propulsion (EMP)

**Electromagnetic Propulsion (EMP)** refers to a class of advanced marine propulsion systems in which the thrust necessary to drive a vessel through water is generated directly by the interaction of **electric current**, **magnetic fields**, and the **conductive seawater** itself, without the use of conventional rotating machinery such as propellers, shafts, or gears.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Preferred Wording):**
> *Electromagnetic propulsion is a non-mechanical thrust-generating method that exploits the Lorentz force ($\vec{F} = \vec{J} \times \vec{B}$) developed when an electric current is passed through a conducting fluid (seawater) that is simultaneously exposed to a transverse magnetic field. The reactive force propels the vessel forward in accordance with Newton's Third Law of Motion.*

The "etc." in the syllabus topic extends the scope to include ancillary electromagnetic ship systems such as:
1. **Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) Thrusters**
2. **Superconducting Electromagnetic Propulsion**
3. **Electromagnetic Aircraft Launch Systems (EMALS)**
4. **Linear Induction Motors (LIM) for marine vessels**
5. **Railgun and Coilgun Technology (Naval Weapons Application)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Maglev Train in Water" Analogy**
> Imagine a high-speed maglev train that floats above its track using magnets. Now, instead of air, place the same principle inside a tube of **saltwater (a conductor)**. Pass a strong electric current through the water and switch on a powerful magnet. The water itself behaves like the "train" — it is pushed backward by the magnetic force, and the tube (ship) reacts by being pushed **forward**. There are **no blades, no shafts, no gears** — just physics!

A second analogy: **The Electric Motor Inside-Out.** In a normal motor, the coils (rotor) move and the housing (stator) is fixed. In MHD propulsion, **seawater plays the role of the moving conductor**, while the ship's hull acts as the stationary "stator." Force is transferred directly to the hull via the magnetic field.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Magnetic flux density (Earth) | $B_{earth}$ | $\approx 5 \times 10^{-5}$ **T** |
| Seawater conductivity | $\sigma$ | $\approx 4$ to **5 S/m** |
| Magnetic permeability (free space) | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** |
| Lorentz force constant | $q$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** |
| MHD thruster typical $B$ | $B$ | **2 to 10 Tesla** (superconducting) |
| Hall parameter (seawater) | $\beta$ | typically $< 0.1$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physical Principle — The Lorentz Force

The bedrock equation of all electromagnetic propulsion is the **Lorentz force law**. For a single moving charge:

$$\vec{F} = q \left( \vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B} \right)$$

Where:
- $q$ = electric charge (**C**)
- $\vec{E}$ = electric field intensity (**V/m**)
- $\vec{v}$ = velocity of the charge (**m/s**)
- $\vec{B}$ = magnetic flux density (**T**)

For a current-carrying conductor of length $L$ immersed in magnetic field $B$:

$$\vec{F} = I \vec{L} \times \vec{B}$$

For a distributed current density $\vec{J}$ in a fluid (seawater), the **volume force density** is:

$$\vec{f} = \vec{J} \times \vec{B} \quad \text{(N/m}^3\text{)}$$

## 2.2 Operating Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

The MHD thruster follows a strict 4-step physical sequence:

1. **Step 1 — Seawater as Conductor:** Seawater contains dissolved salts (primarily **NaCl**, ~3.5% by mass), making it a moderately good electrical conductor ($\sigma \approx 4$–$5$ S/m).
2. **Step 2 — Electrode Excitation:** A pair of electrodes (anode & cathode) mounted on opposite walls of an internal channel force a DC current $I$ to flow **transversely** through the channel.
3. **Step 3 — Magnetic Field Activation:** Superconducting or permanent magnets mounted on the other two walls establish a magnetic field $B$ **perpendicular** to the current direction.
4. **Step 4 — Lorentz Force Generation:** The cross product $\vec{J} \times \vec{B}$ produces a force along the **axis of the channel**, pushing water aft → reactive thrust pushes the ship **forward** (Newton's 3rd Law).

## 2.3 Governing Equations of MHD Propulsion

### (a) Thrust Generated
The total electromagnetic thrust produced by an MHD channel of cross-sectional area $A_c$ and length $L_c$ carrying current density $J$ across magnetic field $B$:

$$T = F = J \cdot A_c \cdot B \cdot L_c = \sigma E \cdot A_c \cdot B \cdot L_c$$

Where $\sigma$ is the electrical conductivity of seawater, and $E$ is the applied electric field.

### (b) Power Balance — Useful Mechanical Power
The useful propulsive power is:

$$P_{useful} = T \cdot v = F \cdot v$$

### (c) Electrical Input Power
$$P_{electrical} = V \cdot I = E \cdot L_c \cdot J \cdot A_c$$

### (d) Propulsive Efficiency
$$\eta_{propulsive} = \frac{P_{useful}}{P_{electrical} + P_{magnetic}} = \frac{T \cdot v}{V I + \text{Magnet losses}}$$

### (e) Faraday's Law Applied to the MHD Channel
The induced back-EMF as the ship moves through Earth's field (or as fluid moves relative to magnets):

$$\varepsilon_{induced} = B \cdot v \cdot L_c$$

This is **subtracted** from the applied voltage, reducing net effective thrust at high speeds — known as the **"Velocity Saturation Effect."**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All quantities below are the most-likely formulas to appear in KTU End Semester Examinations for PBNST404.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lorentz force on charge | $F = qvB\sin\theta$ | **N** | Fundamental principle |
| 2 | Force on current conductor | $F = BIL\sin\theta$ | **N** | MHD channel design |
| 3 | Volume force density | $\vec{f} = \vec{J} \times \vec{B}$ | **N/m³** | Distributed thrust |
| 4 | MHD Thrust | $T = \sigma E B A_c L_c$ | **N** | Direct calculation |
| 5 | MHD Power (electrical) | $P_e = E^2 \sigma A_c L_c$ | **W** | Input power |
| 6 | Induced back-EMF | $\varepsilon = B v L_c$ | **V** | Velocity limit |
| 7 | Hall parameter | $\beta = \omega_e \tau_e$ | dimensionless | Plasma correction |
| 8 | Magnetic Reynolds No. | $R_m = \mu_0 \sigma v L$ | dimensionless | Flow regime |
| 9 | Interaction parameter | $N = \frac{\sigma B^2 L_c}{\rho v}$ | dimensionless | Force dominance |
| 10 | Hartmann number | $Ha = B L_c \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\mu}}$ | dimensionless | Boundary layer |

> **Critical Reminder:** In the KTU exam, students frequently lose marks by mixing up **$B$ (Tesla) vs $\mu_0 H$ (A/m)**. Always write the unit of $B$ as **Tesla (T) = Wb/m² = kg/(A·s²)**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Japanese MS-Yamato-1 (1992):** World's first ship to use superconducting MHD propulsion. Displacement ~280 tonnes, achieved speed of ~**8 knots** using 6 superconducting magnets.
> - **US Navy DDG-1000 Zumwalt & Ford-class carriers:** Use **EMALS (Electromagnetic Aircraft Launch System)** replacing steam catapults.
> - **Future Submarines:** EMP allows **acoustic stealth** — no propeller cavitation noise, no shaft seals, no engine vibration.
> - **Naval Weapons:** Railguns (e.g., US Navy's prototype) use the same Lorentz principle but for projectile launch at **hypersonic speeds > Mach 7**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: MHD Thrust from First Principles

**Given:** A rectangular MHD channel of length $L_c$ (in direction of motion), height $h$ (between electrodes), and width $w$ (between magnets).

**Step 1 — Geometry and Current Density**

The electrode pair is on the top and bottom walls. Current density $J$ flows in the vertical ($y$) direction:

$$J = \sigma E_y = \frac{\sigma V}{h}$$

**Step 2 — Magnetic Field Orientation**

Superconducting magnets are on port and starboard walls. The magnetic flux density is in the $z$-direction (horizontal, perpendicular to current):

$$\vec{B} = B \hat{k}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Force Density**

Using the volume force density equation:

$$\vec{f} = \vec{J} \times \vec{B} = (J \hat{j}) \times (B \hat{k}) = JB (\hat{j} \times \hat{k}) = JB \hat{i}$$

So the force density points in the **$x$-direction** (along the ship), as required.

**Step 4 — Integrate Over Channel Volume**

The total thrust on the fluid in the channel is the volume integral of $f$ over the channel volume $V_{ch} = h \cdot w \cdot L_c$:

$$T = \iiint_{V_{ch}} f \, dV = JB \cdot V_{ch} = JB \cdot h \cdot w \cdot L_c$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $J$ from Step 1**

$$T = \left( \frac{\sigma V}{h} \right) \cdot B \cdot h \cdot w \cdot L_c$$

$$\boxed{T = \sigma V B w L_c}$$

This is the **master equation for MHD thrust**. Note that the channel height $h$ cancels out — meaning the designer can choose $h$ freely to optimize pressure drop.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity-Limited Performance Curve

**Step 1 — Apply Faraday's Law**

As the ship (and the channel) moves with velocity $v$ through its **own** magnetic field, the seawater (now moving aft at relative velocity $u_{rel}$) experiences a motional EMF:

$$\varepsilon_{back} = B \cdot u_{rel} \cdot L_c$$

**Step 2 — Net Effective Voltage Across Electrodes**

$$V_{eff} = V_{applied} - \varepsilon_{back} = V - B L_c u_{rel}$$

**Step 3 — Net Current Density**

$$J_{net} = \sigma \cdot \frac{V_{eff}}{h} = \frac{\sigma (V - B L_c u_{rel})}{h}$$

**Step 4 — Net Thrust (Velocity-Dependent)**

$$T(v) = J_{net} \cdot B \cdot h \cdot w \cdot L_c = \sigma (V - B L_c u_{rel}) \cdot B \cdot w \cdot L_c$$

$$\boxed{T(v) = \sigma B w L_c (V - B L_c u_{rel})}$$

**Step 5 — Stall Condition (Zero Velocity)**

At $u_{rel} = 0$:

$$T_{max} = \sigma B w L_c V = \text{(full electromagnetic thrust)}$$

**Step 6 — No-Load Velocity (Zero Thrust)**

Set $T = 0$:

$$V = B L_c u_{rel, max} \implies u_{rel, max} = \frac{V}{B L_c}$$

This represents the **theoretical maximum exhaust velocity** of the MHD system — analogous to the jet velocity in a rocket.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Propulsive Efficiency

**Step 1 — Useful Propulsive Power**

$$P_{useful} = T \cdot v_{ship}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical Input Power**

$$P_{elec} = V \cdot I = V \cdot J \cdot h \cdot w = V \cdot \frac{\sigma V_{eff}}{h} \cdot h \cdot w = \sigma w V V_{eff}$$

**Step 3 — Define Velocity Ratio**

Let $k = \frac{v_{ship}}{u_{rel, max}} = \frac{B L_c v_{ship}}{V}$.

**Step 4 — Express Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{T v_{ship}}{P_{elec}} = \frac{\sigma B w L_c (V - B L_c v) \cdot v}{\sigma w V (V - B L_c v)} = \frac{B L_c v}{V}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{propulsive} = k = \frac{B L_c v_{ship}}{V}}$$

**Critical Insight:** The efficiency equals the **velocity ratio** $k$, and approaches 100% only as $v_{ship} \to u_{rel,max}$. This is identical in form to the propulsive efficiency of a propeller/jet — an important KTU conceptual link.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Parameter | MHD (Conventional) | Superconducting MHD | Conventional Propeller |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thrust Density** | Low | High | High |
| **Acoustic Signature** | Near-zero | Near-zero | High (cavitation) |
| **Moving Parts** | None | Cryocooler only | Many |
| **Max Practical Speed** | ~10 knots | ~15 knots | 30+ knots |
| **Efficiency** | 30–50% | 60–80% | 70–85% |
| **Power Source** | Nuclear (required) | Nuclear (required) | Any |
| **Cost** | Very high | Extremely high | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MHD Thruster Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Hull / Pressure Hull] --> B[MHD Channel Assembly]
    B --> C1[Electrode Pair - Anode Top]
    B --> C2[Electrode Pair - Cathode Bottom]
    B --> D1[Superconducting Magnet - Port Side]
    B --> D2[Superconducting Magnet - Starboard Side]
    C1 --> E[DC Current Source via Power Conditioning Unit]
    C2 --> E
    D1 --> F[Cryogenic Helium Cooling Loop at 4 Kelvin]
    D2 --> F
    E --> G[Ship Nuclear Reactor or Gas Turbine Generator]
    F --> G
    B --> H[Seawater Intake Forward]
    H --> I[Acceleration via Lorentz Force J cross B]
    I --> J[Seawater Jet Expelled Aft]
    J --> K[Reactive Thrust Pushes Ship Forward]
    G --> L[EMALS - Aircraft Catapult System if Carrier]
    L --> M[Linear Motor Acceleration of Aircraft]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Force & Flow Vector Orientation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Channel
        direction LR
        X[Current J direction: top to bottom - Y axis] --> Y[Magnetic Field B: starboard to port - Z axis]
        Y --> Z[Resultant Lorentz Force: aft direction - X axis]
    end
    Z --> W[Water Exits Aft at velocity u]
    W --> V[Ship Moves Forward at velocity v - Newton's Third Law]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — EMALS Architecture (Aircraft Launch)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steam Catapult Legacy System] -.Replaced by.-> B[EMALS - Linear Induction Motor]
    B --> C[Energy Storage Module - Flywheel or Capacitor Bank]
    C --> D[Power Conversion - AC to DC to Variable Frequency]
    D --> E[Linear Motor Stator along Track 100m]
    E --> F[Shuttle with Aircraft attached]
    F --> G[Accelerates Aircraft to 78 m/s in 2 seconds]
    G --> H[Launch Speed 160 knots approx]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Superconducting MHD Subsystem (Yamato-1 Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Propulsion MHD Channel - 6 modules in parallel] --> B[Superconducting Magnet Banks]
    B --> C[Liquid Helium Cryostat - 4.2 K]
    C --> D[Cryocooler - Gifford McMahon type]
    A --> E[Electrode Power Feed - 2000 A typical]
    E --> F[Thyristor Rectifier from AC Bus]
    F --> G[Ship Service Generator or Reactor]
    B --> H[Magnetic Shielding - BIO Shield for Crew]
    A --> I[Seawater Flow Straighteners]
    I --> J[Acoustic Anomaly Detector]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]:**
> Define the term **Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) propulsion**. State two advantages and one limitation of MHD propulsion as applied to marine vessels.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Board Key):**
MHD propulsion is a method of generating thrust by passing an electric current through a conducting fluid (seawater) in the presence of a magnetic field. The interaction produces a Lorentz force on the fluid; the reactive force on the channel pushes the ship forward.
- **Advantage 1:** No moving mechanical parts in the thrust-generating region → extremely low acoustic signature (stealth).
- **Advantage 2:** No propeller cavitation → higher achievable efficiency in some regimes.
- **Limitation:** Requires very high magnetic fields (superconducting magnets) and large electrical power — practically feasible only with **nuclear propulsion**.

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]:**
> Write the expression for **Lorentz force per unit volume** in an MHD thruster and identify each variable. What is the **SI unit** of the result?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
$$\vec{f} = \vec{J} \times \vec{B}$$
Where $\vec{J}$ = current density (**A/m²**) and $\vec{B}$ = magnetic flux density (**T = Wb/m²**). The SI unit of $\vec{f}$ is **N/m³** (Newton per cubic metre — it is a *volume* force density).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> In 14-mark questions, students **commonly lose 3–4 marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting to **draw/describe a labelled diagram** of the MHD channel.
> 2. Mixing up **$B$ (Tesla)** with **$H$ (A/m)** when stating units.
> 3. **Omitting the velocity-saturation effect** in efficiency derivations.
> 4. Writing $\vec{F} = BIL$ without specifying the **right-hand rule** or vector cross product orientation.
> Always include a sketch, vector directions, units, and a concluding statement.

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

> **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) ship propulsion system**. Clearly indicate the directions of current, magnetic field, and resulting force. Discuss the role of **seawater conductivity** and **magnetic field strength** in determining thrust. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **thrust and propulsive efficiency** of an MHD channel, clearly showing the **velocity saturation effect**. A prototype MHD ship uses a channel of length $L_c = 5$ m, width $w = 1.2$ m, applied voltage $V = 250$ V, magnetic field $B = 4$ T, and seawater conductivity $\sigma = 5$ S/m. If the ship moves at $v = 4$ m/s, calculate: **(i)** the **stall thrust**, **(ii)** the **net thrust at the given speed**, and **(iii)** the **propulsive efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Sketch (2 Marks):** A rectangular MHD channel is built into the hull. Two electrodes (anode top, cathode bottom) drive current $I$ vertically. Two superconducting magnets (port & starboard) create field $B$ horizontally. The cross product $J \times B$ produces thrust along the ship's longitudinal axis.

**Step 2 — Physics (3 Marks):** The Lorentz force on a charge carrier $q$ moving with drift velocity $v_d$ is $F = qv_d B$. Summing over all charge carriers in the channel volume gives the volume force density $\vec{f} = \vec{J} \times \vec{B}$. Integrating over the channel volume $V = w h L_c$ yields the total thrust:
$$T = J B w L_c \quad \text{(assuming h cancels via } J = \sigma V/h\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Role of Parameters (2 Marks):**
- **Seawater conductivity $\sigma$:** Higher $\sigma$ → more current flows for a given applied voltage → **greater thrust** ($T \propto \sigma$).
- **Magnetic field $B$:** Stronger $B$ → greater force per unit current → **greater thrust** ($T \propto B$).
- Hence both $\sigma$ and $B$ must be maximized; this motivates **superconducting magnets** and the use of **saltwater** (not freshwater).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (3 Marks):** As shown in Section 3.2, the velocity-dependent thrust is:
$$T(v) = \sigma B w L_c (V - B L_c v)$$

**Numerical Calculation (4 Marks):**
Given: $L_c = 5$ m, $w = 1.2$ m, $V = 250$ V, $B = 4$ T, $\sigma = 5$ S/m, $v = 4$ m/s.

**(i) Stall Thrust** (at $v = 0$):
$$T_{stall} = \sigma B w L_c V = 5 \times 4 \times 1.2 \times 5 \times 250$$
$$T_{stall} = 5 \times 4 \times 1.2 \times 5 \times 250 = 30{,}000 \text{ N} = 30 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Net Thrust at v = 4 m/s:**
$$T(v) = \sigma B w L_c (V - B L_c v) = 5 \times 4 \times 1.2 \times 5 \times (250 - 4 \times 5 \times 4)$$
$$= 30{,}000 \times (250 - 80) = 30{,}000 \times 170 = 5{,}100{,}000 \text{ N} = 5.1 \text{ MN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** The induced back-EMF $BL_c v = 4 \times 5 \times 4 = 80$ V is **less than** applied $V = 250$ V, so thrust is positive. If $BL_c v > V$, the system would *decelerate* — students often forget to check this.

**(iii) Propulsive Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{B L_c v}{V} = \frac{4 \times 5 \times 4}{250} = \frac{80}{250} = 0.32 = 32\%$$

**Conclusion:** The MHD system produces **30 kN of stall thrust**, **5.1 MN at 4 m/s**, with a propulsive efficiency of **32%**. Higher efficiency requires higher ship speeds (closer to $v_{max} = V/(BL_c) = 12.5$ m/s).

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Supplementary Style]

> **(a)** Compare **MHD propulsion with conventional propeller propulsion** under the following heads: principle of thrust generation, acoustic signature, efficiency, moving parts, and power source requirement. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Explain the working of the **Electromagnetic Aircraft Launch System (EMALS)** used in modern aircraft carriers. How does it differ from the legacy **steam catapult**? List **two advantages** of EMALS. A linear induction motor of length 100 m accelerates a 30-tonne aircraft from rest to a launch speed of 78 m/s. Calculate the **average force** exerted on the aircraft and the **average power** delivered during the launch. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Head | MHD Propulsion | Conventional Propeller |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Lorentz force $F = J \times B$ on conductive seawater | Hydrodynamic lift on rotating blades |
| **Acoustic Signature** | Near-silent — no cavitation, no rotating blades | High — cavitation noise, blade-rate tones |
| **Efficiency** | 30–60% (research stage) | 70–85% (mature technology) |
| **Moving Parts** | None in thrust path; cryocooler for SC magnets | Propeller, shaft, gearbox, bearings |
| **Power Source** | Requires very high DC power → nuclear only | Diesel, gas turbine, nuclear all feasible |

**Conclusion:** MHD is preferred where **stealth** is paramount (military submarines), while propellers dominate where **efficiency and cost** matter (merchant vessels).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**EMALS Description (3 Marks):** EMALS uses a **linear induction motor (LIM)** instead of a steam piston. Energy is stored in a **rotating flywheel** or **supercapacitor bank**, then released through a **power converter** as variable-frequency AC to the linear motor's stator windings. The moving "rotor" (shuttle) is attached to the aircraft and accelerates it down the 100-m track to launch speed.

**Differences from Steam Catapult (2 Marks):**
- Steam catapults use a single steam piston with fixed energy profile — **hard on airframes**.
- EMALS uses **programmable, smooth acceleration** — reduces airframe stress, allows launch of lighter and heavier aircraft using the same system.

**Numerical Calculation (2 Marks):**
Given: $m = 30{,}000$ kg, $u = 0$, $v = 78$ m/s, $s = 100$ m.

Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$:
$$78^2 = 0 + 2a(100) \implies a = \frac{6084}{200} = 30.42 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Average Force:**
$$F = ma = 30{,}000 \times 30.42 = 9.126 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 912.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Average Power:** Using $P_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} F v$ (for uniform acceleration):
$$P_{avg} = 0.5 \times 9.126 \times 10^5 \times 78 \approx 35.6 \text{ MW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** Some textbooks use the work-energy theorem directly: $P_{avg} = \frac{\Delta KE}{t}$. The kinematic approach used above is equivalent and often faster in exams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision List — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Master Equation:** $T = \sigma B w L_c V$ — the *only* formula to derive thrust.
- ✅ **Lorentz Force Direction:** Use **Right-Hand Rule** (Index = $J$, Middle = $B$, Thumb = $F$).
- ✅ **Velocity Saturation:** Thrust decreases linearly with ship speed; at $v = V/(BL_c)$, thrust = 0.
- ✅ **Propulsive Efficiency:** $\eta = BL_c v / V$ — increases with speed, max ~100% asymptotically.
- ✅ **Key Ship:** Japanese **Yamato-1** (1992) — only operational superconducting MHD ship to date.
- ✅ **Seawater $\sigma$:** ~**4–5 S/m** — orders of magnitude lower than copper ($\sim 6 \times 10^7$ S/m), hence need for large currents.
- ✅ **EMALS:** Used in **USS Gerald R. Ford (CVN-78)** — replaced steam catapults.
- ✅ **No Moving Parts** in MHD thrust path → **stealth advantage** for submarines.
- ✅ **Hall Effect** becomes significant at high $B$ — reduces effective thrust (advanced topic).
- ✅ **Units Trivia:** $1$ **T** = $1$ **Wb/m²** = $1$ **kg/(A·s²)** = $10{,}000$ **Gauss** (CGS).
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Don't confuse **magnetic flux $\Phi$ (Wb)** with **magnetic flux density $B$ (T)**.
- ✅ **For KTU 14-mark answers:** Always draw the channel diagram, label $\vec{J}$, $\vec{B}$, $\vec{F}$, and state the limiting factor.

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Electromagnetic Propulsion etc.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship Standardisation Trials
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Ship Standardisation Trials — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Standardisation Trials (SST)**, also called **Sea Trials** or **Performance Trials**, are a series of **systematically planned and instrumented runs** conducted on a newly built (or majorly overhauled) vessel under **controlled sea conditions** to determine its **true speed–power–fuel relationship** at a known displacement, expressed under the **International Standard Sea Conditions (ISSC)** as defined by **ISO 15016:2015** and **ISO 3046**.

The objective is to obtain a **calibrated baseline performance curve** (Power vs. Speed and RPM vs. Speed) that the shipowner, shipbuilder, and engine builder use as the **contractual reference** for the vessel's entire operational life.

### Key Governing Standards

| Standard | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **ISO 15016:2015** | Ships and marine technology — Guidelines for the analysis of speed/power trial results |
| **ISO 3046-1** | Reciprocating internal combustion engines — Power, speed, fuel consumption |
| **IACS S21** | International Association of Classification Societies — Standardised trial procedure |
| **IMO Resolution A.601(15)** | Code on noise levels on board ships (related vibration) |
| **ASTM F3060** | Standard practice for analysis of speed and power trial data |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Carburetor Tune-Up" of a Ship

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine buying a new car.**
> The showroom claims it does **20 km/l at 80 km/h on a flat highway, 25 °C, no headwind, dry road, no passengers**.
> The dealer takes you for a **standardised test drive** — flat road, no wind, single driver, 25 °C, and confirms the claim by actual run.
> That test drive IS the **car's standardisation trial**.

For a ship, however, the variables are vastly more complex:
- **Ocean currents** (can give 2–3 knots for free, or take it away)
- **Wind and waves** (can add **40–60 %** to required power)
- **Water temperature and salinity** (change density and resistance)
- **Shallow water** (increases resistance by up to **20–30 %**)
- **Hull fouling** (a clean hull vs. fouled hull can differ by **15–25 %** in power)

The trial must therefore be **mathematically reduced** to a **fictitious "standard condition"** — so that the results are **comparable** between ships, between trials, and against the contract guarantee.

---

## 1.3 Why Are Standardisation Trials Conducted?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Contractual Verification** — to confirm the ship meets the **speed and power guarantee** in the building contract (typically the **Contracted Continuous Service Rating, CSR**).
> 2. **Baseline Performance Record** — to establish the **"as-built" reference curve** used throughout the ship's life for fuel budgeting, voyage planning, and hull/propeller performance monitoring.
> 3. **Engine–Propeller Matching** — to verify the **MCR (Maximum Continuous Rating)** RPM, **CSR (Continuous Service Rating)** RPM, and propeller efficiency at the design point.
> 4. **Regulatory Compliance** — to satisfy **Classification Society** (LR, DNV, ABS, BV) and **Flag State** requirements for entry into service.
> 5. **Diagnostic Baseline** — to provide a reference for **subsequent inclining experiments, fuel trials, and noise/vibration trials**.

---

## 1.4 The Standard Reference Conditions (ISSC)

The **International Standard Sea Conditions (ISSC)** used for correcting trial data are:

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Sea water density, $\rho_s$ | **1.025 t/m³** (or **1025 kg/m³**) |
| Water temperature | **15 °C** |
| Salinity | **3.5 %** (35 ‰) |
| Air temperature | **20 °C** |
| Atmospheric pressure | **1.013 × 10⁵ Pa** (1013 mbar) |
| Wind | **Zero** (calm) |
| Sea state | **Zero** (calm, no waves) |
| Current | **Zero** |
| Water depth | **Deep water** (depth ≥ **6 × draft**) |
| Hull | **Clean and new** |

> [!NOTE]
> A vessel that achieves **20.0 knots at 25,000 kW in actual trial weather** may, after correction, be quoted as achieving **20.5 knots at 23,200 kW in standard conditions** — a significant commercial difference in contract performance.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of a Standardisation Trial

A standardisation trial is conducted in **three well-defined phases**, each with its own purpose and instrumentation.

### Phase I — Pre-Trial Preparations
- **Fuel tank sounding** and **slops measurement** for accurate load displacement.
- **Deadweight survey** (lightship + consumables + cargo + crew + stores).
- **Calibration of instruments** — shaft torque meter, RPM pickup, GPS speed log, weather instruments, echo sounder.
- **Pre-trial checks**: hull inspection (clean?), propeller inspection (polished?), rudder centred, bilges dry, appendages verified.
- **Stabilisation runs** at low power to bring machinery to thermal equilibrium.

### Phase II — Speed–Power Runs
The vessel is run at **5 to 7 different speed points**, typically:
1. **Bare steerage** (minimum RPM for steerage, ~30–40 % MCR).
2. **Service speed** (~85–90 % MCR).
3. **Sea speed / Contract speed** (= **CSR, 100 % MCR**).
4. **One or two intermediate points**.
5. **Maximum continuous rating (MCR)** if contractually required.
6. **Hove-to (zero speed)** for current measurement.

At each speed, the vessel runs **at least 3 nautical miles in each direction** along a measured course (typically between two pairs of **DGPS-positioned transit marks** or in a **circular course**).

### Phase III — Post-Trial Analysis
- Tabulation of raw data.
- Application of corrections (wind, current, water density, depth).
- **Regression analysis** to fit clean power curves.
- Issue of **Final Trial Report** and **Standardisation Curves**.

---

## 2.2 The Correction Hierarchy

The trial data must be corrected through **five sequential corrections**:

```
Raw Trial Data (Measured Power, Pm, and Measured Speed, Vm)
        │
        ▼
1. CURRENT CORRECTION  →  Speed corrected for ocean current
        │
        ▼
2. WIND CORRECTION  →  Speed/Power corrected for wind force
        │
        ▼
3. WATER DENSITY CORRECTION  →  Power corrected for ρ ≠ 1025 kg/m³
        │
        ▼
4. WATER DEPTH CORRECTION  →  Power corrected for shallow water
        │
        ▼
5. AIR DENSITY CORRECTION  →  Shaft Power corrected to engine air reference
        │
        ▼
STANDARD POWER (Ps) AT STANDARD SPEED (Vs) IN ISSC
```

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical reference** for all numerical problems on Ship Standardisation Trials.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard Power | $P_s = P_m \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^3$ | kW | Core ISO 15016 correction |
| 2 | Standard Speed (after wind/current) | $V_s = \dfrac{V_m}{1 + f_w}$ | knots | $f_w$ = wind/current factor |
| 3 | Admiralty Coefficient | $C_A = \dfrac{V^{2.4} \cdot \Delta^{2/3}}{P}$ or $\dfrac{V^3 \cdot \Delta^{2/3}}{P}$ | — | For comparing similar hulls |
| 4 | Power Index Law (cube law) | $P \propto V^n$, where $n \approx 3$ for displacement hulls | — | $n = 3$ for full displacement, $n = 5$ for planing |
| 5 | Speed–Power Regression (Power) | $P = a \cdot N^{d}$ | kW | Non-linear regression; or $\log P = \log a + d \cdot \log N$ |
| 6 | Speed–RPM Regression (Speed) | $V = b \cdot N + c$ | knots | Linear in $N$ over normal range |
| 7 | Effective Power | $P_E = R_T \cdot V_s$ | kW | $R_T$ = total resistance in kN |
| 8 | Delivered Power | $P_D = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot Q$ | kW | $Q$ = shaft torque, $N$ = rev/s |
| 9 | Propulsive Coefficient | $\eta_D = \dfrac{P_E}{P_D}$ | — | Typically **0.55–0.70** |
| 10 | Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient | $\eta_{QD} = \eta_O \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_T$ | — | Includes rotational losses |
| 11 | Shaft Power (Brake) | $P_B = \dfrac{P_D}{\eta_S}$ | kW | $\eta_S$ = shaft efficiency, ≈ **0.97–0.99** |
| 12 | Wind Force Coefficient (approx) | $f_w \approx \dfrac{F_w}{2 \cdot C_R \cdot A_L \cdot V_m^2}$ | — | From ISO 15016 Annex B |
| 13 | Froude Propeller Slip | $s = 1 - \dfrac{V_A}{n \cdot D}$ | — | $V_A$ = wake-adj. speed, $n$ = rev/s |
| 14 | Resistance (Froude Scaling) | $R \propto \rho \cdot g \cdot L^3 \cdot f(F_n)$ | N | For model-to-ship extrapolation |
| 15 | Froude Number | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$ | — | Wave-making similarity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In any numerical problem, the **first step is always to apply the current and wind correction to speed, then the density correction to power, in that exact order.** Reversing the order leads to a wrong answer.

---

## 2.4 The Speed–Power Curve (Key Engineering Output)

The **Speed–Power Curve** (also called the **Standardisation Curve** or **Trial Curve**) is the **signature document** of a ship's performance. It has the following characteristics:

- **X-axis**: Ship speed $V$ in **knots**.
- **Y-axis**: Power $P$ in **kW** (left axis) and/or **RPM** $N$ (right axis).
- **Shape**: A **steeply rising curve** in the displacement regime ($P \propto V^3$), becoming **even steeper** as the vessel approaches the **hull speed limit** ($F_n \approx 0.40$ for slender ships, lower for fuller forms).
- **Validity range**: From **bare steerage** to the **highest tested speed (typically 110 % CSR)**.
- **The three plotted curves**:
  1. **Shaft Power (SHP / Brake Power) vs. V** — the contractual curve.
  2. **Effective Power (EHP) vs. V** — the hydrodynamic curve.
  3. **RPM vs. V** — for the engine builder's records.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why steep?** Doubling the speed of a displacement hull requires approximately **eight times the power**. The cube law dominates, plus the **wave-making resistance** rises even faster as the ship approaches its **hull speed** ($V_{hull} \approx 1.34 \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ in knots, with $L_{WL}$ in metres).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Trial Data

| Stakeholder | Use of Trial Data |
|---|---|
| **Shipowner** | Voyage planning, charter party speed claims, bunker budgeting |
| **Shipbuilder** | Contract performance certificate, defect liability |
| **Engine Builder (licensee)** | Verification of MCR match, fuel map validation |
| **Classification Society** | Sea-going qualification, propeller-efficiency record |
| **Naval Architect (in-service)** | Hull-fouling trend monitoring, trim optimisation |
| **Charterer** | Speed and consumption claim arbitration |
| **Port State Control** | EEDI / EEXI / CII compliance baseline |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Power Correction

We begin from the fundamental resistance-power-speed relationship for a displacement hull.

### Step 1 — Resistance Decomposition
The total resistance $R_T$ of a ship moving at speed $V$ in water of density $\rho$ is:

$$
R_T = R_F + R_W + R_A + R_B
$$

where:
- $R_F$ = frictional resistance (skin friction)
- $R_W$ = wave-making resistance
- $R_A$ = aerodynamic resistance (above-water)
- $R_B$ = correlation (form factor) resistance
- $R_T$ = total resistance

### Step 2 — Effective Power Definition
The effective power required to tow the ship at speed $V$ in water of density $\rho$ is:

$$
P_E = R_T \cdot V
$$

For a **geometrically similar hull** (Froude scaling) at the same Froude number, the wave-making resistance scales with $\rho g L^3$, while the frictional resistance scales with $\rho V^2 L^2$. The combined scaling is approximately:

$$
P_E \propto \rho \cdot V^3
$$

This is the basis of the **cube law**.

### Step 3 — Density Correction
If the trial is run in water of density $\rho$ (kg/m³) and we wish to convert to the standard density $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³, holding speed fixed, the effective power scales linearly with density:

$$
P_{E,s} = P_{E,m} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho}
$$

### Step 4 — Speed Correction
If the measured speed $V_m$ (after wind/current correction) is to be adjusted to the standard speed $V_s$, we use the cube law:

$$
\dfrac{P_{E,s}}{P_{E,m}} = \left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^3
$$

### Step 5 — Combining Density and Speed
Combining the two corrections (density and speed), and noting that the shaft power $P$ is proportional to $P_E$ through the (assumed invariant) **propulsive coefficient** $\eta_D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s &= P_m \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^3 \\[6pt]
\boxed{P_s &= P_m \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **ISO 15016 Master Correction Equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The density correction uses the ratio $\rho_s / \rho$ because **heavier water means more wave-making resistance and more skin friction per unit volume of displaced water**. The speed correction is cubed because **resistance grows approximately as $V^2$** (mostly) and **power is force times speed**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Admiralty Coefficient

The Admiralty Coefficient $C_A$ is a **dimensionless figure of merit** used to compare hulls of similar type. It assumes that the propulsive coefficient and the resistance form factor are roughly constant for similar hulls.

### Step 1 — Resistance Proportionality
For a family of similar hulls at the same Froude number:

$$
R_T \propto \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^2
$$

because displacement $\Delta \propto L^3$, so $L \propto \Delta^{1/3}$, and substituting into $R_T \propto \rho L^2 V^2$ gives $R_T \propto \Delta^{2/3} V^2$.

### Step 2 — Effective Power
$$
P_E = R_T \cdot V \propto \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3
$$

### Step 3 — Shaft Power
If propulsive coefficient is invariant:
$$
P \propto \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3
$$

### Step 4 — Rearranging
Rearranging so that a constant is on one side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{P} \\[6pt]
\boxed{C_A &= \dfrac{V^3 \cdot \Delta^{2/3}}{P}}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **higher** $C_A$ implies a **more efficient** ship (more speed per unit power for a given displacement).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Speed–Power Index "n"

Often the trial data is summarised as:

$$
P = K \cdot V^n
$$

where $n$ is the **power index** (typically **3.0–3.5** for displacement hulls, **5–7** for semi-displacement, and **> 7** for planing).

### Step 1 — Logarithmic Linearisation
Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$
\ln P = \ln K + n \cdot \ln V
$$

### Step 2 — Linear Regression
If we have $m$ data points $(V_i, P_i)$, the least-squares estimate of $n$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \dfrac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln V_i \\[4pt]
\bar{y} &= \dfrac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln P_i \\[4pt]
n &= \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\ln V_i - \bar{x})(\ln P_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\ln V_i - \bar{x})^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then:

$$
K = e^{\bar{y} - n \bar{x}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For displacement hulls** (the KTU-standard case), the expected value is $n = 3.0$. If the regression yields $n > 3.5$, it usually indicates that the ship is **approaching its hull speed limit** where wave-making resistance is growing faster than $V^2$.

---

## 3.4 Work-Out Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern — Part B, 14 marks):**
> A bulk carrier is on standardisation trial. The measured speed on the measured mile is **$V_m = 18.0$ knots** and the shaft power is **$P_m = 14{,}200$ kW**. The trial is run in water of density **$\rho = 1020$ kg/m³**. The wind/current correction factor is **$f_w = 0.025$** (i.e., the measured speed is 2.5 % higher than the true ship-through-water speed). Find the **standard power $P_s$** at the **standard speed $V_s$** in ISSC.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Correct the measured speed for wind and current.**

The standard speed is the true through-water speed, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \dfrac{V_m}{1 + f_w} \\[4pt]
V_s &= \dfrac{18.0}{1 + 0.025} \\[4pt]
V_s &= \dfrac{18.0}{1.025} \\[4pt]
\boxed{V_s &= 17.561 \text{ knots}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the ISO 15016 master correction for standard power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s &= P_m \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^3 \\[6pt]
P_s &= 14{,}200 \cdot \dfrac{1025}{1020} \cdot \left(\dfrac{17.561}{18.0}\right)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2a: Density ratio term.**

$$
\dfrac{1025}{1020} = 1.00490
$$

**Step 2b: Speed ratio term.**

$$
\dfrac{17.561}{18.0} = 0.97561
$$

$$
(0.97561)^3 = 0.92836
$$

**Step 2c: Multiply all three factors.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s &= 14{,}200 \times 1.00490 \times 0.92836 \\[4pt]
P_s &= 14{,}200 \times 0.93290 \\[4pt]
\boxed{P_s &\approx 13{,}247 \text{ kW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**

- The **true through-water speed** (in calm sea) is **17.56 knots**, not 18.0.
- The **standard power** required is **13,247 kW**, **6.7 % lower** than the measured power.
- The 0.5 % density gain is dwarfed by the 7.2 % speed correction, showing that **speed correction is the dominant term** in standardisation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If the same ship were tested in colder (1027 kg/m³) and calm water at exactly 18.0 knots true speed, the standard power would be slightly higher than measured (≈ 14,140 kW).** Trial engineers must always correct *down to* standard, not up.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Finding the Power Index n

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern — Part B, 14 marks, part (a)):**
> The following trial data is obtained for a general cargo ship at displacement $\Delta = 18{,}500$ t:

| Trial # | $V$ (knots) | $P$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.0 | 3,200 |
| 2 | 14.0 | 5,450 |
| 3 | 16.0 | 8,300 |
| 4 | 18.0 | 12,400 |
| 5 | 19.5 | 16,200 |

> Determine the **power index $n$** and the **constant $K$** in $P = K V^n$ using log-linear regression. Comment on the result.

### Solution

**Step 1: Take natural logs.**

| $V$ | $\ln V$ | $P$ | $\ln P$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 12.0 | 2.4849 | 3,200 | 8.0702 |
| 14.0 | 2.6391 | 5,450 | 8.6029 |
| 16.0 | 2.7726 | 8,300 | 9.0231 |
| 18.0 | 2.8904 | 12,400 | 9.4249 |
| 19.5 | 2.9704 | 16,200 | 9.6933 |

**Step 2: Compute means.**

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{2.4849 + 2.6391 + 2.7726 + 2.8904 + 2.9704}{5} = \dfrac{13.7574}{5} = 2.7515
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{8.0702 + 8.6029 + 9.0231 + 9.4249 + 9.6933}{5} = \dfrac{44.8144}{5} = 8.9629
$$

**Step 3: Compute deviations and cross-products.**

| $V$ | $\ln V$ | $\ln P$ | $\ln V - \bar{x}$ | $\ln P - \bar{y}$ | Product | $(\ln V - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 12.0 | 2.4849 | 8.0702 | −0.2666 | −0.8927 | 0.23799 | 0.07108 |
| 14.0 | 2.6391 | 8.6029 | −0.1124 | −0.3600 | 0.04047 | 0.01263 |
| 16.0 | 2.7726 | 9.0231 | +0.0211 | +0.0602 | 0.00127 | 0.00045 |
| 18.0 | 2.8904 | 9.4249 | +0.1389 | +0.4620 | 0.06416 | 0.01929 |
| 19.5 | 2.9704 | 9.6933 | +0.2189 | +0.7304 | 0.15986 | 0.04792 |
| **Σ** |  |  |  |  | **0.50375** | **0.15137** |

**Step 4: Compute $n$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{0.50375}{0.15137} \\[4pt]
\boxed{n &= 3.328}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $K$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln K &= \bar{y} - n \bar{x} \\[4pt]
\ln K &= 8.9629 - (3.328)(2.7515) \\[4pt]
\ln K &= 8.9629 - 9.1570 \\[4pt]
\ln K &= -0.1941 \\[4pt]
K &= e^{-0.1941} \\[4pt]
\boxed{K &= 0.8236}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final fitted equation and comment.**

$$
\boxed{P = 0.824 \cdot V^{3.33}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** The power index $n = 3.33$ is **slightly above the theoretical 3.0 for pure displacement mode**, indicating the ship is **beginning to experience significant wave-making resistance** at the upper end of the speed range (16–19.5 knots). The hull is approaching its **hull speed limit** for its length.


---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Ship Standardisation Trials
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A, Q1a, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer (target: 50–80 words):**

> **Ship Standardisation Trials** are a series of **calibrated, instrumented speed and power runs** conducted on a newly built or overhauled vessel, typically over a **measured mile**, to determine the ship's **speed–power–fuel relationship** at a **known displacement**, and to express the result in the **International Standard Sea Conditions (ISSC)** as per **ISO 15016:2015**. The trials are required by the **classification society** to verify the contractual speed and power guarantee and to provide the **baseline performance curves** for the ship's operational life.

---

### Question 2: State the International Standard Sea Conditions (ISSC) and Their Numerical Values
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1b, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **International Standard Sea Conditions (ISSC)** are the reference conditions to which all trial results are mathematically corrected. They are:

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Sea water density, $\rho_s$ | **1.025 t/m³** |
| Water temperature | **15 °C** |
| Salinity | **3.5 %** (35 ppt) |
| Wind, waves, current | **Zero** |
| Water depth | **Deep** (≥ **6 × draft**) |
| Hull condition | **Clean, newly painted** |
| Air pressure | **1013 mbar** |
| Air temperature | **20 °C** |

These values are codified in **ISO 15016:2015** and **IACS Recommendation 71**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Numerical + Theory Combination

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B, Q4a, 14 marks]**
> **(a)** A container ship on standardisation trial recorded a **measured speed of 22.0 knots** at a **shaft power of 38,500 kW**. The water density at the trial was **1018 kg/m³** and the wind/current correction factor was **$f_w = 0.020$**. Compute the **standard power** corresponding to the **standard speed** under ISSC. **[7 marks]**
> **(b)** Briefly explain the **five sequential corrections** applied to trial data before it is converted to standard conditions, and state which correction is the **largest in magnitude** for a typical high-speed container ship. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1: Correct the speed for wind and current.**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{V_m}{1 + f_w} = \dfrac{22.0}{1 + 0.020} = \dfrac{22.0}{1.020} = 21.569 \text{ knots}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the ISO 15016 standard-power correction.**

$$
P_s = P_m \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^3
$$

$$
P_s = 38{,}500 \times \dfrac{1025}{1018} \times \left(\dfrac{21.569}{22.0}\right)^3
$$

$$
P_s = 38{,}500 \times 1.00688 \times (0.98041)^3
$$

$$
P_s = 38{,}500 \times 1.00688 \times 0.94232
$$

$$
\boxed{P_s \approx 36{,}514 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Answer:** The standard power at the standard speed is approximately **36,514 kW**, which is **5.16 % less** than the measured power.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

The five sequential corrections are:

1. **Current correction** — the measured speed-over-ground is decomposed into through-water speed and current velocity using the **cosine law of velocities**, and the **average of reciprocal runs** is used to cancel out the current.
2. **Wind correction** — the relative wind force on the hull and superstructure is estimated using the **ISO 15016 Annex B formula** (a function of wind speed, wind angle, and the lateral projected area $A_L$); the power required to overcome the wind is computed and removed.
3. **Water-density correction** — the actual $\rho$ is computed from the trial temperature and salinity using the **UNESCO EOS-80 equation of state**, and the power is scaled by the ratio $\rho_s / \rho$.
4. **Water-depth correction** — if the trial is in **shallow water** ($h/T < 6$), the **slack-water sinkage and trim** corrections and the increased resistance from the **blockage effect** are applied.
5. **Air-density correction** — the **shaft power** is normalised to the standard air density of **1.225 kg/m³** for the engine's reference, since the **charge-air pressure and mass flow** depend on $\rho_{air}$.

> **Largest correction for a high-speed container ship:** the **wind correction** typically dominates, because the **large superstructure and high freeboard** present a substantial windage area (often 800–1500 m²), and at 22 knots the **apparent wind speed is high**. The wind correction may add **5–10 %** to the power reading.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Theory + Short Numerical)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q4b, 14 marks]**
> **(a)** What is the **Admiralty Coefficient**? Derive its mathematical form and explain its **engineering utility** in comparing the performance of similar merchant ships. **[7 marks]**
> **(b)** A bulk carrier has the following trial data at a displacement of **28,000 t**:

| Trial # | $V$ (knots) | $P$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.0 | 4,800 |
| 2 | 14.0 | 7,900 |
| 3 | 15.5 | 11,200 |

> Calculate the **Admiralty Coefficient** for each trial and comment on the trend. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Definition:**
The **Admiralty Coefficient** $C_A$ is a **dimensionless figure of merit** that quantifies the **propulsive efficiency** of a ship by relating its **speed, displacement, and delivered power**. It is widely used in the **merchant ship design office** to compare sister-ships and to detect performance deviations.

**Derivation:**

For a family of geometrically similar displacement hulls, Froude's law of comparison gives:

$$
R_T \propto \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_E = R_T \cdot V \propto \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3
$$

Assuming the **propulsive coefficient $\eta_D$** is approximately constant for a given hull family:

$$
P \propto \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{\eta_D}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{C_A = \dfrac{V^3 \cdot \Delta^{2/3}}{P}}
$$

**Engineering utility:**
- **Benchmarking** of similar vessels (e.g., Panamax bulk carriers).
- **Quick order-of-magnitude power estimation** during concept design.
- **Hull-fouling monitoring**: a drop in $C_A$ over years indicates fouling.
- **Trim-optimisation feedback**: $C_A$ peaks at the design trim.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

For each trial, we use:

$$
C_A = \dfrac{V^3 \cdot \Delta^{2/3}}{P}
$$

with $\Delta = 28{,}000$ t, so $\Delta^{2/3} = (28{,}000)^{2/3}$.

**Compute $\Delta^{2/3}$:**

$$
\Delta^{2/3} = (28{,}000)^{2/3} = (28{,}000^{1/3})^2
$$

Cube root of 28,000:

$$
28{,}000^{1/3} \approx 30.41
$$

Squaring:

$$
\Delta^{2/3} \approx 924.5
$$

**Compute $V^3$ and $C_A$ for each trial:**

| Trial | $V$ (kt) | $V^3$ | $P$ (kW) | $C_A$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.0 | 1,728 | 4,800 | $\dfrac{1{,}728 \times 924.5}{4{,}800} = 332.8$ |
| 2 | 14.0 | 2,744 | 7,900 | $\dfrac{2{,}744 \times 924.5}{7{,}900} = 321.1$ |
| 3 | 15.5 | 3,724 | 11,200 | $\dfrac{3{,}724 \times 924.5}{11{,}200} = 307.3$ |

**Comment on the trend:**

The Admiralty Coefficient **decreases** from **332.8 → 321.1 → 307.3** as the speed rises from 12.0 to 15.5 knots. This is a **~7.7 % drop**, indicating that the vessel is operating in the **displacement regime where $P \propto V^n$ with $n > 3$** — the **wave-making resistance is growing faster than the cube law** as the ship approaches its **hull speed limit**. For a constant $C_A$, the **power index** $n$ can be inferred: in this case, the effective $n$ from the data is:

$$
n = \dfrac{\log(11{,}200 / 4{,}800)}{\log(15.5 / 12.0)} = \dfrac{\log(2.333)}{\log(1.292)} = \dfrac{0.8463}{0.2565} \approx 3.30
$$

This $n = 3.30 > 3$ confirms the ship is **slightly above the pure cube law**, which is why $C_A$ drops at higher speeds. For a single ship, $C_A$ should be **approximately constant** only if the **power index is exactly 3.0**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Ship Standardisation Trials Problems:**
> 1. **Order-of-operations error** — applying the **density correction to the speed** instead of to the **power**. The density ratio multiplies the power; the speed ratio (cubed) also multiplies the power. Both are power multipliers, not speed multipliers.
> 2. **Forgetting the cube** on the speed ratio — writing $\dfrac{V_s}{V_m}$ instead of $\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^3$ is a **3-mark killer**.
> 3. **Using the wrong water density** — the standard is **1025 kg/m³**, not 1000 kg/m³ (fresh water) and not 1030 kg/m³ (the value some textbooks incorrectly use).
> 4. **Sign convention on wind/current correction** — the standard speed is the **true through-water calm condition**, which is **lower** than the GPS speed if there is a **following current** and **higher** if there is a **head current**. The factor $(1 + f_w)$ in the denominator is correct for a **head wind/current**, which is the **safe and conservative** assumption when the direction is unknown.
> 5. **Inverting $P_s$ and $P_m$** — standard power is the power the ship **would have needed** in standard conditions. For most trials, **$P_s < P_m$** because trial conditions are usually rougher than ISSC.
> 6. **Mixing up units** — speed must be in **knots** (or **m/s**, but consistent), power in **kW**, density in **kg/m³**. Mixing SI with nautical causes 10–20 % errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Ship Standardisation Trials (Module 4, PBNST404)**

### A. Definitions
- **Ship Standardisation Trials** — calibrated sea runs to determine speed–power–fuel in **ISSC** (ISO 15016:2015).
- **ISSC** — the imaginary reference condition: $\rho_s = 1.025$ t/m³, $T = 15$ °C, $S = 3.5$ %, zero wind, zero waves, zero current, deep water, clean hull.
- **Measured Speed $V_m$** — the GPS-recorded speed-over-ground.
- **Standard Speed $V_s$** — the through-water speed in ISSC after corrections.
- **Measured Power $P_m$** — the brake/shaft power recorded.
- **Standard Power $P_s$** — the power the ship would require in ISSC.
- **Effective Power $P_E$** — $R_T \times V$, the pure hydrodynamic power.
- **Propulsive Coefficient $\eta_D$** — $P_E / P_D$, typically 0.55–0.70.
- **Delivered Power $P_D$** — $2\pi N Q$, the power at the propeller.

### B. Master Equations (must memorise)
- **ISO 15016 Standard Power:** $\boxed{P_s = P_m \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^3}$
- **Standard Speed:** $\boxed{V_s = \dfrac{V_m}{1 + f_w}}$
- **Admiralty Coefficient:** $\boxed{C_A = \dfrac{V^3 \Delta^{2/3}}{P}}$
- **Power Index Law:** $\boxed{P = K V^n}$ with $n = 3$ for displacement hulls.
- **Speed–RPM regression:** $\boxed{V = bN + c}$ (linear).
- **Power–RPM regression:** $\boxed{P = aN^d}$ (log-linear).

### C. Standard Values
- $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³
- $T_{standard} = 15$ °C
- $S_{standard} = 35$ ‰
- $n = 3.0$ for pure displacement
- $C_A$ for a clean bulk carrier ≈ **300–400**
- $C_A$ for a clean container ship ≈ **250–350**
- $\eta_D \approx 0.55$–$0.70$

### D. Procedure Order
1. **Pre-trial** (calibration, displacement, hull check).
2. **Speed-power runs** (5–7 points, reciprocal direction).
3. **Post-trial** (5 corrections → regression → standardisation curves).

### E. Five Corrections (in order)
1. Current (vector)
2. Wind
3. Density ($\rho_s/\rho$)
4. Depth
5. Air density

### F. Exam Tips
- Always **show the cube of the speed ratio explicitly**.
- Always state the **assumed power index** (3.0 unless stated).
- Always **quote the standard** (ISO 15016:2015) when referencing corrections.
- Always include **units** in the final answer.
- The **Admiralty Coefficient is constant only if the power index is exactly 3.0** — a subtle but frequently asked concept.

### G. Practical Sanity Check
- A 10 % increase in speed → **~33 % increase in power** (cube law).
- A 10 % increase in displacement → **~6.7 % increase in power** (2/3 law on $\Delta$).
- A 5 % fouling of hull → **~15 % increase in power** at the same speed.
- A 2 °C warmer sea → **~0.3 % less power** required (density drop).

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Ship Standardisation Trials**
> **Total Marks Weightage Reference (KTU 2024 ESE):** 2-Part Question = 14 marks; Part-A = 3 marks; cumulative across Modules 1–5.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propeller Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST404 - SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Propeller Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Propeller materials* are engineered metallic (and in advanced applications, non-metallic composite) alloys specifically selected and formulated to withstand the combined hostile marine environment of **seawater corrosion**, **cavitation erosion**, **mechanical stress** (centrifugal, hydrodynamic, vibratory), and **galvanic interaction** with the hull, while maintaining hydrodynamic efficiency over the design service life of the propeller (typically **20–25 years** or **100,000+ operating hours**).

The choice of material is governed by:
- Ship type and operating profile
- Power absorption (P_D) and rotational speed (n)
- Wake distribution and cavitation susceptibility
- Sea water chemistry (salinity, temperature, dissolved oxygen)
- Manufacturing technology available at the shipyard
- Life-cycle cost (CAPEX + OPEX)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per KTU Module 4, the focus is on **manganese bronze, nickel-aluminum bronze, and stainless steel** as the principal marine propeller alloys, along with corrosion mechanisms and material selection criteria.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Submarine Bicycle Wheel"**
> Imagine a bicycle wheel spinning underwater at high speed, attacked continuously by invisible sandblasting (cavitation bubbles), saltwater (corrosion), and the pulling force of a moving truck (hydrodynamic thrust). The "spokes" of this wheel are made from a special metal that must:
> 1. **Not rust** (corrosion resistance) — like a stainless steel spoon left in salt water
> 2. **Not chip** (cavitation resistance) — like a strong ceramic mug surviving a hammer tap
> 3. **Not stretch or break** (tensile strength) — like a heavy-duty towing cable
> 4. **Not poison the hull** (galvanic compatibility) — like using a non-reactive coating between two dissimilar metals
>
> Different "recipes" of copper, aluminum, nickel, iron, and manganese give us **manganese bronze**, **nickel-aluminum bronze**, and other marine alloys — each with its own trade-off.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Metrics for Propeller Materials:**
> - **Yield Strength (σ_y):** **275–650 MPa** (typical range for marine prop alloys)
> - **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u):** **450–900 MPa**
> - **Elongation at break (A%):** **15–30%** (ductility indicator)
> - **Brinell Hardness (HB):** **120–250**
> - **Density (ρ):** **7,500 – 8,900 kg/m³**
> - **Fatigue Limit (endurance limit):** **~40–50% of σ_u** for bronze; **~45–55% of σ_u** for stainless steel
> - **Cavitation erosion resistance index (ASTM G32):** Cumulative mass loss in **mg** over **20 hours** at **20 kHz, 50 μm amplitude**
> - **Standard Reference:** **ASTM B584** (copper-alloy castings), **ASTM A743** (cast stainless steel), **BS 1400** (UK equivalent)
> - **Standard Salt Water Composition:** **3.5% NaCl** (≈ **0.6 M NaCl**), pH ≈ **8.2**

---

## 1.4 Why Material Selection Matters in Ship Propulsion

> [!NOTE]
> A 70,000 DWT bulk carrier's propeller can develop **15,000–20,000 kW** at the design point, with blade tip speeds reaching **35–45 m/s** and **RPM** of 70–120. The blade root bears **centrifugal loads up to 30 MPa**, while cavitation pits initiate on the suction side where local pressures drop below **vapor pressure** (≈ **2.3 kPa at 25°C seawater**). A single propeller replacement due to material failure can cost **USD 0.5 – 2 million** and **30–60 days of off-hire**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Material Requirements (Operational Logic)

The propeller must simultaneously satisfy **five competing engineering demands**. Each material is a compromise:

| # | Demand | Engineering Meaning | Quantifiable Parameter |
|---|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical Strength** | Withstand centrifugal, hydrodynamic, and ice loads | σ_y, σ_u (MPa) |
| 2 | **Corrosion Resistance** | Resist general, pitting, and galvanic corrosion in seawater | Corrosion rate (mm/yr), pitting potential E_pit (mV vs SCE) |
| 3 | **Cavitation Erosion Resistance** | Resist surface pitting from bubble collapse | Volume loss (mm³), incubation time (h) |
| 4 | **Fatigue Strength** | Survive 10⁸–10⁹ stress cycles | Endurance limit σ_e (MPa) |
| 5 | **Manufacturability & Cost** | Castable, machinable, weldable, affordable | Foundry capability, weld repair-ability, $/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the trade-off:** Adding aluminum (Al) and nickel (Ni) to copper (Cu) improves corrosion and cavitation resistance but **reduces castability** and **raises cost**. Adding iron (Fe) and manganese (Mn) refines grain structure, boosting strength, but excessive Fe creates brittle phases.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Marine Propeller Materials

### A. Copper-Based Alloys (Traditional — 90% of merchant fleet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Copper alloys are preferred because Cu forms a stable, self-healing **Cu₂O / CuO passive oxide layer** in seawater, which dramatically slows further corrosion.

#### (i) Manganese Bronze (High-Tensile Brass)
- **Composition:** Cu **55–60%**, Zn **35–40%**, Mn **0.5–3%**, Al **0.5–2%**, Fe **0.5–1.5%**, Sn optional
- **UNS Designation:** **UNS C86100 – C86800**
- **Standard:** ASTM B584, BS 1400 HTB1/HTB2
- **σ_y:** ~ **275–345 MPa** | **σ_u:** ~ **450–620 MPa** | **Elongation:** ~ **20–30%**
- **Density:** ~ **8,400 kg/m³**
- **Cavitation resistance:** Moderate
- **Cost:** Low (USD 8–12/kg)
- **Limitation:** Susceptible to **dezincification** (selective leaching of Zn in seawater) → loss of strength

#### (ii) Nickel-Aluminum Bronze (NAB) — *The Workhorse*
- **Composition:** Cu **76–80%**, Al **8.5–11%**, Ni **3–5.5%**, Fe **3–5.5%**, Mn **0.5–3%**
- **UNS Designation:** **UNS C95500, C95800, C95520**
- **Standard:** ASTM B584, **BS 1400 AB2 / AB3**, **DEF STAN 02-747** (UK Naval)
- **σ_y:** ~ **320–450 MPa** | **σ_u:** ~ **600–800 MPa** | **Elongation:** ~ **15–25%**
- **Density:** ~ **7,650 kg/m³** (lighter than Mn-bronze)
- **Cavitation resistance:** **Excellent** (best among Cu-alloys)
- **Cost:** Medium (USD 15–22/kg)
- **Why it's dominant:** Combination of high strength, excellent seawater corrosion resistance, and superior cavitation performance.

#### (iii) Manganese-Aluminum Bronze (MAB)
- **Composition:** Cu ~ 70–75%, Zn 20–25%, Al 3–6%, Mn 2–5%, Fe 1–3%
- **Used in:** Medium-duty commercial propellers
- **Property:** Sits between Mn-bronze and NAB in cost/performance

### B. Stainless Steel (Modern — High-Speed / Naval)

- **Grades:** **Austenitic 17-4 PH**, **Super-duplex (UNS J93380, Zeron 100)**, **Austenitic 316L** (limited)
- **Composition (Super-duplex):** Cr **24–26%**, Ni **6–8%**, Mo **3–5%**, N **0.2–0.3%**, balance Fe
- **σ_y:** ~ **550–700 MPa** (duplex) | **σ_u:** ~ **750–900 MPa**
- **Density:** ~ **7,800 kg/m³**
- **Cavitation resistance:** Excellent (hard surface, ~250 HB)
- **Corrosion resistance:** Outstanding (pitting resistance equivalent number PREN > 40)
- **Cost:** High (USD 25–40/kg), but **casting is difficult** — usually fabricated from **forged/welded assemblies** or **investment cast hubs + welded blades**
- **Application:** Naval combatants, ice-strengthened propellers, high-speed vessels (e.g., naval frigates, LNG carriers)

### C. Special / Advanced Materials

| Material | Use Case | Note |
|----------|----------|------|
| **Ni-Al Bronze + cladding** | Cavitation-prone zones | Weld overlay on suction side |
| **Titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V)** | Submarines, deep-diving | Very high cost, excellent in seawater |
| **GFRP / CFRP composites** | Small craft, research vessels | Lightweight, but cavitation and impact issues |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and follow KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 expectations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma_c = \rho \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r^2$ | Centrifugal stress in rotating blade (thin-walled disc approx.) | Pa |
| 2 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{\rho \, \omega^2}{2}\left(R^2 - r^2\right) \cdot K$ | Centrifugal stress in solid disc (Lamé form) | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_c + \sigma_h + \sigma_b$ | Combined stress (centrifugal + hydrodynamic + bending) | Pa |
| 4 | $\sigma_e \geq 0.4\,\sigma_u$ | Endurance limit (for bronze) | Pa |
| 5 | $N_f = \left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e}\right)^{-k}$ | S-N (Basquin) fatigue relation, $k = 6$–$10$ for bronzes | cycles |
| 6 | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3\%\text{Mo} + 16\%\text{N}$ | Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (stainless) | — |
| 7 | $E_{\text{corr}} = E^0_{\text{Cu/Cu}^{2+}} + \dfrac{0.0592}{2}\log[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$ | Nernst corrosion potential | V vs SHE |
| 8 | $v_{0.6} = 0.6 \cdot n \cdot D$ | Design tip speed (0.6 rule, Burns) | m/s |
| 9 | $P_D = \rho \, n^3 D^5 K_T$ | Power identity (used in cavitation correlation) | W |
| 10 | $\text{SF}_{\text{cav}} = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2}$ | Cavitation similarity parameter (sigma-σ_n) | — |
| 11 | $C_{\text{rate}} = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{\text{corr}}}{\rho_{\text{alloy}} \cdot n_e}$ | Corrosion rate (Faraday's law) | mm/yr |
| 12 | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | Allowable stress, $n = 1.5$–$2.0$ for marine | Pa |

> Where: $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = local radius (m), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $R$ = tip radius, $D$ = propeller diameter, $K$ = stress concentration factor, $p_0$ = ambient, $p_v$ = vapor pressure, $K_T$ = thrust coefficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula (1) sets the **minimum yield strength** required for a given RPM and diameter. For example, a **6 m diameter, 100 rpm** propeller requires σ_y ≥ **~250 MPa** for safe operation — directly driving the material choice toward NAB or duplex stainless.

---

## 2.4 Corrosion Mechanisms in Seawater

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four types of corrosion attack a propeller:**

1. **General (uniform) corrosion** — slow metal loss across the surface, **0.05–0.3 mm/yr** for NAB
2. **Pitting corrosion** — localized deep cavities, especially in stainless steel if passivation is broken
3. **Galvanic corrosion** — when bronze propeller couples with steel hull (potential difference **0.2–0.4 V**); mitigated by **sacrificial Zn/Al anodes** on shaft or hull
4. **Cavitation-erosion-corrosion (CEC)** — **synergistic** mechanism; mechanical damage removes protective oxide → accelerated corrosion
5. **Dezincification** (for Mn-bronze) — selective loss of zinc leaving porous Cu-rich skeleton

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I: Centrifugal Stress in a Rotating Propeller Blade (Thin-Disc Approximation)

### Given
- Propeller: 4-bladed, $D = 4.0$ m
- Service RPM: $n = 120$ rpm
- Blade approximated as a thin rotating disc of density $\rho_{\text{disc}}$
- Material: NAB, $\rho_{\text{disc}} = 7{,}650$ kg/m³

### Find
- Centrifugal (radial) stress at the blade root ($r = 0.45 \cdot D$)

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to angular velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{n}{60} \\
&= 2\pi \cdot \frac{120}{60} \\
&= 2\pi \cdot 2 \\
&= 12.566 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define radial coordinate from disc centre to tip:**

$$
R_{\text{disc}} = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{4.0}{2} = 2.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the rotating thin-disc stress formula** (Timoshenko, *Theory of Plates and Shells*):

$$
\sigma_r(r) = \frac{3 + \nu}{8}\,\rho\,\omega^2 \left(R_{\text{disc}}^2 - r^2\right)
$$

Assume Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.33$ for NAB.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the constant multiplier:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{3 + \nu}{8} &= \frac{3 + 0.33}{8} \\
&= \frac{3.33}{8} \\
&= 0.4163
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\omega^2$:**

$$
\omega^2 = (12.566)^2 = 157.91 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Determine $r$ at blade root** (root radius taken as 0.45D from hub centre):

$$
r = 0.45 \cdot 2.0 = 0.90 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute $\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 - r^2 &= (2.0)^2 - (0.90)^2 \\
&= 4.00 - 0.81 \\
&= 3.19 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute radial stress at root:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r &= 0.4163 \cdot 7{,}650 \cdot 157.91 \cdot 3.19 \\
&= 0.4163 \cdot 7{,}650 \cdot 503.73 \\
&= 0.4163 \cdot 3{,}853{,}535 \\
&\approx 1.604 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 1.60 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A purely radial centrifugal stress of **~1.6 MPa** at the root is *small* compared to the **~20–30 MPa** stress from hydrodynamic bending in a heavily loaded blade. The dominant design driver is therefore **combined (von-Mises) stress**, not pure centrifugal.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN) for Super-Duplex Stainless Steel

### Given
- Composition: Cr = 25%, Mo = 4%, N = 0.27%

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Recall PREN formula:**

$$
\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute each term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PREN} &= 25 + 3.3 \cdot 4 + 16 \cdot 0.27 \\
&= 25 + 13.2 + 4.32 \\
&= 42.52
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PREN > 40 ⇒ **highly resistant to pitting** in ambient-temperature seawater. This is the engineering basis for selecting **super-duplex stainless steel** for naval and ice-class propellers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Cavitation Number (Sigma) for a Marine Propeller

### Given
- Depth of blade immersion $h = 4$ m
- $p_{\text{atm}} = 101.3$ kPa
- $\rho_{\text{sw}} = 1{,}025$ kg/m³ (standard seawater)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $V = 8$ m/s (ship speed)
- $p_v = 2.3$ kPa at 25°C

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Total static pressure at immersion depth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_0 &= p_{\text{atm}} + \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot h \\
&= 101{,}300 + 1{,}025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 4 \\
&= 101{,}300 + 40{,}221 \\
&= 141{,}521 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 141.5 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic pressure reference:**

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1{,}025 \cdot 64 = 32{,}800 \text{ Pa} = 32.8 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Cavitation number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \frac{p_0 - p_v}{q} \\
&= \frac{141{,}521 - 2{,}300}{32{,}800} \\
&= \frac{139{,}221}{32{,}800} \\
&\approx 4.24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> $\sigma_n > 1.5$ ⇒ no significant sheet cavitation. Since 4.24 ≫ 1.5, the propeller is **cavitation-safe** under these conditions. The threshold value comes from **Burrill's empirical chart** and **Barnaby–Barnwell cavitation diagrams**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Material Selection by Ship Type (KTU High-Yield)

| Ship Type | Power Range | Recommended Material | Reason |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|--------|
| Small fishing boat (<500 kW) | Low | **Mn-Bronze (HTB1)** | Cost-effective, low stress |
| General cargo / bulk carrier (5,000–20,000 kW) | Medium | **NAB (UNS C95800)** | Best strength-cavitation-cost balance |
| Naval frigate / combatant (20,000–40,000 kW, high n) | High | **Super-duplex stainless / 17-4 PH** | High σ_y, fatigue, tip speed |
| Ice-breaker / polar vessel | High + impact | **NAB with Cl / Duplex** | Impact toughness, cavitation |
| LNG carrier (BOG-driven, slow) | Medium-low | **NAB (BS 1400 AB2)** | Long service, cavitation prone |
| Submarine (deep submergence) | Variable | **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V** | Seawater + non-magnetic + strength |
| Superyacht / fast ferry | High n | **NAB or Mn-bronze** | Moderate cost, high finish |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Propeller Material Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Operational Profile] --> B{Power & RPM}
    B -->|Low <2,000 kW| C[Manganese Bronze HTB1]
    B -->|Medium 2,000-20,000 kW| D[Nickel-Aluminum Bronze NAB]
    B -->|High >20,000 kW High RPM| E[Super-Duplex Stainless Steel]

    A --> F{Service Environment}
    F -->|Cold Icy Seas| G[Add Impact Toughness Cr]
    F -->|Warm Tropical| H[Check Pitting PREN]
    F -->|Long Route Cargo| I[Prioritize Fatigue Limit]

    A --> J{Lifecycle Cost}
    J -->|Minimize CAPEX| K[Mn-Bronze]
    J -->|Minimize OPEX| L[NAB]
    J -->|Maximize Availability| M[Duplex or NAB]

    C --> N[Selected Propeller Material]
    D --> N
    E --> N
    G --> N
    H --> N
    I --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N

    N --> O[Verification: Sigma Cavitation Number, PREN, Yield Stress]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Corrosion Mechanism Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SW[Seawater Environment] --> GC[General Corrosion]
    SW --> PC[Pitting Corrosion]
    SW --> GC2[Galvanic Corrosion]
    SW --> DZ[Dezincification]

    H2[Cavitation Bubble Collapse] --> CE[Cavitation Erosion]
    CE --> CEC[Synergistic CEC Damage]
    GC --> CEC
    PC --> CEC
    GC2 --> CEC
    DZ --> CEC

    CEC --> FL[Fatigue Crack Initiation]
    FL --> FF[Fatigue Failure]
    CEC --> MM[Mass Loss Wall Thinning]
    MM --> FF
```

---

## 4.3 Architecture: Material Property Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Propeller Material Universe] --> CU[Copper-Based Alloys 90%]
    P --> SS[Stainless Steels 8%]
    P --> SP[Specialty 2%]

    CU --> MB[Manganese Bronze HTB1 HTB2]
    CU --> MAB[Manganese-Aluminum Bronze]
    CU --> NAB[Nickel-Aluminum Bronze AB2 AB3]

    SS --> DUP[Duplex S31803]
    SS --> SDUP[Super-Duplex Zeron 100]
    SS --> PH[17-4 PH Martensitic-Austenitic]

    SP --> TI[Titanium Ti-6Al-4V]
    SP --> CO[Composites CFRP GFRP]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic: Galvanic Series in Seawater (Key for Material Pairing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AN[Anodic - Less Noble - Corrodes]
        ZN[Zinc Anode]
        AL[Aluminum Anode]
        STEEL[Carbon Steel Hull]
    end

    subgraph CAT[Cathodic - More Noble - Protected]
        MNB[Manganese Bronze]
        NAB2[Nickel-Aluminum Bronze]
        DUP2[Super-Duplex Stainless]
        TI2[Titanium]
    end

    ZN -.sacrificial.-> STEEL
    NAB2 -.galvanic couple.-> STEEL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A propeller (NAB) coupled to a steel hull forms a galvanic cell. The hull becomes the **anode** and corrodes. To protect the hull, **zinc or aluminum sacrificial anodes** are mounted on the **shaft bracket** or **rudder** — these are *more anodic* and corrode preferentially.
