# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]
**List the three principal materials used for marine propellers. State the primary metallurgical advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

1. **Manganese Bronze (HTB1/HTB2):** — Cu-Zn-Mn-Al-Fe alloy. *Primary advantage:* **low cost and good castability** for small to medium merchant propellers; σ_y ≈ 275–345 MPa; susceptible to dezincification.

2. **Nickel-Aluminum Bronze (NAB / AB2):** — Cu-Al-Ni-Fe-Mn alloy. *Primary advantage:* **excellent combination of cavitation-erosion resistance, seawater corrosion resistance and high strength**; forms stable Al₂O₃ passive film; σ_y ≈ 320–450 MPa; the most widely used propeller alloy.

3. **Super-Duplex Stainless Steel (Zeron 100 / 17-4 PH):** — Fe-Cr-Ni-Mo-N alloy. *Primary advantage:* **very high strength and pitting resistance (PREN > 40)**; used for high-speed, naval, and ice-class propellers; σ_y ≈ 550–700 MPa; but expensive and difficult to cast.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]
**Explain why a propeller made of Manganese Bronze may suffer "dezincification" in service. What remedial measure is taken at the design stage?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Dezincification** is a **selective corrosion** mechanism in which zinc atoms (more electrochemically active, $E^0_{\text{Zn}} = -0.76$ V vs SHE) are preferentially dissolved from the Cu-Zn alloy, leaving a **porous, mechanically weak Cu-rich skeleton**. The phenomenon is accelerated in **warm, low-velocity, aerated seawater** and at higher Zn content (> 35%).

**Mathematical basis (kinetic):**

$$
\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^- \quad \text{(faster than Cu dissolution)}
$$

**Remedial measures:**
- **Limit Zn content** to < 35% in the alloy specification
- **Add tin (Sn 0.5–1%)** to suppress dezincification (e.g., *admiralty brass* variant)
- **Use NAB instead** (no zinc content; aluminum + iron + nickel are added for strength)
- **Cathodic protection** via zinc anodes
- **Apply protective coatings** (e.g., epoxy, anti-fouling) on the blade surface

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU Model Question — Module 4 Choice Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the mechanical and corrosion properties of Manganese Bronze and Nickel-Aluminum Bronze as propeller materials. Discuss in detail the four major corrosion mechanisms that attack a marine propeller, with one engineering counter-measure for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 5-bladed propeller of diameter $D = 5.5$ m is to be designed for a service speed of $n = 90$ rpm in seawater ($\rho = 1{,}025$ kg/m³). The design point is at depth $h = 3.5$ m and the vessel speed is $V = 9$ knots (= **4.63 m/s**). Calculate the **cavitation number** and **comment on cavitation susceptibility**. If the design is changed to $n = 110$ rpm at the same speed, recompute and interpret.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat block diagrams, classify marine propeller materials and explain the criteria for selection between NAB and super-duplex stainless steel for a 25,000 kW naval frigate propeller.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A super-duplex stainless steel blade section is found to have the following composition: Cr 24.5%, Mo 3.8%, N 0.25%, balance Fe. Calculate its **PREN** and determine whether it is suitable for **warm tropical seawater (30°C, salinity 3.6%)** service.

---

### FULL MODEL SOLUTIONS

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a)** — [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Manganese Bronze (HTB2) | Nickel-Aluminum Bronze (NAB / AB2) |
|----------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Main alloying | Cu-Zn-Mn-Al-Fe | Cu-Al-Ni-Fe-Mn |
| σ_y (MPa) | 275–345 | 320–450 |
| σ_u (MPa) | 450–620 | 600–800 |
| Elongation % | 20–30 | 15–25 |
| Density (kg/m³) | ~8,400 | ~7,650 |
| Cavitation resistance | Moderate | Excellent |
| Dezincification | Yes (susceptible) | No (no Zn) |
| Cost (USD/kg) | 8–12 | 15–22 |
| Typical use | Small–medium propellers | Most merchant & naval propellers |

**Four Corrosion Mechanisms + Counter-measures (4 marks):**

| # | Mechanism | Description | Counter-measure |
|---|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **General corrosion** | Uniform metal loss across surface, ~0.05–0.3 mm/yr for NAB | Cathodic protection by **Zn/Al sacrificial anodes** |
| 2 | **Pitting corrosion** | Localized deep cavities, especially in stainless when passive film is breached | Maintain **PREN > 40**; keep surface clean and passivated |
| 3 | **Galvanic corrosion** | Potential difference between propeller (Cu) and hull (steel) drives current | **Shaft grounding brush**; **isolating coupling**; sacrificial anodes |
| 4 | **Cavitation-erosion-corrosion (CEC)** | Bubble implosions damage surface → accelerates corrosion | Polish blade (low surface roughness); avoid over-loading; use NAB with Al₂O₃ film |

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b)** — [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $D = 5.5$ m, $n_1 = 90$ rpm, $h = 3.5$ m, $V = 4.63$ m/s
- $p_{\text{atm}} = 101.3$ kPa, $p_v = 2.3$ kPa, $\rho_{\text{sw}} = 1{,}025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Compute static pressure at depth $h$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_0 &= p_{\text{atm}} + \rho_{\text{sw}} g h \\
&= 101{,}300 + 1{,}025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3.5 \\
&= 101{,}300 + 35{,}193 \\
&= 136{,}493 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 136.5 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute dynamic pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1{,}025 \cdot (4.63)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1{,}025 \cdot 21.44 \\
&= 10{,}989 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 11.0 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute cavitation number at $n_1 = 90$ rpm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{n1} &= \frac{p_0 - p_v}{q} \\
&= \frac{136{,}493 - 2{,}300}{10{,}989} \\
&= \frac{134{,}193}{10{,}989} \\
&\approx 12.21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute cavitation number at $n_2 = 110$ rpm:**

> [!NOTE]
> $n$ does **not** appear in the cavitation number formula *directly*, but **higher n → higher blade tip velocity → lower local minimum pressure on suction side**, hence **cavitation is more likely**. However, if we assume the **ship's effective wake field changes only modestly**, the *bulk* cavitation number stays the same (= **12.21**). The cavitation *susceptibility* increases because the **cavitation inception number** (a function of blade section, angle of attack, and Reynolds number) becomes **higher** at higher n due to Reynolds scaling and tip-vortex effects.

**Step 5 — Interpret using Burrill's limit:**

| σ_n | Regime | Comment |
|-----|--------|---------|
| σ_n > 2.0 | No cavitation | Safe for all blade sections |
| 0.6 < σ_n < 2.0 | Sheet / bubble cavitation | Tolerable for well-designed sections |
| σ_n < 0.6 | Severe cavitation, erosion | Redesign needed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** With σ_n = **12.21 ≫ 2.0**, the propeller is **cavitation-safe in the bulk sense** for both 90 rpm and 110 rpm at the design ship speed. However, increasing n from 90 to 110 rpm increases **thrust loading coefficient** $K_T$ and the **tip speed** from $V_{\text{tip},1} = \pi D n_1/60 = 25.9$ m/s to $V_{\text{tip},2} = 31.7$ m/s, raising the local cavitation risk on the **outer 0.7R** region. **NAB or super-duplex** is recommended for the higher rpm design.

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a)** — [7 Marks]

(Diagrams and explanation as covered in SECTION 4.)

For a 25,000 kW naval frigate propeller:
- **n > 200 rpm** typical; tip speed > 40 m/s
- Operates in aggressive environments (salt spray, cavitation, possible shock)
- **Selection: Super-duplex stainless steel** (e.g., UNS J93380 / Zeron 100) is preferred over NAB because:
  1. Higher **σ_y ≈ 600 MPa** (vs NAB 350 MPa) — needed for high-RPM design
  2. Higher **PREN > 40** — resists pitting in warm/humid bays
  3. Higher **hardness ~250 HB** — better **cavitation erosion resistance**
  4. **Magnetic signature** is low (paramagnetic) — important for **degaussed naval vessels**
- **NAB** is the second choice when **cost** or **castability** is critical and the vessel is not high-RPM.

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b)** — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — PREN formula:**

$$
\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PREN} &= 24.5 + 3.3 \cdot 3.8 + 16 \cdot 0.25 \\
&= 24.5 + 12.54 + 4.0 \\
&= 41.04
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the rule-of-thumb threshold:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **PREN > 40** ⇒ suitable for **warm tropical seawater** service
> - **PREN 30–40** ⇒ marginal; may suffer pitting in stagnant, warm water
> - **PREN < 30** ⇒ not suitable for long-term seawater immersion

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

> [!NOTE]
> Since PREN = **41.04 > 40**, the alloy is **suitable for warm tropical seawater** service. It will resist pitting initiation; however, **cathodic protection** is still recommended as a design margin, and the propeller should be **polished to a surface finish < 0.8 μm Ra** to avoid crevice corrosion at surface defects.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Mn-Bronze with NAB** — Mn-Bronze contains **zinc** and is **dezincification-prone**; NAB contains **no zinc** and is **aluminum-based**. Examiners specifically test this distinction. ❌
> 2. **Incorrect units in cavitation number** — Always use **Pa** consistently; mixing kPa and Pa leads to wrong answer. ❌
> 3. **Forgetting ρ in the dynamic pressure term** — A common error is writing $q = \tfrac{1}{2} V^2$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$. ❌
> 4. **Misidentifying PREN threshold** — A PREN of 36 is **NOT suitable** for warm tropical seawater; it must be **> 40**. ❌
> 5. **Omitting density or ω²** in the centrifugal stress derivation. ❌
> 6. **Writing "copper alloy" without specifying the exact UNS / BS grade** — Examiners want **C95800, AB2, C95520** etc. ❌
> 7. **Forgetting the *vapor pressure* $p_v$** term in the cavitation number formula. ❌
> 8. **Not converting knots to m/s** in numerical problems. ❌

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Propeller Materials (Module 4)**
> - **Three main materials:** Manganese Bronze, Nickel-Aluminum Bronze (NAB), Super-Duplex Stainless Steel.
> - **Mn-Bronze:** Cu-Zn based, σ_y ~ 280–345 MPa, cheap, but **dezincification risk**; for small/medium propellers.
> - **NAB (C95800 / AB2):** Cu-Al-Ni-Fe-Mn, σ_y ~ 320–450 MPa, **best general-purpose propeller material**, excellent cavitation resistance.
> - **Super-Duplex Stainless:** Fe-Cr-Ni-Mo-N, σ_y ~ 550–700 MPa, PREN > 40, used for **high-speed naval / ice-class** propellers.
> - **Four corrosion mechanisms:** General, Pitting, Galvanic, Cavitation-Erosion-Corrosion (CEC).
> - **Dezincification:** Loss of Zn from brass → porous Cu skeleton; **limit Zn < 35%**; prefer NAB.
> - **Cavitation number formula:** $\sigma_n = (p_0 - p_v) / (0.5 \rho V^2)$ — must be **> 1.5** for cavitation-free design.
> - **PREN threshold:** PREN > 40 for **warm tropical / aggressive seawater**; 30–40 marginal; < 30 not suitable.
> - **Centrifugal stress:** $\sigma_c = \rho \omega^2 (R^2 - r^2)/2 \cdot K$ — sets lower bound on σ_y.
> - **Sacrificial anodes (Zn/Al):** Always mounted on **shaft or hull** to protect from galvanic corrosion.
> - **Galvanic series in seawater (most → least noble):** Zn → Al → Steel → Mn-Bronze → NAB → Duplex SS → Ti.
> - **Standards to memorize:** **ASTM B584**, **BS 1400 AB2/AB3**, **UNS C95800**, **DEF STAN 02-747**.
> - **Cavitation erosion is the #1 in-service failure mode** — choose material with hard, adherent passive film (NAB's Al₂O₃ layer is ideal).
> - **Material density matters for weight-sensitive vessels:** NAB is **9% lighter** than Mn-bronze; SS is **~7% lighter** than Mn-bronze.
> - **Cost order (low → high):** Mn-Bronze < NAB < Super-Duplex SS < Titanium.
> - **Naval extra requirement:** **Low magnetic signature** — favor non-ferritic alloys or austenitic/duplex SS over carbon-steel alternatives.
> - **Always compute the cavitation number FIRST** in design — if σ_n < 1.5, either increase $h$ (deeper immersion) or reduce blade area ($A_E/A_0$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propeller Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST404 - SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Propeller Strength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Propeller Strength** is the engineering discipline that evaluates the structural integrity of a marine propeller blade under combined service loads—centrifugal, hydrodynamic (thrust/torque), and hydrodynamic-induced bending. It is expressed numerically as the **combined equivalent stress** at the critical blade section (typically the root, 0.4R, or 0.6R) and is compared against the **allowable stress** of the blade material prescribed by classification societies (IACS UR M3, DNV, Lloyd's Register).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the subject is grounded in three pillars:
- **Blade Stress Analysis** — using the blade as a tapered cantilever beam.
- **Section Modulus Verification** — proving the blade cross-section is geometrically adequate.
- **Material Strength Verification** — comparing induced stresses with the yield/ultimate strength of the chosen alloy (typically **Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB)** or **Manganese Bronze**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Spinning Ceiling Fan Blade**
> Imagine a ceiling fan blade. It is *fixed* at the hub (root) and *free* at the tip. As it spins, two things happen: (1) the blade's own weight tries to fling it outward (centrifugal force), pulling the root in *tension*; (2) the air pushing the blade backward (thrust reaction) tries to *bend* it like a diving board. The root experiences the worst combination of **tensile** + **bending** stress — exactly where the blade snaps if under-designed. A marine propeller is a high-speed, water-loaded version of this same physics.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Material Density (ρ)** for NAB: **8,600 kg/m³** (≈ 0.305 lb/in³).
- **Allowable Tensile Stress (σ_allow)** per IACS UR M3 ≈ **80 N/mm²** for NAB (derated for fatigue).
- **Stress Concentration Factor (K_t)** at the root fillet: typically **1.5 – 2.5**.
- **Young's Modulus (E)** for NAB: **120 GPa**.
- **Maximum design peripheral speed**: **V_tip ≤ 70 m/s** for fixed-pitch propellers.
- **Yield Strength of NAB**: **~ 480 MPa**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Propeller Strength Critical in KTU 2024?**
> Module 4 focuses on *design verification*. A well-designed propeller can still fail structurally if its strength is not verified against combined cyclic (fatigue) and static loads. Classification society rules (IACS Unified Requirements) make this a **mandatory statutory check** before a propeller is approved for sea trials.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Tapered Cantilever Blade Model

The classical strength analysis treats the propeller blade as a **non-uniform cantilever beam** projecting from the hub. The blade has variable chord (c), variable thickness (t), and variable section modulus (Z) along its radius.

**Step-by-Step Loading Logic:**

- **Step 1 – Centrifugal Loading (F_c):** Each elemental ring of the blade at radius `r` has mass `dm = ρ · A(r) · dr`. Rotation at angular velocity `ω` produces a centrifugal force `dF_c = dm · r · ω² = ρ · A(r) · r · ω² · dr` directed radially outward.
- **Step 2 – Thrust Loading (dT):** Thrust is distributed along the span. An elemental section at radius `r` carries thrust proportional to the local blade area and local thrust loading coefficient.
- **Step 3 – Bending Moment (M_b):** The thrust component acting on the blade face creates a bending moment about the root, with maximum bending at the root section.
- **Step 4 – Combined Stress:** The root section experiences: Direct tensile stress (from F_c) + Bending tensile/compressive stress (from M_b). The critical point is the trailing-edge fillet (suction side) where both stresses add.

### 2.2 Why and How of Each Step

- **Why centrifugal?** Even a static (non-thrusting) rotating blade carries its own weight outward. At 200 rpm with R = 2 m, peripheral velocity = 21 m/s, generating enormous tension.
- **Why thrust distribution?** Hydrodynamic pressure is not uniform along the blade span — peak loading occurs at 0.7R.
- **Why combined stress?** A simple tensile check ignores bending. A simple bending check ignores centrifugal pull. Real failure involves *both* acting simultaneously at the root fillet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In 2024 Scheme ESE questions, the examiner expects you to *superpose* stresses — not solve them in isolation. Marks are awarded for the **stress combination equation**, not just F_c or M_b alone.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Centrifugal force on elemental ring | $dF_c = \rho \cdot A(r) \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \cdot dr$ | N (Newton) |
| 2 | Total centrifugal force (tapered blade) | $F_c = \int_{r_h}^{R} \rho \cdot A(r) \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \, dr$ | Integrated over blade span |
| 3 | Centrifugal tensile stress at section | $\sigma_t = F_c \,/\, A_x$ | N/mm² |
| 4 | Thrust on element (per span) | $dT = T \cdot \dfrac{dr/R}{\text{span fraction}}$ | N |
| 5 | Bending moment at section r from tip | $M_b(r) = \int_r^R (R - r') \cdot dT(r')$ | N·m |
| 6 | Section modulus (elliptical blade) | $Z = \dfrac{\pi \cdot b \cdot t^2}{32} \cdot K_{shape}$ | mm³ |
| 7 | Bending stress | $\sigma_b = M_b \,/\, Z$ | N/mm² |
| 8 | Combined maximum stress | $\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot (\sigma_t + \sigma_b)$ | Multiplied by concentration factor |
| 9 | Allowable stress (IACS NAB) | $\sigma_{allow} = 0.4 \cdot \sigma_{UTS}$ | For fatigue derating |
| 10 | Peripheral tip speed limit | $V_{tip} = \pi D n \,/\, 60 \le 70$ m/s | m/s |
| 11 | Mass per blade (integral) | $m_b = \int_{r_h}^{R} \rho \cdot A(r) \, dr$ | kg |
| 12 | Centroidal radius | $r_G = \dfrac{\int r \cdot A(r) \, dr}{\int A(r) \, dr}$ | m |

> [!WARNING]
> Always substitute units in **SI (N, m, kg, s, Pa)**. A common KTU mark-loss is forgetting the unit conversion from `kg/cm²` to `N/mm²` (1 kg/cm² = 0.0981 N/mm²).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Classification approval:** Every commercial propeller is checked under IACS UR M3 before installation.
- **Failure forensics:** 70% of reported propeller failures (per Bureau Veritas surveys) trace back to under-verified root stresses or fatigue at 0.4R.
- **Material selection:** The strength analysis dictates whether a vessel needs NAB (premium, stronger) or Manganese Bronze (cheaper, lower yield).
- **Skew and wide-blade design:** Modern high-skew propellers have *lower* root stresses but require more rigorous FE validation — the analytical formulas above are calibrated to **wide-blade** propellers only.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Centrifugal Force on a Tapered Blade

We consider a blade with cross-sectional area `A(r) = A_0 · (1 - k·(r-r_h))`, i.e., linearly tapered. The hub radius is `r_h` and the tip radius is `R`. Angular velocity is `ω = 2πn/60`.

$$
F_c = \int_{r_h}^{R} \rho \cdot A(r) \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \, dr
$$

Expanding `A(r)` as a linear taper `A(r) = A_0 [1 - k(r - r_h)]`:

$$
F_c = \rho \cdot \omega^2 \int_{r_h}^{R} A_0 [1 - k(r - r_h)] \cdot r \, dr
$$

Pulling out constants:

$$
F_c = \rho \cdot A_0 \cdot \omega^2 \int_{r_h}^{R} \left[ r - k \cdot r \cdot (r - r_h) \right] dr
$$

Expanding the integrand: `r - k(r² - r·r_h) = r - k·r² + k·r·r_h`

$$
F_c = \rho \cdot A_0 \cdot \omega^2 \int_{r_h}^{R} \left[ (1 + k \cdot r_h) \cdot r - k \cdot r^2 \right] dr
$$

Integrating term by term:

$$
F_c = \rho \cdot A_0 \cdot \omega^2 \left[ (1 + k \cdot r_h) \cdot \dfrac{r^2}{2} - k \cdot \dfrac{r^3}{3} \right]_{r_h}^{R}
$$

Substituting the limits:

$$
F_c = \rho \cdot A_0 \cdot \omega^2 \left\{ (1 + k \cdot r_h) \cdot \dfrac{R^2 - r_h^2}{2} - k \cdot \dfrac{R^3 - r_h^3}{3} \right\}
$$

> **Logic Check:** This is the *total* radial force from one blade. For an N-bladed propeller, divide by N at the root (each blade carries 1/Nth of the load path to the hub).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Centrifugal Tensile Stress at the Root

The cross-section at the root `r = r_h` has area `A_root = A_0`. The tensile stress (assuming each blade carries its own load):

$$
\sigma_t = \dfrac{F_c}{A_0}
$$

Dividing the result of 3.1 by `A_0`:

$$
\sigma_t = \rho \cdot \omega^2 \left\{ (1 + k \cdot r_h) \cdot \dfrac{R^2 - r_h^2}{2} - k \cdot \dfrac{R^3 - r_h^3}{3} \right\}
$$

> **Logic Check:** This is the *static* (centrifugal only) stress. It must be combined with bending stress next.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bending Moment from Distributed Thrust

Assume thrust loading per unit span is `w(r)` (N/m). For a parabolic distribution peaking at 0.7R:

$$
w(r) = w_0 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{(r - 0.7R)^2}{0.49 R^2}\right)
$$

The bending moment at section `r` is the integral of `w(r')` acting at distance `(r' - r)` outward:

$$
M_b(r) = \int_{r}^{R} (r' - r) \cdot w(r') \, dr'
$$

Substituting `w(r')`:

$$
M_b(r) = w_0 \int_{r}^{R} (r' - r) \left[ 1 - \dfrac{(r' - 0.7R)^2}{0.49 R^2} \right] dr'
$$

This integral is solved by expanding and integrating term by term. For a uniform simplification often used in KTU problems, take `w(r) = w_0` (constant):

$$
M_b(r) = w_0 \int_{r}^{R} (r' - r) \, dr' = w_0 \cdot \dfrac{(R - r)^2}{2}
$$

At the root (`r = r_h`):

$$
M_b(r_h) = w_0 \cdot \dfrac{(R - r_h)^2}{2}
$$

where `w_0 = T / (R - r_h)` for a uniform distribution.

$$
M_b(r_h) = \dfrac{T \cdot (R - r_h)}{2}
$$

> **Logic Check:** A uniform thrust `T` distributed over span `L = (R - r_h)` gives a *triangular* pressure profile, and the moment at the root is `T·L/2` — the standard cantilever formula.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Combined Maximum Stress

The blade root fillet is the critical point. The stress concentration factor `K_t` accounts for geometry:

$$
\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \left( \sigma_t + \sigma_b \right)
$$

where

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{M_b(r_h)}{Z_{root}}
$$

and `Z_root` is the section modulus at the root fillet.

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \left[ \dfrac{F_c}{A_{root}} + \dfrac{M_b(r_h)}{Z_{root}} \right]
$$

> **Logic Check:** This is the *governing equation* for propeller strength verification. The factor `K_t` is **never omitted** in a board-exam answer (IACS rules penalize its omission).

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Critical Section Identification)

| Radial Station | Governing Stress | Why Critical | KTU Hint |
|----------------|------------------|--------------|----------|
| 0.2R (root) | $\sigma_{max}$ (combined) | Max bending + high centrifugal | Most common exam locus |
| 0.4R | Fatigue + combined | Stress concentration in mid-blade | Failure-prone in service |
| 0.6R | Cavitation erosion | Hydrodynamic, not structural | Mentioned in cavitation modules |
| 0.7R | Peak hydrodynamic load | Maximum lift distribution | Used for thrust moment calcs |
| 0.8R – 0.95R (tip) | Centrifugal only | Low bending, high speed | Checked for tip strength |
| 1.0R (tip) | Erosion + vibration | Edge conditions | Not used in strength formula |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Verdict:** Always design-check at **0.2R and 0.4R** — these are the IACS-prescribed critical sections for *wide-bladed* propellers.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Propeller Strength Evaluation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given T, n, D, blade geometry] --> B[Compute omega from n]
    B --> C[Define A of r as function of chord and thickness]
    C --> D[Integrate centrifugal force F sub c over span]
    D --> E[Compute tensile stress sigma sub t at root]
    E --> F[Define thrust distribution w of r]
    F --> G[Integrate bending moment M sub b at root]
    G --> H[Compute section modulus Z at root]
    H --> I[Compute bending stress sigma sub b]
    I --> J[Apply Kt concentration factor]
    J --> K[Compute combined sigma sub max]
    K --> L{Compare with sigma sub allow}
    L -- Pass --> M[Propeller strength verified]
    L -- Fail --> N[Increase thickness or change material]
    N --> O[Recompute Z and repeat]
    O --> K
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Blade as Tapered Cantilever

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Hub r sub h] --- A1[Station 0.2R]
    A1 --- A2[Station 0.4R]
    A2 --- A3[Station 0.6R]
    A3 --- A4[Station 0.8R]
    A4 --- A5[Tip R]
    
    subgraph Loads
        L1[Fc outward along span] 
        L2[T distributed pressure on face]
        L3[Mb bending at root]
    end
    
    Loads --> A0
    A0 -.critical section.-> A1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Stress Superposition Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Propeller Blade Cross Section] --> Q[Centrifugal sigma sub t]
    P --> R[Hydrodynamic sigma sub h]
    P --> S[Thermal sigma sub th]
    Q --> U[Algebraic Sum at Fillet]
    R --> U
    S --> U
    U --> V[Multiply by Kt concentration factor]
    V --> W[sigma sub max]
    W --> X[Comparison with allowable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings?**
> Propeller stress visualization requires 3D blade cross-sections with pressure contours. Block diagrams convey the **causal chain** (load → stress → verification) which is the analytical skill KTU examiners test. Use these in your answer sheets to demonstrate **process understanding**, not just final numbers.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define the term "section modulus" of a propeller blade section. Why is it a critical design parameter in propeller strength verification?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Section modulus `Z` of a propeller blade cross-section is the ratio of the second moment of area `I` to the distance `c_max` from the neutral axis to the outermost fibre: $Z = I \,/\, c_{max}$. For an elliptical blade section, it is $Z = \pi b t^2 \,/\, 32$ where `b` is the chord width and `t` is the maximum thickness.
- **Critical Parameter (1.5 marks):** It directly governs the **bending stress** at the section: $\sigma_b = M_b / Z$. A higher `Z` (achieved by increasing thickness `t`) reduces bending stress without changing the blade geometry or hydrodynamic performance. Classification societies (IACS) prescribe a **minimum section modulus** at 0.2R and 0.4R based on thrust and diameter, making it a **statutory design check**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *State the IACS prescribed maximum peripheral tip speed for a fixed-pitch propeller. Why is this limit imposed?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** $V_{tip} = \pi D n / 60 \le 70$ m/s for a fixed-pitch propeller in free-running condition.
- **Reason 1 (1 mark):** To limit the **centrifugal tensile stress** at the root — peripheral speed scales as `ωR` and the centrifugal stress scales as `ω²R²`, so exceeding 70 m/s risks blade separation at the root.
- **Reason 2 (1 mark):** To control **cavitation inception** and **tip vortex erosion** — higher tip speeds correlate with sheet cavitation onset, reducing hydrodynamic efficiency and accelerating blade erosion. The 70 m/s limit is empirical, derived from service failure data of the 1970s–80s.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as 7 + 7.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **A (a) [7 Marks]** A 4-bladed fixed-pitch propeller of diameter **D = 3.2 m** rotates at **n = 180 rpm**. The blade has a mean pitch ratio of 0.7 and delivers a thrust of **T = 180 kN** in the design condition. The hub radius is **r_h = 0.45 m**. Assuming a **uniform thrust distribution** over the blade span and approximating the blade as having a **linearly tapered section area** from `A_root = 0.022 m²` at the root to `A_tip = 0.005 m²` at the tip, compute the **centrifugal tensile stress** at the root. Take blade material density **ρ = 8600 kg/m³** and ignore the hub taper coefficient. Comment on whether the blade is safe, given an allowable stress of **80 N/mm²**.

> **A (b) [7 Marks]** For the same propeller, compute the **bending stress** at the root section using a uniform thrust distribution and a section modulus `Z = 2.4 × 10⁻⁴ m³`. Apply a stress concentration factor of `K_t = 1.8`. Find the **combined maximum stress** and verify against the allowable.

---

**Model Solution A (a):**

Given: D = 3.2 m, n = 180 rpm, r_h = 0.45 m, A_root = 0.022 m², A_tip = 0.005 m², ρ = 8600 kg/m³.

**Step 1: Compute ω.**
$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi n}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 180}{60} = 6\pi = 18.85 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2: Define the linear taper.**
$$
A(r) = A_{root} - (A_{root} - A_{tip}) \cdot \dfrac{r - r_h}{R - r_h} = 0.022 - 0.017 \cdot \dfrac{r - 0.45}{1.15}
$$

**Step 3: Set up the centrifugal force integral.**
$$
F_c = \int_{0.45}^{1.6} \rho \cdot A(r) \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \, dr
$$

**Step 4: Compute ω².**
$$
\omega^2 = 355.3 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 5: Substitute and integrate.** Let `A(r) = 0.022 - 0.017(r-0.45)/1.15` = `0.022 - 0.01478(r-0.45)` = `0.02865 - 0.01478 r`.

$$
F_c = 8600 \cdot 355.3 \int_{0.45}^{1.6} (0.02865 - 0.01478 r) \cdot r \, dr
$$

**Step 6: Expand the integrand.**
$$
(0.02865 r - 0.01478 r^2)
$$

**Step 7: Integrate term by term.**
$$
\int_{0.45}^{1.6} (0.02865 r - 0.01478 r^2) \, dr = \left[ 0.014325 r^2 - 0.004927 r^3 \right]_{0.45}^{1.6}
$$

At r = 1.6: `0.014325 · 2.56 - 0.004927 · 4.096` = `0.036672 - 0.020181` = `0.016491`

At r = 0.45: `0.014325 · 0.2025 - 0.004927 · 0.091125` = `0.002901 - 0.000449` = `0.002452`

Difference: `0.016491 - 0.002452 = 0.014039` m³

**Step 8: Compute F_c.**
$$
F_c = 8600 \cdot 355.3 \cdot 0.014039 = 42,888 \text{ N} \approx 42.9 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 9: Compute tensile stress at root (per blade).**
$$
\sigma_t = \dfrac{F_c}{A_{root}} = \dfrac{42,888}{0.022} = 1,949,455 \text{ Pa} = 1.95 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** $\sigma_t = 1.95$ N/mm² is far below 80 N/mm². The blade passes the **centrifugal stress** check with a large margin.

---

**Model Solution A (b):**

**Step 1: Compute the uniform thrust per unit span.**
$$
w_0 = \dfrac{T}{R - r_h} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{1.6 - 0.45} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{1.15} = 156{,}522 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 2: Bending moment at the root (uniform distribution).**
$$
M_b(r_h) = w_0 \cdot \dfrac{(R - r_h)^2}{2} = 156{,}522 \cdot \dfrac{1.15^2}{2} = 156{,}522 \cdot 0.6613 = 103{,}500 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3: Bending stress.**
$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{M_b}{Z} = \dfrac{103{,}500}{2.4 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.31 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 431 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4: Combined stress (with K_t).**
$$
\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot (\sigma_t + \sigma_b) = 1.8 \cdot (1.95 + 431) = 1.8 \cdot 432.95 = 779.3 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** $\sigma_{max} = 779$ N/mm² **far exceeds** the allowable 80 N/mm². The blade is **NOT safe** — the section modulus is too small. Recommend increasing `Z` by ~10× (increase thickness from current to about 1.8× current value) or reduce thrust loading.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students use `T × R` instead of `T × (R - r_h)/2` for root moment — losing 3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to apply `K_t` — losing 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Computing $\sigma_t$ per blade and $\sigma_b$ per blade inconsistently — ensure both are at the **same root section** with the **same per-blade load path**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **B (a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **centrifugal tensile stress** at the root of a marine propeller blade, assuming a **uniform cross-section** `A_b` (a non-tapered approximation). Comment on why this over-estimates the true stress for a real tapered blade.

> **B (b) [7 Marks]** A 5-bladed propeller of diameter **D = 4.0 m**, hub radius **r_h = 0.5 m**, rotates at **n = 140 rpm**. The blade is approximated as uniform with `A_b = 0.030 m²`. Compute the **total centrifugal force per blade**. If the allowable tensile stress is **100 N/mm²**, find the **maximum permissible blade mass** without exceeding the limit. Take ρ = 8600 kg/m³.

---

**Model Solution B (a):**

**Derivation:**

For a uniform cross-section `A(r) = A_b = constant`:

$$
F_c = \int_{r_h}^{R} \rho \cdot A_b \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \, dr = \rho \cdot A_b \cdot \omega^2 \int_{r_h}^{R} r \, dr
$$

Integrating:

$$
F_c = \rho \cdot A_b \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{r^2}{2} \right]_{r_h}^{R} = \rho \cdot A_b \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{R^2 - r_h^2}{2}
$$

The tensile stress at the root (section area = A_b, so it cancels):

$$
\sigma_t = \dfrac{F_c}{A_b} = \rho \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{R^2 - r_h^2}{2}
$$

> **Comment (2 Marks):** A real propeller blade is **tapered** (thicker at root, thinner at tip). The uniform approximation over-estimates the cross-sectional area in the outer radii, which contribute the *largest* centrifugal force per unit mass (since `F_c ∝ r`). Thus, $\sigma_t^{uniform} > \sigma_t^{real}$. The taper gives a real blade ~20–30% lower stress than the uniform model predicts.

---

**Model Solution B (b):**

**Step 1: Compute ω and ω².**
$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 140}{60} = 14.66 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega^2 = 214.9 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute the integral factor.**
$$
\dfrac{R^2 - r_h^2}{2} = \dfrac{2^2 - 0.5^2}{2} = \dfrac{4 - 0.25}{2} = 1.875 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Compute F_c.**
$$
F_c = 8600 \cdot 0.030 \cdot 214.9 \cdot 1.875 = 103{,}900 \text{ N} \approx 103.9 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Compute tensile stress at root.**
$$
\sigma_t = \dfrac{F_c}{A_b} = \dfrac{103{,}900}{0.030} = 3.46 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 3.46 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5: Find the maximum blade mass for σ_allow = 100 N/mm².**

The mass of a uniform blade is $m_b = \rho \cdot A_b \cdot (R - r_h) = 8600 \cdot 0.030 \cdot 1.5 = 387$ kg.

For a general uniform blade, $\sigma_t = m_b \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{R + r_h}{2}$ (since $F_c = m_b \cdot r_G \cdot \omega^2$ where $r_G = (R+r_h)/2$).

Setting $\sigma_t \le \sigma_{allow}$:

$$
m_{b,max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow} \cdot 2}{\omega^2 \cdot (R + r_h)} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^6 \cdot 2}{214.9 \cdot 2.5} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^8}{537.25} = 372{,}300 \text{ kg}
$$

> **Conclusion (1 Mark):** The maximum permissible blade mass is **372,300 kg** — clearly, the design blade at 387 kg just exceeds this when stress-concentration is applied. The 5-bladed configuration is *not* safe in the uniform approximation; a tapered or section-reinforced design is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using `R²` instead of `(R² - r_h²)/2` — losing 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $m_b$ (mass per blade) with `M_total` (mass of all blades) — losing 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In part (a), students *skip the comment* on the over-estimation. KTU 2024 rewards **qualitative reasoning** for 2 marks separately.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Combined stress equation** $\sigma_{max} = K_t (\sigma_t + \sigma_b)$ is the *governing check* — never evaluate stresses in isolation.
- [ ] **Critical sections** for IACS strength: 0.2R and 0.4R.
- [ ] **Allowable stress** ≈ **0.4 × UTS** for fatigue derating in marine NAB (≈ 80 N/mm² for typical NAB).
- [ ] **Tip speed limit**: $V_{tip} = \pi D n / 60 \le 70$ m/s.
- [ ] **Bending moment at root** for uniform thrust = $T \cdot (R - r_h) / 2$.
- [ ] **Centrifugal force** is *tensile* — it pulls the blade *outward*; combined with bending, the suction-side trailing edge is the critical point.
- [ ] **Stress concentration factor** `K_t` is **mandatory** in board answers.
- [ ] **Material density ρ** for NAB = **8,600 kg/m³**; for Manganese Bronze ≈ **8,400 kg/m³**.
- [ ] **Tapered blade** has lower stress than uniform — uniform approx is *conservative*.
- [ ] **Section modulus** is the **knob you turn** when adjusting strength without altering hydrodynamic performance.
- [ ] Always verify the **per-blade load path** — divide total thrust by N if computing moment per blade.
- [ ] **Units check**: 1 N/mm² = 1 MPa; 1 kg/cm² = 0.0981 N/mm².

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The 14-mark questions in Module 4 almost always ask a *combined stress* problem with both centrifugal + bending. Master the *superposition* technique and the rest follows. Time allocation in the ESE: 7 minutes for Part A, 18 minutes per Part B sub-question.

---
